topics.title: Strategy of Experimentation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strategy of Experimentation

# ­¤ōś Strategy of Experimentation ŌĆö Module 1

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### ­¤ōī Formal Definition

> **Strategy of Experimentation** refers to the systematic, pre-planned sequence of steps and statistical principles followed by an experimenter to design, conduct, and analyze experiments such that valid, objective, and economically efficient conclusions can be drawn about the relationships between input **factors** (independent variables) and output **responses** (dependent variables).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OECHT727), this topic forms the foundation for understanding *why* we move from ad-hoc laboratory testing to a rigorous, statistical framework called **Design of Experiments (DoE)**.

---

### ­¤Ź│ Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Master Chef's Experiment"

Imagine you are a **chef trying to perfect a biryani recipe**. You suspect four factors influence taste:

- Cooking temperature (┬░C)
- Spices quantity (g)
- Marination time (hrs)
- Rice variety (type)

A **novice chef (Classical approach)** would:
1. Fix three factors at "comfortable" values.
2. Vary *only* temperature and record taste scores.
3. Then try varying spicesŌĆ” one factor at a time.

A **master chef (Statistical DoE approach)** would:
1. Plan a **structured set of trials** where *all four factors vary simultaneously* across carefully chosen levels.
2. **Randomize** the cooking order so that a random stove fluctuation does not bias one factor's effect.
3. **Replicate** the most promising combos to confirm consistency.
4. Analyze results using a statistical model to extract the *simultaneous* effect of each factor ŌĆö and even spot **interactions** (e.g., "spices ├Ś marination time" synergy).

The *strategy of experimentation* is the master chef's **rulebook**.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 ŌĆō OECHT727):**
> Students must understand the **limitations of the classical one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) approach**, the **need for statistical experimentation**, and the **three basic principles of DoE**: *Randomization, Replication, and Blocking*. These are **high-yield, guaranteed exam topics**.

### ­¤öæ Key Terminology (Bold Definitions)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Experiment** | A planned test in which deliberate changes are made to input variables so that the effects on output responses may be observed. |
| **Factor (X)** | An independent controllable input variable whose levels are deliberately varied. |
| **Response (Y)** | The dependent output variable being measured. |
| **Level** | A specific value or setting of a factor used in the experiment. |
| **Treatment** | A specific combination of factor levels applied to an experimental unit. |
| **Experimental Unit** | The smallest entity (object, batch, subject) to which a treatment is applied. |
| **Replication** | Independent repetition of a treatment under identical conditions. |
| **Effect** | The change in response produced by a change in the level of a factor. |

> [!NOTE]
> An **experiment differs from observational study** ŌĆö in an experiment we *actively manipulate* factors, while in observation we merely record them. DoE requires the former.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### ­¤¦Ł Why Do We Need a "Strategy"?

Without a deliberate strategy, an experimenter faces three classic pitfalls:

1. **Bias** ŌĆö systematic favoritism toward certain factor levels.
2. **Confounding** ŌĆö inability to separate the effect of one factor from another.
3. **Inefficiency** ŌĆö running too many experiments to extract minimal information.

A well-designed strategy addresses all three.

---

### ­¤Åø’ĖÅ Two Historical Approaches to Experimentation

#### **A. Classical / OFAT (One-Factor-at-a-Time) Approach**

- **Logic:** Change only one factor while keeping all others fixed at a baseline.
- **Procedure:**
  1. Choose baseline settings for all factors.
  2. Vary Factor A across its levels ŌåÆ record responses.
  3. Reset to baseline ŌåÆ vary Factor B ŌåÆ record.
  4. Repeat for each factor sequentially.
- **Advantages:**
  - Simple, intuitive, easy to implement.
  - No need for statistical training.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - ŌØī Cannot detect **interactions** between factors.
  - ŌØī Wastes experimental runs (each factor studied in isolation).
  - ŌØī Optimal region may be **missed** entirely.
  - ŌØī Results valid only within the narrow tested region.
- **Best Use:** Screening of a single dominant factor; preliminary exploration.

#### **B. Statistical Design of Experiments (DoE) Approach**

- **Logic:** Vary *all relevant factors simultaneously* across a structured matrix of runs, then analyze using statistical models.
- **Procedure:**
  1. State the objective and define response variables.
  2. Identify all factors and their ranges.
  3. Choose a suitable experimental design (e.g., full factorial, fractional factorial).
  4. Randomize the run order.
  5. Execute experiments and collect data.
  6. Analyze via ANOVA / regression.
  7. Confirm conclusions via confirmation runs.
- **Advantages:**
  - Ō£ģ Efficient ŌĆö fewer runs for same information.
  - Ō£ģ Captures **main effects** AND **interactions**.
  - Ō£ģ Provides a **mathematical model** of the response.
  - Ō£ģ Valid across the entire experimental region.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - Requires statistical training and software.
  - Requires careful planning and randomization discipline.

---

### ­¤ö║ The Three Basic Principles of DoE (MANDATORY for KTU)

#### **1. Randomization**
- **Definition:** The order in which experimental runs are performed is *randomly determined*, not arbitrarily or sequentially.
- **Purpose:** Protects against the influence of **lurking variables** (e.g., humidity drift, operator fatigue, equipment warm-up) by spreading their effect evenly across all treatments.
- **Why it matters:** Without randomization, the estimated effect of Factor A may actually be the effect of "time of day".

#### **2. Replication**
- **Definition:** Each treatment is repeated **independently** multiple times.
- **Purpose:**
  - Provides an independent estimate of the **experimental error (Žā┬▓)**.
  - Increases the **precision** of factor-effect estimates.
  - Enables statistical significance testing.
- **Effect on confidence interval:**

$$
\text{SE}(\bar{Y}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

where $n$ is the number of replicates. Doubling $n$ reduces SE by $\sqrt{2}$.

#### **3. Blocking**
- **Definition:** Grouping similar experimental units together (a "block") to account for a known but uncontrollable source of variation.
- **Purpose:** Removes the variability *between blocks* from the error term, increasing sensitivity to the factors of interest.
- **Classic Example:** Running all experiments with Operator A on Day 1 and Operator B on Day 2 ŌĆö "Operator/Day" is the block. This prevents operator skill from being confounded with factor effects.

---

### ­¤¦Ł The Strategy Flowchart (Montgomery's 5-Step Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU exam framework** ŌĆö students are expected to reproduce this from memory in ESE questions.

1. **Recognition of the Problem** ŌĆö Define the problem statement and objective clearly.
2. **Choice of Factors and Levels** ŌĆö Identify independent variables, their ranges, and levels.
3. **Selection of Response Variable** ŌĆö Choose measurable Y (must be quantifiable, ideally continuous).
4. **Choice of Experimental Design** ŌĆö Full factorial, fractional, screening, RSM, etc.
5. **Conducting the Experiment** ŌĆö Randomize, replicate, block, then execute.
6. **Statistical Analysis of Data** ŌĆö ANOVA, regression, residual diagnostics.
7. **Conclusions and Recommendations** ŌĆö Confirmation runs + process optimization.

---

### ­¤ōŖ KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Strategy of Experimentation

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Purpose |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | Effect of Factor A | $A_{\text{effect}} = \bar{Y}_{A+} - \bar{Y}_{A-}$ | Quantify main effect |
| 2 | Standard Error of Mean | $\text{SE}(\bar{Y}) = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Precision measure |
| 3 | Two-sample t-test | $t_0 = \dfrac{\bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2}{\sqrt{S_p^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{n_1}+\dfrac{1}{n_2}\right)}}$ | Compare two means |
| 4 | Pooled Variance | $S_p^2 = \dfrac{(n_1-1)S_1^2 + (n_2-1)S_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}$ | Combined error estimate |
| 5 | Replication benefit | $\text{Var}(\bar{Y}) \propto \dfrac{1}{n}$ | More reps ŌåÆ less variance |
| 6 | Interaction AB | $AB = \dfrac{1}{2}[(AB)_{++} + (AB)_{--}] - \dfrac{1}{2}[(AB)_{+-} + (AB)_{-+}]$ | Detect synergy |
| 7 | Blocking model | $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}$ | Remove nuisance variation |
| 8 | Statistical model | $Y = f(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_k) + \epsilon$ | Empirical response surface |

> [!NOTE]
> Formulas 1, 3, 4, and 7 are the **most frequently tested** in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

---

### ­¤ÅŁ Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Chemical Engineering:** Optimizing yield of a chemical reaction by varying temperature, pressure, and catalyst concentration.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Determining cutting parameters (speed, feed, depth) that minimize surface roughness.
- **Civil Engineering:** Studying compressive strength of concrete as a function of water-cement ratio, curing time, admixtures.
- **Electronics:** Optimizing a semiconductor etching process involving 6 factors with 2 levels each ŌåÆ $2^6$ full factorial.
- **Biotechnology:** Optimizing fermentation yield by manipulating pH, temperature, agitation speed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### ­¤¦« Derivation 1 ŌĆö Standard Error Reduction via Replication

**Statement:** Show that the variance of the sample mean decreases inversely with the number of replications.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\text{Let } Y_1, Y_2, \dots, Y_n \text{ be independent observations with } Y_i \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2).
$$

The sample mean is:

$$
\bar{Y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i
$$

Apply the variance operator to both sides:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{Y}) = \text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i\right)
$$

Since $\frac{1}{n}$ is a constant, pull it out squared:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{Y}) = \frac{1}{n^2}\text{Var}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i\right)
$$

Since the $Y_i$ are independent, variance of the sum equals the sum of variances:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{Y}) = \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\text{Var}(Y_i) = \frac{1}{n^2}(n\sigma^2)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{Y}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}
$$

Taking square root:

$$
\text{SE}(\bar{Y}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** To halve the standard error, you must **quadruple** the number of replications ŌĆö a critical insight for experimental cost planning.

---

### ­¤¦« Derivation 2 ŌĆö Two-Sample t-Test for Comparing Two Means

**Statement:** Derive the test statistic for comparing the means of two independent groups with equal variances.

Let $\bar{Y}_1, \bar{Y}_2$ be sample means with equal variance $\sigma^2$ and sample sizes $n_1, n_2$.

The difference of means has expectation:

$$
E(\bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2) = \mu_1 - \mu_2
$$

The variance of the difference is:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2) = \text{Var}(\bar{Y}_1) + \text{Var}(\bar{Y}_2) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma^2}{n_2}
$$

Factor out $\sigma^2$:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2) = \sigma^2 \left(\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}\right)
$$

Since $\sigma^2$ is unknown, estimate it using the pooled variance:

$$
S_p^2 = \frac{(n_1-1)S_1^2 + (n_2-1)S_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}
$$

The t-statistic under $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$ is therefore:

$$
t_0 = \frac{\bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2}{S_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1}+\dfrac{1}{n_2}}}
$$

with degrees of freedom:

$$
df = n_1 + n_2 - 2
$$

**Rejection rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\vert t_0 \vert > t_{\alpha/2,\, n_1+n_2-2}$.

---

### ­¤¦« Derivation 3 ŌĆö Computation of Main Effect in a Two-Level Factorial

For a two-level factor A with levels $(-)$ and $(+)$, the main effect is the *average change in response* when A moves from low to high:

$$
A = \bar{Y}_{A+} - \bar{Y}_{A-}
$$

If there are $n$ replicates per treatment:

$$
\bar{Y}_{A+} = \frac{\text{sum of responses at } A^+}{n \cdot 2^{k-1}}
$$

$$
\bar{Y}_{A-} = \frac{\text{sum of responses at } A^-}{n \cdot 2^{k-1}}
$$

The number of observations at each level is $n \cdot 2^{k-1}$ (the replication factor times half of the design).

**Example:** For a $2^2$ design (factors A, B) with 2 replicates ŌĆö total $2^2 \times 2 = 8$ runs. Each level of A appears in $2 \times 2 = 4$ runs.

---

### ­¤ōŗ Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison Table for KTU Exam

| Aspect | Classical (OFAT) Approach | Statistical DoE Approach |
|--------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Factors varied | One at a time | All simultaneously |
| Interactions detected | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Optimal region | May be missed | Located reliably |
| Number of runs | High for same info | Efficient |
| Statistical model | None | Full regression model |
| Randomization required | Not needed | Mandatory |
| Replication | Sometimes | Always required |
| Blocking | Rarely used | Systematic |
| Analytical tool | Graph plotting | ANOVA + Regression |
| Validity region | Narrow | Entire design space |
| Modern industrial use | Legacy/screening only | Standard practice |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### ­¤öü Mermaid Diagram 1 ŌĆö Strategy of Experimentation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Problem Recognition] --> B[Step 2: Define Response Y]
    B --> C[Step 3: Identify Factors and Levels]
    C --> D[Step 4: Choose Experimental Design]
    D --> E[Step 5: Randomize Run Order]
    E --> F[Step 6: Execute Experiments with Replication]
    F --> G[Step 7: Statistical Analysis ANOVA Regression]
    G --> H{Conclusions Valid?}
    H -- No --> I[Refine Model and Re Run]
    I --> C
    H -- Yes --> J[Step 8: Confirmation Runs]
    J --> K[Final Conclusions and Optimization]
```

### ­¤Åø’ĖÅ Mermaid Diagram 2 ŌĆö The Three Pillars of DoE

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1 [Pillar 1]
        R1[Randomization]
    end
    subgraph P2 [Pillar 2]
        R2[Replication]
    end
    subgraph P3 [Pillar 3]
        R3[Blocking]
    end
    R1 --> CORE[Valid Statistical Inference]
    R2 --> CORE
    R3 --> CORE
    CORE --> OUT[Unbiased Factor Effect Estimates]
```

### ­¤ö¼ Mermaid Diagram 3 ŌĆö OFAT vs Full Factorial Design Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph OFAT [OFAT Approach]
        O1[B1 B2 B3 B4 - Fix others - Vary A]
        O2[C1 C2 C3 C4 - Fix others - Vary B]
    end
    subgraph FACT [Statistical Full Factorial]
        F1[Run 1: A- B-]
        F2[Run 2: A+ B-]
        F3[Run 3: A- B+]
        F4[Run 4: A+ B+]
    end
    OFAT -.->|Misses interactions| MISS[Suboptimal Region]
    FACT -->|Captures main effects and AB interaction| OPT[Optimal Region]
```

### ­¤¦Ā Mermaid Diagram 4 ŌĆö Sources of Variation in an Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Y[Observed Response Y]
    Y --> M[Systematic Part: f X1 X2 ... Xk]
    Y --> E[Random Part: epsilon N 0 sigma squared]
    M --> M1[Main Effects]
    M --> M2[Two Factor Interactions]
    M --> M3[Curvature Quadratic Terms]
    E --> E1[Lurking Variables]
    E --> E2[Measurement Error]
    E --> E3[Inherent Process Noise]
```

### ­¤ōÉ Mermaid Diagram 5 ŌĆö Blocking Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BLOCK1 [Block 1: Day 1 Operator A]
        T1[Treatment 1]
        T2[Treatment 2]
    end
    subgraph BLOCK2 [Block 2: Day 2 Operator B]
        T3[Treatment 3]
        T4[Treatment 4]
    end
    T1 --> Y1[Response]
    T2 --> Y2[Response]
    T3 --> Y3[Response]
    T4 --> Y4[Response]
    Y1 --> MODEL[Analysis: Y = mu + tau + beta + epsilon]
    Y2 --> MODEL
    Y3 --> MODEL
    Y4 --> MODEL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Drawing the strategy flowchart (Diagram 1) in the exam earns full marks even if no numerical calculation is performed ŌĆö examiners reward the **structural clarity**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Set A]`

> Define the strategy of experimentation. Why is it preferred over the classical OFAT approach?

**Model Answer:**

The strategy of experimentation is a systematic, statistically rigorous plan that guides the experimenter through problem definition, factor and level selection, response measurement, design choice, randomization, replication, and analysis to obtain valid and efficient conclusions.

It is preferred over the classical **One-Factor-at-a-Time (OFAT)** approach because:

1. **Efficiency:** Studies multiple factors simultaneously with fewer runs.
2. **Interaction Detection:** Captures two-factor and higher-order interactions.
3. **Validity Region:** Results valid across the entire experimental region.
4. **Statistical Inference:** Provides confidence intervals and significance tests.

**Marks:** Definition (1.5) + Any two advantages (1.5) = **3 marks**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Set B]`

> State and briefly explain any two basic principles of Design of Experiments.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle 1 ŌĆö Randomization:**
The order of experimental runs is randomly determined. This prevents systematic bias from uncontrolled variables (e.g., humidity drift, equipment aging) from being confounded with the factor effects.

**Principle 2 ŌĆö Replication:**
Each treatment combination is repeated independently multiple times. This provides an estimate of the **experimental error variance** and increases the precision of estimated factor effects. The standard error of the mean decreases as $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$, where $n$ is the number of replications.

**Marks:** Each principle: 1.5 marks = **3 marks**

---

### ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (Module Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain in detail the three basic principles of Design of Experiments with suitable examples. **\[7 marks\]**
>
> **(b)** An experimenter is studying the effect of two factors ŌĆö Temperature (A: 80┬░C, 100┬░C) and Catalyst type (B: Cat-1, Cat-2) ŌĆö on the yield of a chemical reaction. He performs a $2^2$ full factorial design with 3 replicates. Compute the main effect of factor A, factor B, and the AB interaction. The response data are given below:
>
> | Run | A | B | Replicate | Yield |
> |-----|-----|-----|-----------|-------|
> | 1 | -1 | -1 | 1 | 28 |
> | 2 | +1 | -1 | 1 | 36 |
> | 3 | -1 | +1 | 1 | 18 |
> | 4 | +1 | +1 | 1 | 31 |
> | 5 | -1 | -1 | 2 | 25 |
> | 6 | +1 | -1 | 2 | 32 |
> | 7 | -1 | +1 | 2 | 19 |
> | 8 | +1 | +1 | 2 | 30 |
> | 9 | -1 | -1 | 3 | 27 |
> | 10 | +1 | -1 | 3 | 35 |
> | 11 | -1 | +1 | 3 | 20 |
> | 12 | +1 | +1 | 3 | 33 |
>
> **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö The Three Principles of DoE**

1. **Randomization:** Run order is decided randomly so that uncontrolled disturbances (operator fatigue, ambient drift, machine warm-up) are equally likely to affect all treatments, ensuring unbiased effect estimates.

2. **Replication:** Independent repetition of each treatment provides an unbiased estimate of experimental error ($\sigma^2$) and improves the precision of effect estimates. Example: testing a new drug on 100 patients instead of 10.

3. **Blocking:** Grouping similar experimental units or performing experiments in homogeneous blocks to remove a known source of nuisance variability. Example: Testing two fertilizers in two different farms ŌĆö "Farm" is the block. This isolates the treatment effect from farm-to-farm variability.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Effect Computations**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Average yield for each treatment combination (across 3 reps):**

$$
(1)_{\text{avg}} = \frac{28+25+27}{3} = \frac{80}{3} = 26.67
$$

$$
(a)_{\text{avg}} = \frac{36+32+35}{3} = \frac{103}{3} = 34.33
$$

$$
(b)_{\text{avg}} = \frac{18+19+20}{3} = \frac{57}{3} = 19.00
$$

$$
(ab)_{\text{avg}} = \frac{31+30+33}{3} = \frac{94}{3} = 31.33
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Main Effect of A (Temperature):**

$$
A = \bar{Y}_{A+} - \bar{Y}_{A-} = \frac{34.33+31.33}{2} - \frac{26.67+19.00}{2}
$$

$$
A = 32.83 - 22.835 = 10.00
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Main Effect of B (Catalyst):**

$$
B = \bar{Y}_{B+} - \bar{Y}_{B-} = \frac{19.00+31.33}{2} - \frac{26.67+34.33}{2}
$$

$$
B = 25.165 - 30.50 = -5.33
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö AB Interaction Effect:**

$$
AB = \frac{1}{2}\left[(AB)_{++} + (AB)_{--}\right] - \frac{1}{2}\left[(AB)_{+-} + (AB)_{-+}\right]
$$

$$
AB = \frac{1}{2}[31.33 + 26.67] - \frac{1}{2}[34.33 + 19.00]
$$

$$
AB = 29.00 - 26.665 = 2.33
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Interpretation:**

- Increasing temperature from 80┬░C to 100┬░C raises yield by **10 units** on average.
- Switching from Cat-1 to Cat-2 *reduces* yield by **5.33 units**.
- The AB interaction of **2.33** is moderate ŌĆö temperature's benefit is slightly different across catalysts.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Students often forget to **average over replicates first** before computing effects. This is the #1 reason for marks deduction in KTU Part B.
> 2. Sign convention: $(-1)$ denotes low level, $(+1)$ denotes high level. Reversing this flips the sign of all effects.
> 3. Do **not** round intermediate results ŌĆö keep at least 4 decimals until the final answer.

**Marks distribution (Part b):** Effect A (2.5) + Effect B (2.5) + Interaction AB (2) = **7 marks**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Compare the Classical One-Factor-at-a-Time (OFAT) approach with the Statistical Design of Experiments (DoE) approach. Mention at least six points of comparison. **\[7 marks\]**
>
> **(b)** A researcher wants to study the compressive strength of concrete. She identifies 3 factors: Water-Cement ratio (A), Curing time (B), and Aggregate type (C), each at 2 levels. With 2 replicates, state:
> - The total number of experimental runs.
> - The standard error of the mean if $\sigma = 2.5$ MPa.
> - Why randomization is critical in this experiment.
>
> **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Comparison Table**

| Sl. No. | Aspect | Classical (OFAT) | Statistical DoE |
|---------|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Factors varied | One at a time | All simultaneously |
| 2 | Interactions | Cannot detect | Detects main + interaction effects |
| 3 | Total runs for k factors at 2 levels | $2k$ | $2^k$ (full) or fewer (fractional) |
| 4 | Randomization | Not necessary | Mandatory |
| 5 | Statistical validity | Limited | Full ANOVA + regression possible |
| 6 | Region of validity | Narrow tested point | Entire design region |
| 7 | Cost efficiency | Low for same info | High information density |
| 8 | Modern use | Preliminary screening only | Industry standard |

**Part (b) ŌĆö Quantitative Answers**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Total Number of Runs:**

For a $2^3$ full factorial design with 2 replicates:

$$
N = 2^k \times n = 2^3 \times 2 = 8 \times 2 = 16 \text{ runs}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Standard Error of the Mean:**

Given $\sigma = 2.5$ MPa and $n = 2$ replicates per cell:

$$
\text{SE}(\bar{Y}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2.5}{1.4142} = 1.768 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Why Randomization is Critical:**

Concrete strength experiments are sensitive to ambient temperature, humidity, batch mixing order, and operator skill. If the experimenter performs all runs with factor A at the high level first and then at the low level, the effect of *time-related drift* (e.g., humidity rising through the day) will be **completely confounded** with the effect of A. The estimated "main effect of A" would then actually be *A + drift*. Randomization ensures that any uncontrolled variable is equally likely to affect every treatment, allowing its effect to be **absorbed into the error term** rather than biasing a factor estimate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B):**
> 1. Students often write $2^k + n$ instead of $2^k \times n$ ŌĆö error of **2 marks**.
> 2. For SE, do NOT use the total runs (16) as $n$; use the **number of replicates per cell** ($n=2$).
> 3. The comparison table must have **at least 6 distinct points**; repeating the same idea in different words will not earn full marks.

**Marks distribution (Part b):** Runs (2) + SE (2.5) + Randomization reasoning (2.5) = **7 marks**

---

### ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ **Strategy of Experimentation** = systematic pre-planned framework for conducting valid, efficient experiments.
- Ō£ģ **OFAT approach** = one factor varied at a time; **misses interactions**; **inefficient**.
- Ō£ģ **DoE approach** = all factors varied simultaneously; **detects main + interaction effects**; **efficient**.
- Ō£ģ **Three Pillars of DoE:** **Randomization**, **Replication**, **Blocking** ŌĆö must know definitions, purposes, and examples.
- Ō£ģ **Seven-Step Strategy Framework** (Montgomery): Recognition ŌåÆ Factors/Levels ŌåÆ Response ŌåÆ Design Choice ŌåÆ Randomize ŌåÆ Execute ŌåÆ Analyze ŌåÆ Confirm.
- Ō£ģ **Effect computation formulas:**
  - Main effect: $\bar{Y}_{+} - \bar{Y}_{-}$
  - Interaction AB: $\tfrac{1}{2}[(AB)_{++} + (AB)_{--}] - \tfrac{1}{2}[(AB)_{+-} + (AB)_{-+}]$
- Ō£ģ **Number of runs:** Full factorial = $2^k \times n$; Fractional = $2^{k-p} \times n$.
- Ō£ģ **Replication benefit:** $\text{Var}(\bar{Y}) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$ ŌĆö doubling runs reduces SE by $\sqrt{2}$.
- Ō£ģ **Blocking model:** $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}$.
- Ō£ģ **Always randomize** ŌĆö even if the design looks "balanced", sequential execution without randomization introduces hidden time-based confounding.
- Ō£ģ **Confirm conclusions** with confirmation runs before declaring an optimum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Exam-Focused Final Tip:**
> The most common 14-mark KTU question on this topic is: *"Compare OFAT vs DoE" + "Compute main and interaction effects from a $2^2$ or $2^3$ data table."* Practice the **effect computation procedure** until it is automatic ŌĆö that alone will secure **10ŌĆō12 of the 14 marks** in ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Applications of Experimental Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Typical Applications of Experimental Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Design of Experiments (DOE)** is a systematic, statistically rigorous methodology used to plan, conduct, analyze, and interpret controlled tests (experiments) in order to establish the causal relationship between a set of **independent variables (factors)** and one or more **dependent variables (responses)**. It is formally defined by the KTU 2024 Open Elective syllabus as a structured body of techniques drawn from statistics, engineering, and operations research that allows the experimenter to extract the maximum amount of *reliable information* from the *fewest possible experimental runs*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Definition Highlights**
> - **Factor (Independent Variable):** A controlled input variable whose effect on the response is to be studied (e.g., Temperature, Pressure, Catalyst Concentration).
> - **Response (Dependent Variable):** The measurable output whose behavior we are trying to predict or optimize (e.g., Yield, Strength, Cycle Time).
> - **Treatment:** A specific combination of factor levels applied in an experimental run.
> - **Experimental Unit:** The smallest entity to which a treatment is independently applied.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a chef trying to perfect a new recipe for a coffee shop chain. You have **three knobs (factors)** you can turn: the amount of coffee powder (low, medium, high), the water temperature (cold, warm, hot), and the brewing time (short, medium, long). Your **goal (response)** is to maximize taste rating.

If you randomly tweak these one by one, you might use 100 cups of coffee and still miss the perfect blend. This is the *one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT)* approach, which is inefficient and can lead to wrong conclusions.

**Design of Experiments (DOE)** is like a *scientific pairing strategy* where you carefully plan a small, smart set of tasting sessions (e.g., 9 cups in a *Latin Square*) that simultaneously test combinations, so you can identify:
- Which ingredient matters most?
- Do coffee powder and water temperature *interact* (e.g., more powder needs hotter water)?
- What is the optimum combination?

You save time, money, and resources ŌĆö and gain *statistically proven* answers instead of guesses.

### 1.3 Why DOE? The Strategic Necessity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of DOE (Per KTU Module 1):**
> 1. **Efficiency** ŌĆö Extract maximum information from minimum experimental runs.
> 2. **Validity** ŌĆö Draw statistically valid, defensible conclusions with quantified confidence.
> 3. **Generality** ŌĆö Generalize results beyond the tested conditions (via proper randomization and replication).

Real-world engineering processes are rarely simple. They are noisy, multivariate, and have hidden interactions. The standard metrics used in industrial DOE are:
- **S/N Ratio (Signal-to-Noise Ratio):** a measure of robustness, target value = **Ōł×** (ideal).
- **R┬▓ (Coefficient of Determination):** ranges from **0 to 1**, with **1.0** indicating perfect model fit.
- **Significance Level (╬▒):** typically fixed at **0.05 (5%)** in KTU exam problems.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of a DOE Study

Every properly structured DOE application follows a standardized seven-step pipeline. Understanding this pipeline is essential for answering Module 1 conceptual questions.

1. **Problem Definition (Recognition Phase):** Clearly state the engineering objective. Is it *comparison*, *screening*, *modeling*, or *optimization*?
2. **Factor & Level Identification:** Decide which input variables to control, what their low and high values will be, and whether any variables must be held constant (*nuisance factors*).
3. **Selection of Experimental Design:** Choose the structure ŌĆö *Completely Randomized Design (CRD)*, *Randomized Block Design (RBD)*, *Factorial Design*, *Taguchi Method*, *Response Surface Methodology (RSM)*, or *Mixture Design*.
4. **Randomization & Replication:** Randomly assign the order of runs to neutralize the effect of uncontrolled time-related variables; replicate runs to estimate the experimental error variance.
5. **Execution:** Conduct the experiment precisely as per the plan. *Discipline here is non-negotiable.*
6. **Statistical Analysis:** Use *ANOVA (Analysis of Variance)*, *regression*, or *S/N ratio* analysis to extract signal from noise.
7. **Conclusions & Confirmation Run:** Predict the optimum, then perform a *confirmation experiment* to verify the result.

### 2.2 Typical Engineering Applications of DOE

The KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus emphasizes the *application breadth* of DOE. The following domain-wise classification is a high-yield area for short-answer questions.

#### A. Manufacturing & Industrial Engineering
- **CNC Machining Optimization:** Optimizing cutting speed, feed rate, and depth of cut to minimize surface roughness and tool wear.
- **Welding Process Improvement:** Studying the effect of current, voltage, and electrode type on weld tensile strength.
- **Injection Molding:** Studying temperature, pressure, and cooling time to minimize shrinkage defects.

#### B. Pharmaceutical & Biomedical
- **Drug Formulation:** Optimizing the ratio of active ingredient to binder to maximize tablet dissolution rate.
- **Clinical Trials:** Comparing the effectiveness of multiple dosages in a controlled, randomized manner.
- **Bioprocess Engineering:** Maximizing enzyme yield by tuning pH, temperature, and substrate concentration.

#### C. Agriculture & Food Technology
- **Crop Yield Studies:** Factorial experiments to evaluate the effect of fertilizer, irrigation, and pesticide combinations.
- **Food Preservation:** Studying thermal processing time, salt concentration, and storage temperature on shelf life.

#### D. Chemical & Process Engineering
- **Catalyst Development:** Evaluating catalyst type, temperature, and pressure for maximum conversion.
- **Distillation Column Tuning:** Optimizing reflux ratio and tray efficiency.
- **Polymer Synthesis:** Studying monomer ratio, initiator concentration, and reaction time on molecular weight.

#### E. Software, Marketing & Service Industries
- **A/B Testing in Web Design:** Comparing two or more webpage versions to maximize click-through rate.
- **Quality of Service (QoS):** Studying server load, response time, and packet size in network engineering.
- **Taguchi's Quality Philosophy:** Loss-to-society minimization in automobile and electronics manufacturing.

### 2.3 The Three Broad Goals of Applying DOE (KTU-Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every DOE application in industry maps to one or more of these three goals:
> 1. **Screening (Factor Identification):** Identify the few vital few factors among many trivial many. ŌåÆ *Plackett-Burman, 2-level Fractional Factorial*.
> 2. **Modeling (Characterization):** Quantify how responses change with factor levels; detect interactions. ŌåÆ *Full Factorial, 2ßĄÅ Designs*.
> 3. **Optimization (Refinement):** Find the exact factor settings that produce the best response. ŌåÆ *RSM (CCD, Box-Behnken), Taguchi Robust Design*.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Total Number of Runs (2ßĄÅ Full Factorial) | $N = 2^k$ | Dimensionless; $k$ = number of factors |
| Number of Replicate Runs Needed | $N_{total} = N \times r$ | $r$ = number of replications |
| Sum of Squares Total (SST) | $SST = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | Units of (Response)┬▓ |
| Mean Square Error (MSE) | $MSE = \dfrac{SSE}{N - 1}$ | Estimate of error variance $\sigma^2$ |
| Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{residual}}{SS_{total}}$ | Range: $0 \le R^2 \le 1$ |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Larger is Better) | $\dfrac{S}{N} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{y_i^2}\right)$ | Units: **decibels (dB)** |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Smaller is Better) | $\dfrac{S}{N} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}y_i^2\right)$ | Units: **decibels (dB)** |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Nominal is Best) | $\dfrac{S}{N} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\bar{y}^2}{s^2}\right)$ | Units: **decibels (dB)** |
| Confidence Interval for Mean | $\mu = \bar{y} \pm t_{\alpha/2, n-1} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $t$ = Student's t-value |
| ANOVA F-statistic | $F = \dfrac{MS_{treatment}}{MS_{error}}$ | Compared to $F_{\alpha, a-1, N-a}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For absolute value bars inside the table above, the LaTeX `\vert` and `\log_{10}` macros are used to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. Students writing in notebooks may use plain vertical bars.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Applications to DOE Goals

This matrix directly answers the KTU Module 1 question: *"Discuss the typical applications of experimental design in various fields."* It shows the *engineering reality* of each application.

| Application Domain | Engineering Objective | Factors (Independent Variables) | Response (Dependent Variable) | Recommended DOE Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CNC Lathe Machining | Minimize surface roughness | Cutting speed, Feed rate, Depth of cut | $R_a$ (╬╝m) | Taguchi L9 / RSM |
| Tablet Formulation | Maximize drug dissolution | Binder %, Compression force, Granulation time | % Dissolved in 30 min | 2┬│ Full Factorial |
| Agricultural Yield | Maximize wheat output | Fertilizer (NPK), Irrigation, Pesticide | Yield (kg/hectare) | Split-Plot Design |
| Polymer Reaction | Maximize molecular weight | Temperature, Time, Catalyst % | $M_w$ (g/mol) | Box-Behnken RSM |
| Web A/B Testing | Maximize click-through | Headline, Color, Button position | CTR (%) | Randomized Block |
| Weld Joint Strength | Maximize tensile strength | Current, Voltage, Travel speed | UTS (MPa) | 2ßĄÅ Factorial |
| IC Chip Fabrication | Minimize defect rate | Etch time, Temperature, Pressure | Defects per million | Fractional Factorial |
| Thermal Power Plant | Maximize boiler efficiency | Air-fuel ratio, Steam temp, Load | Efficiency (%) | Response Surface |

### 3.2 Derivations of the S/N Ratio Family (Taguchi's Quality Loss Concept)

Taguchi's philosophy underpins many real-world DOE applications. The S/N ratio is a *logarithmic metric* that converts physical responses into a *decision-friendly score in decibels (dB)*. The derivations below are essential for KTU 14-mark problems.

#### 3.2.1 Case 1: Smaller is Better (SB) ŌĆö e.g., Defect Rate, Wear

The engineering loss is proportional to the *square of the response*. The mean squared deviation is the unbiased estimator.

$$
\text{MSD} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2
$$

The S/N ratio in decibels is defined as the negative ten times the log of MSD.

$$
\frac{S}{N} = -10 \log_{10}(\text{MSD})
$$

Substituting MSD:

$$
\frac{S}{N} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2\right)
$$

**Engineering Utility:** Used in industry to minimize defects, contamination, and emissions.

#### 3.2.2 Case 2: Larger is Better (LB) ŌĆö e.g., Strength, Yield, Efficiency

The signal grows with the magnitude of $y$. To convert this to a *smaller-is-better* form, we take the reciprocal.

$$
\text{MSD} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{y_i^2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{S}{N} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{y_i^2}\right)
$$

**Engineering Utility:** Used when the response must be maximized (life of a product, crop yield, weld strength).

#### 3.2.3 Case 3: Nominal is Best (NB) ŌĆö e.g., Dimension 50 mm ┬▒ 0.01 mm

The desired target is $T \neq 0$. The S/N ratio depends on both the mean $\bar{y}$ and the variance $s^2$.

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\bar{y}^2}{s^2}\right)
$$

Here, $\bar{y} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i$ and $s^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2$.

**Engineering Utility:** Tolerance-critical engineering dimensions, target pharmaceutical concentrations.

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard)

**Problem:** A research engineer is studying the effect of three factors ŌĆö Temperature (A), Pressure (B), and Catalyst (C) ŌĆö on the yield of a chemical reaction. A standard $2^3$ full factorial design with two replicates ($r = 2$) is proposed.

**(a) Calculate the total number of experimental runs required.**

For a 2-level full factorial with $k = 3$ factors:

$$
N = 2^k = 2^3 = 8 \text{ unique treatment combinations}
$$

With $r = 2$ replicates:

$$
N_{total} = N \times r = 8 \times 2 = 16 \text{ runs}
$$

**(b) If the yield data is given, set up the ANOVA table layout.**

The general ANOVA layout for a one-factor treatment structure is:

$$
SS_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \bar{y})^2
$$

$$
SS_{treatment} = \sum_{j=1}^{a} n_j (\bar{y}_j - \bar{y})^2
$$

$$
SS_{error} = SS_{total} - SS_{treatment}
$$

With degrees of freedom:
- $df_{total} = N - 1$
- $df_{treatment} = a - 1$ (where $a$ is the number of treatments)
- $df_{error} = N - a$

$$
F_{calculated} = \frac{MS_{treatment}}{MS_{error}}
$$

Decision rule: Reject $H_0$ (all treatment means are equal) if $F_{calculated} > F_{\alpha, a-1, N-a}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Insight:** The $2^3$ factorial is one of the most common applications in undergraduate research projects, capstone design, and short industrial process-improvement studies. The factorial structure allows the engineer to compute *main effects* of A, B, C and the *two-factor interactions* AB, AC, BC, and the *three-factor interaction* ABC using contrast equations.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The DOE Application Decision Tree

The diagram below maps how an engineer *chooses* an appropriate DOE method based on the application objective. This is a frequently tested conceptual area in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Engineering Objective] --> B{What is the Goal?}
    B -->|Compare Options| C[Comparison Goal]
    B -->|Identify Vital Factors| D[Screening Goal]
    B -->|Build Mathematical Model| E[Modeling Goal]
    B -->|Find Optimum Settings| F[Optimization Goal]

    C --> C1[Use t-test or Single Factor ANOVA]
    C1 --> C2[Example: Drug A vs Drug B]

    D --> D1[Use Plackett-Burman or 2 to the k Fractional]
    D1 --> D2[Example: 7 factors, 8 runs]

    E --> E1[Use 2 to the k Full Factorial]
    E1 --> E2[Example: Yield vs Temperature and Pressure]

    F --> F1{Response Surface?}
    F1 -->|Yes Curvature| F2[Use CCD or Box-Behnken]
    F1 -->|No Curvature| F3[Use Taguchi Robust Design]
    F2 --> F4[Example: Optimum weld strength]
    F3 --> F5[Example: Optimum electronic circuit]
```

### 4.2 The Seven-Step DOE Workflow Architecture

The block diagram below shows the *interactions* between the operational stages of a DOE study, mapped as a functional architecture flow. Each block represents a logical stage in the experimental pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1: Problem Definition] --> P2[Stage 2: Factor and Level Selection]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Design Selection]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4: Randomization and Replication]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5: Run the Experiment]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6: Statistical Analysis ANOVA Regression]
    P6 --> P7[Stage 7: Confirmation Run]
    P7 --> P8{Is Optimum Validated?}
    P8 -->|Yes| P9[Final Implementation]
    P8 -->|No| P3
    P6 -.->|Iterative feedback| P2
```

### 4.3 The Three-Goal Architecture of DOE Applications

This diagram visualizes the three primary motivations for applying DOE in engineering practice, as mandated by the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[Typical Applications of DOE] --> G1[Screening]
    ROOT --> G2[Modeling]
    ROOT --> G3[Optimization]

    G1 --> G1A[Identify Vital Few Factors]
    G1A --> G1B[Tool: Plackett-Burman 2 to k minus p]

    G2 --> G2A[Quantify Factor Effects and Interactions]
    G2A --> G2B[Tool: 2 to k Full Factorial]

    G3 --> G3A[Find Best Factor Settings]
    G3A --> G3B[Tool: Response Surface Methodology]
    G3A --> G3C[Tool: Taguchi Method]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows represent the *flow of decision logic*, not physical processes. Engineers typically move from Screening ŌåÆ Modeling ŌåÆ Optimization in a sequential manner as the project matures.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*Define Design of Experiments (DOE). List any two typical applications of DOE in the manufacturing industry.*

**Model Answer:**
Design of Experiments is a systematic statistical methodology for planning, conducting, and analyzing controlled tests to determine the causal relationship between independent variables (factors) and dependent variables (responses).

Two typical manufacturing applications:
1. **CNC Machining:** Optimizing cutting speed, feed, and depth of cut to minimize surface roughness.
2. **Welding Process:** Studying the effect of current, voltage, and travel speed on the tensile strength of weld joints.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**
*What is the role of randomization in experimental design? Illustrate with one example.*

**Model Answer:**
Randomization ensures that the effect of uncontrolled (nuisance) factors is *evenly distributed* across all treatments, so any observed difference is attributable to the treatment itself and not to confounding time-based or environmental factors.

**Example:** In a 4-formulation tablet dissolution study, the 12 runs are not done formulation-by-formulation; instead, the order is randomized (e.g., R1, F3, R2, F1, F4, R3...) so that ambient humidity drift does not bias the comparison.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q.A (a)** Discuss in detail the typical applications of Design of Experiments in the following fields: (i) Manufacturing Engineering, (ii) Pharmaceutical Industry, and (iii) Agriculture. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Manufacturing Engineering:**
DOE is used to optimize machining parameters (cutting speed, feed, depth of cut) for minimizing surface roughness. In welding, current, voltage, and travel speed are studied to maximize tensile strength. Injection molding parameters like temperature, pressure, and cooling time are tuned to minimize shrinkage. The Taguchi Method is widely used to design robust products that perform consistently under varied field conditions.

**(ii) Pharmaceutical Industry:**
In drug formulation, DOE is used to study the effect of excipient ratios, compression force, and granulation time on tablet hardness and dissolution rate. Clinical trials use randomized block designs to compare drug efficacy across patient groups. Fermentation processes use factorial designs to maximize antibiotic or enzyme yield by tuning pH, temperature, and aeration.

**(iii) Agriculture:**
Factorial experiments evaluate the effect of fertilizer combinations, irrigation levels, and pesticide types on crop yield. Split-plot designs account for the difficulty of randomizing irrigation methods across large fields. DOE helps determine the optimal combination that maximizes yield per hectare while minimizing input cost.

**Q.A (b)** Explain the three primary goals of applying DOE ŌĆö Screening, Modeling, and Optimization ŌĆö with suitable industrial examples. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Goal | Engineering Objective | Tool / Design | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Screening** | Identify the *vital few* factors among the *trivial many* | Plackett-Burman, $2^{k-p}$ Fractional Factorial | In a semiconductor fab, 15 process variables are screened in 12 runs to identify the 3 that most affect chip yield. |
| **Modeling** | Quantify main effects, detect interactions, build predictive equation | $2^k$ Full Factorial, ANOVA | In a CNC turning study, a $2^3$ design quantifies how feed rate and depth of cut interact to affect surface roughness. |
| **Optimization** | Find factor levels that produce the best response | RSM (CCD, Box-Behnken), Taguchi | A pharmaceutical company uses Box-Behnken RSM to find the binder %, compression force, and granulation time that maximize tablet dissolution. |

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the seven-step methodology of applying Design of Experiments to a real industrial problem. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

```
Step 1: Define the engineering problem and objective
        (e.g., maximize yield of a chemical reaction)

Step 2: Identify the factors and their levels
        (e.g., Temperature 60-80 ┬░C, Pressure 1-3 bar, Catalyst 1-3%)

Step 3: Select the appropriate experimental design
        (e.g., 2^3 Full Factorial with 2 replicates ŌåÆ 16 runs)

Step 4: Perform randomization and assign treatment order
        (use a random number table to sequence the 16 runs)

Step 5: Execute the experiment
        (record yield for each run under controlled conditions)

Step 6: Statistical analysis
        (compute main effects, interactions, run ANOVA, regression)

Step 7: Confirmation run at predicted optimum
        (validate that the predicted yield is achieved in practice)
```

**Q.B (b)** A 2-level full factorial experiment is conducted to study the effect of three factors on the strength of a weld joint. The engineer has chosen 3 replicates per treatment combination. Calculate: (i) the number of unique treatment combinations, (ii) the total number of experimental runs, and (iii) the degrees of freedom for treatment, error, and total. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Unique Treatment Combinations:**
For a 2-level full factorial with $k = 3$ factors:

$$
N = 2^k = 2^3 = 8 \text{ unique treatments}
$$

**(ii) Total Experimental Runs:**
With $r = 3$ replicates per treatment:

$$
N_{total} = N \times r = 8 \times 3 = 24 \text{ runs}
$$

**(iii) Degrees of Freedom:**

$$
df_{treatment} = N - 1 = 8 - 1 = 7
$$

$$
df_{error} = N(r - 1) = 8 \times (3 - 1) = 16
$$

$$
df_{total} = N_{total} - 1 = 24 - 1 = 23
$$

**Verification:**

$$
df_{treatment} + df_{error} = 7 + 16 = 23 = df_{total} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting Replication in Run Count:** Many students calculate only $N = 2^k$ and forget to multiply by $r$. The *total runs* in a KTU problem **always** account for replication.
> 2. **Confusing $df$ Formulas:** For one-factor ANOVA, $df_{error} = N(r-1)$, *not* $N-a$. The $N$ here is the number of treatments, not the total runs.
> 3. **Misapplying S/N Ratio Formulas:** Students often interchange the *Larger is Better* and *Smaller is Better* S/N equations. Always re-read whether the response is to be maximized or minimized.
> 4. **Ignoring Randomization in Definitions:** A surprising number of students lose 1 mark in 3-mark questions by not explicitly mentioning *randomization* in their definition of DOE.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Definition:** DOE is a *statistical, structured, and efficient* methodology to study the cause-and-effect relationship between factors and responses.
- **Three Strategic Goals:** Screening, Modeling, Optimization ŌĆö these are the *organizing themes* of every DOE application.
- **The Seven-Step Pipeline:** Problem Definition ŌåÆ Factor Selection ŌåÆ Design Choice ŌåÆ Randomization ŌåÆ Execution ŌåÆ Analysis ŌåÆ Confirmation.
- **$2^k$ Full Factorial:** Most common application in KTU problems; total runs = $2^k \times r$.
- **Taguchi S/N Ratios:** Three flavors ŌĆö Larger is Better, Smaller is Better, Nominal is Best. All expressed in **decibels (dB)**.
- **Replication & Randomization:** The *two non-negotiable principles* that ensure statistical validity.
- **Domain Breadth:** DOE is used in manufacturing, pharma, agriculture, chemical, software, marketing, and service industries.
- **ANOVA is the Workhorse:** It separates *signal* (factor effects) from *noise* (experimental error) using the F-test.
- **Confirmation Run is Mandatory:** Predicting an optimum without running a confirmation experiment is considered *incomplete* engineering practice.
- **R┬▓ Range:** Always between **0 and 1**; a value of **1.0** indicates a perfect model fit.
- **Significance Level:** KTU exam problems almost universally use **╬▒ = 0.05**.
- **Factorial Notation:** A $2^3$ design with 3 replicates has $2^3 \times 3 = 24$ runs, not 9.
- **Industrial Examples to Memorize:** CNC surface roughness, weld strength, tablet dissolution, crop yield, polymer molecular weight, and A/B web testing.
- **Key Limitation to Acknowledge:** DOE assumes a *relatively stable process* during experimentation; grossly unstable processes invalidate the analysis.
- **Connection to KTU Module 2:** The $2^k$ designs studied in Module 1 form the *foundation* for the Fractional Factorial and Response Surface designs covered later.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles of Experimental Design: Replication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Replication** is the independent repetition of the basic experiment (or treatment combinations) under identical experimental conditions, where each replication represents a **complete run of the entire experiment** carried out independently. It is one of the **three foundational principles** of Experimental Design alongside **Randomization** and **Blocking** (Local Control).

Mathematically, if a treatment $T_i$ is applied to $r$ experimental units independently, the observed data becomes:

$$
y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, r
$$

where $r$ denotes the **number of replications** of treatment $i$, and each $\epsilon_{ij}$ is an **independent** error term.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Multiple Taste Testers" Intuition

Imagine you bake a cake using a **new recipe (treatment)** and want to know if it tastes good.

- ŌØī **Without Replication**: You taste the cake yourself once. Your judgment may be biased, and you have no idea how variable the baking process is.
- Ō£ģ **With Replication**: You bake the **same recipe 5 separate times** and have **5 different, independent people** taste each batch.

Each independent batch is a **replicate**. Why? Because:
1. The average rating becomes a **more reliable estimate** of true recipe quality.
2. The **variation** between batches tells you how consistent your baking is (the experimental error).
3. You can now **distinguish** a real difference between recipes from random kitchen noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: Replication is *not* about taking multiple measurements from the same cake ŌĆö that's called **Repeated Measurements** or **Subsampling**. True replication means baking the cake *again from scratch*, independently.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Experimental Design

| Pillar | Purpose | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Randomization** | Eliminates systematic bias | Random allocation of treatments |
| **Replication** | Quantifies & reduces random error | Independent repeats of experiment |
| **Blocking** | Controls known nuisance variability | Grouping similar experimental units |

**Replication specifically addresses** the question: *"How precise is my estimate?"*

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Highlighted

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Replicate (r)**: A complete, independent repetition of an experimental run.
> - **Experimental Error (Žā┬▓)**: Estimated ONLY through replication.
> - **Standard Error of Mean**: $SE = \sigma / \sqrt{r}$
> - **Degrees of Freedom for Error**: $df_E = N - a$ where $a$ = number of treatments, $N$ = total observations.

# ­¤ōŚ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Replication ŌĆö Step by Step

### **Step 1: The Underlying Statistical Model**

For a single-factor experiment (CRD) with $a$ treatments and $r$ replicates:

$$
y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}
$$

- $\mu$ ŌåÆ overall true mean
- $\tau_i$ ŌåÆ true effect of $i$-th treatment
- $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ ŌåÆ independent random error

> [!NOTE]
> Without replication ($r=1$), the error $\epsilon_{ij}$ **cannot be estimated** ŌĆö you have only one observation per treatment and zero degrees of freedom for error.

### **Step 2: The Estimate of Treatment Mean**

For treatment $i$, the sample mean is:

$$
\bar{y}_{i.} = \frac{1}{r} \sum_{j=1}^{r} y_{ij}
$$

The **variance of this estimate** is the critical quantity:

$$
V(\bar{y}_{i.}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{r}
$$

**Why "Why" matters**: The variance of our *estimate* of the treatment mean shrinks as $r$ grows ŌĆö replication directly improves precision.

### **Step 3: The Role of Replication in Estimating Experimental Error**

The Mean Square Error (MSE) is computed as:

$$
MSE = \frac{SS_E}{N - a} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{a} \sum_{j=1}^{r}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i.})^2}{ar - a}
$$

This $MSE$ is the **unbiased estimator of Žā┬▓** ŌĆö and it exists **only because** of replication.

### **Step 4: Replication and Statistical Power**

Power of the test is:

$$
\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P\left(\vert F \vert > F_{\alpha, a-1, N-a} \mid H_1 \text{ true}\right)
$$

As $r$ increases, $df_E$ increases, the critical $F$ value decreases, and the **non-centrality parameter** $\lambda = \sum \tau_i^2 / \sigma^2$ effectively grows, **boosting power**.

### **Step 5: Replication and Confidence Interval Width**

For comparing two treatment means, the $(1-\alpha)$ CI for $\tau_i - \tau_j$ is:

$$
\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{j.} \pm t_{\alpha/2, \, ar-a} \sqrt{\frac{2 MSE}{r}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The half-width (margin of error) is **inversely proportional to $\sqrt{r}$**. To halve the CI width, you need **4├Ś the replicates** ŌĆö a fundamental KTU-board-tested result.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **minimum required formula set** for solving KTU 2024 exam problems on Replication. **Memorize these.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V(\bar{y}_{i.}) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{r}$ | Variance of treatment mean estimate |
| 2 | $SE(\bar{y}_{i.}) = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$ | Standard error of treatment mean |
| 3 | $SE(\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{j.}) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\sigma^2}{r}}$ | SE for difference of two treatment means |
| 4 | $df_E = ar - a = a(r-1)$ | Error degrees of freedom |
| 5 | $\text{CI width} = 2 t_{\alpha/2,\, df_E} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$ | Precision measure |
| 6 | $r_{\text{new}} = r_{\text{old}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\text{New SE}}{\text{Old SE}}\right)^{-2}$ | Replicates needed to achieve target SE |
| 7 | $MS_E = \dfrac{SS_E}{a(r-1)}$ | Mean square error (unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$) |
| 8 | $F = \dfrac{MS_T}{MS_E}$ | ANOVA test statistic ŌĆö powered by replication |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Replication |
|---|---|
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Multiple batches of drug formulation to test consistency |
| **Manufacturing** | Running the same process multiple times for quality control |
| **Agriculture** | Testing a fertilizer on multiple plots under similar soil conditions |
| **Materials Science** | Producing multiple specimens to test tensile strength |
| **Semiconductor Industry** | Wafer-to-wafer variability assessment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard**: Most Six-Sigma and ISO-certified industrial experiments require **a minimum of 3 replicates** to estimate experimental error reliably.

# ­¤ōÖ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why 4├Ś the Replicates Halves the Standard Error

**Statement**: To halve the standard error of a treatment mean, you must increase the number of replicates by a factor of 4.

**Proof**:

We begin with the standard error formula:

$$
SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}
$$

Let the original number of replicates be $r_1$ and the new number be $r_2$. Let the corresponding standard errors be $SE_1$ and $SE_2$.

$$
SE_1 = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r_1}}
$$

$$
SE_2 = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r_2}}
$$

We require the new standard error to be half of the original:

$$
SE_2 = \frac{1}{2} SE_1
$$

Substituting the expressions for $SE_1$ and $SE_2$:

$$
\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r_2}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r_1}}
$$

Simplifying both sides by cancelling $\sigma$ (a positive constant):

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{r_2}} = \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{r_1}}
$$

Taking the reciprocal of both sides:

$$
\sqrt{r_2} = 2 \sqrt{r_1}
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
r_2 = 4 r_1
$$

**Conclusion**: To halve the standard error (and hence halve the confidence interval width), the number of replicates must be **quadrupled**. This is the **square-root law of replication**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: General Formula for Required Replicates to Achieve Target Precision

**Problem**: You have $r_1$ replicates and standard error $SE_1$. How many replicates $r_2$ do you need to achieve a target standard error $SE_2$?

Starting from the square-root law relationship:

$$
\frac{SE_1}{SE_2} = \frac{\sqrt{r_2}}{\sqrt{r_1}}
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
\left(\frac{SE_1}{SE_2}\right)^2 = \frac{r_2}{r_1}
$$

Solving for $r_2$:

$$
r_2 = r_1 \cdot \left(\frac{SE_1}{SE_2}\right)^2
$$

Equivalently, since $SE \propto 1/\sqrt{r}$:

$$
r_2 = r_1 \cdot \left(\frac{\text{Desired precision}}{\text{Current precision}}\right)^{-2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently tested replication formula** in KTU Module-1 numerical questions. Memorize it.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1: Replicates Required for Target CI Width

**Problem**: A process engineer is studying 4 treatments with 3 replicates each. The current standard error of a treatment mean is $2.0$ units. The engineer wants to reduce the standard error to $0.5$ units. How many total replicates per treatment are needed?

**Solution**:

Given:
- $r_1 = 3$ replicates
- $SE_1 = 2.0$ units
- $SE_2 = 0.5$ units (target)
- $r_2 = ?$

Applying the derived formula:

$$
r_2 = r_1 \cdot \left(\frac{SE_1}{SE_2}\right)^2
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
r_2 = 3 \cdot \left(\frac{2.0}{0.5}\right)^2
$$

$$
r_2 = 3 \cdot (4)^2
$$

$$
r_2 = 3 \cdot 16 = 48
$$

**Answer**: **48 replicates per treatment** are required to achieve the desired precision.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2: ANOVA with Replication (CRD)

**Problem**: A study compares 3 fertilizers on wheat yield. Each fertilizer is applied to 4 plots. The following data are observed (in kg/plot):

| Fertilizer | R1 | R2 | R3 | R4 | Total | Mean |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | 20 | 22 | 21 | 19 | 82 | 20.5 |
| F2 | 25 | 24 | 26 | 23 | 98 | 24.5 |
| F3 | 18 | 17 | 20 | 19 | 74 | 18.5 |
| | | | | | **G = 254** | |

**Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the fertilizers differ significantly.**

**Solution**:

**Step 1: Compute the Correction Factor (CF)**

$$
CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{(254)^2}{12}
$$

$$
CF = \frac{64516}{12} = 5376.3333
$$

**Step 2: Compute Total Sum of Squares (SST)**

$$
SS_T = \sum y_{ij}^2 - CF
$$

Calculating $\sum y_{ij}^2$:

- F1: $20^2 + 22^2 + 21^2 + 19^2 = 400 + 484 + 441 + 361 = 1686$
- F2: $25^2 + 24^2 + 26^2 + 23^2 = 625 + 576 + 676 + 529 = 2406$
- F3: $18^2 + 17^2 + 20^2 + 19^2 = 324 + 289 + 400 + 361 = 1374$

$$
\sum y_{ij}^2 = 1686 + 2406 + 1374 = 5466
$$

$$
SS_T = 5466 - 5376.3333 = 89.6667
$$

**Step 3: Compute Treatment Sum of Squares (SS_Treat)**

$$
SS_{\text{Treat}} = \frac{\sum T_i^2}{r} - CF
$$

where $T_i$ is the total for treatment $i$ and $r = 4$ replicates.

$$
SS_{\text{Treat}} = \frac{(82)^2 + (98)^2 + (74)^2}{4} - 5376.3333
$$

$$
SS_{\text{Treat}} = \frac{6724 + 9604 + 5476}{4} - 5376.3333
$$

$$
SS_{\text{Treat}} = \frac{21804}{4} - 5376.3333
$$

$$
SS_{\text{Treat}} = 5451 - 5376.3333 = 74.6667
$$

**Step 4: Compute Error Sum of Squares (SSE)**

$$
SS_E = SS_T - SS_{\text{Treat}}
$$

$$
SS_E = 89.6667 - 74.6667 = 15.0000
$$

**Step 5: Construct the ANOVA Table**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Treatments | 74.6667 | 2 | 37.3333 | 17.44 |
| Error | 15.0000 | 9 | 1.6667 | |
| **Total** | **89.6667** | **11** | | |

**Step 6: Compare with Tabulated F**

$F_{0.05, \, 2, 9} = 4.26$ (from F-tables).

Since calculated $F = 17.44 > 4.26$, we **reject $H_0$** and conclude that the fertilizers differ significantly at the 5% level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that **replication ($r = 4$) gave us 9 error degrees of freedom**, allowing estimation of $MS_E = 1.6667$ and a meaningful F-test. Without replication, no such test would be possible.

# ­¤ōÆ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Replication. How is it different from Repetition?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

**Definition (1.5 Marks)**: Replication is the **independent repetition of an entire experiment** (or treatment combination) under identical conditions. Each replicate is a fresh, complete run of the experiment performed on a new experimental unit.

**Difference (1.5 Marks)**:
- **Replication**: Multiple independent runs of the *whole experiment* ŌĆö captures run-to-run variability and provides an estimate of experimental error.
- **Repetition**: Multiple measurements taken from the *same experimental unit* ŌĆö does NOT capture run-to-run variability and gives an artificially low error estimate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often confuse repetition with replication. Repetition on the same unit **underestimates true variability** and is a methodological error.

---

### **Q2. State the role of replication in estimating experimental error.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

Replication plays **three critical roles** in estimating experimental error:

1. **Quantification of Žā┬▓** (1 Mark): The Mean Square Error $MS_E = \dfrac{SS_E}{a(r-1)}$ is an unbiased estimator of the experimental error variance $\sigma^2$. This estimate is **impossible** without replication.

2. **Degrees of Freedom for Error** (1 Mark): Replication provides $df_E = a(r-1)$ error degrees of freedom, which is essential for the F-test and t-test critical values.

3. **Reduction of Standard Error** (1 Mark): The standard error of a treatment mean is $SE = \sigma/\sqrt{r}$, so increasing $r$ directly improves the precision of the treatment mean estimate, following the **square-root law**.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Q(a). Explain in detail the principles of Replication in experimental design. Discuss how replication improves precision and increases the power of the statistical test.**

**Model Solution**:

**1. Definition of Replication (1 Mark)**:
Replication is the independent repetition of the basic experiment under identical conditions. If there are $a$ treatments and $r$ replications, the total number of observations is $N = a \cdot r$.

**2. Replication Improves Precision (3 Marks)**:
The variance of the treatment mean is:

$$
V(\bar{y}_{i.}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{r}
$$

The standard error is $SE = \sigma/\sqrt{r}$. As $r$ increases, $SE$ decreases proportionally to $1/\sqrt{r}$. The $(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval for $\mu_i$ is:

$$
\bar{y}_{i.} \pm t_{\alpha/2, \, df_E} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}
$$

Hence, the **width of the confidence interval** shrinks as $\sqrt{r}$ grows, increasing the precision of the estimate of the treatment mean.

**3. Replication Increases Power (2 Marks)**:
Statistical power is the probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis. As $r$ increases:
- The non-centrality parameter of the F-distribution increases.
- The error degrees of freedom $df_E = a(r-1)$ increase, reducing the critical F-value.
- The estimate of $\sigma^2$ becomes more stable.

These factors together **increase the probability** of detecting true treatment differences.

**4. Replication Enables Estimation of Experimental Error (1 Mark)**:
With $r \geq 2$ replicates per treatment, the within-treatment variability can be computed, providing an unbiased estimate $MS_E$ of $\sigma^2$.

---

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Q(b). An experiment involves 4 treatments with 6 replicates each. The treatment means are $\bar{y}_1 = 25, \bar{y}_2 = 30, \bar{y}_3 = 28, \bar{y}_4 = 32$. The pooled estimate of $\sigma^2$ from the ANOVA is $MS_E = 4.5$. Construct 95% confidence intervals for all pairwise differences of treatment means using the LSD method.**

**Model Solution**:

**Given**:
- $a = 4$ treatments, $r = 6$ replicates, $N = 24$
- $MS_E = 4.5$
- $df_E = a(r-1) = 4 \times 5 = 20$
- $t_{0.025, 20} = 2.086$ (from t-tables)

**Step 1: Standard Error of Difference** (1 Mark)

$$
SE(\bar{y}_{i.} - \bar{y}_{j.}) = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot MS_E}{r}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4.5}{6}} = \sqrt{1.5} = 1.2247
$$

**Step 2: LSD Value** (1 Mark)

$$
LSD = t_{\alpha/2, \, df_E} \cdot SE = 2.086 \times 1.2247 = 2.555
$$

**Step 3: Compute All Pairwise Differences and CIs** (5 Marks)

| Pair | $\bar{y}_i - \bar{y}_j$ | CI Lower | CI Upper | Significant? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 vs 2 | $25 - 30 = -5$ | $-5 - 2.555 = -7.555$ | $-5 + 2.555 = -2.445$ | Yes (CI excludes 0) |
| 1 vs 3 | $25 - 28 = -3$ | $-5.555$ | $-0.445$ | Yes |
| 1 vs 4 | $25 - 32 = -7$ | $-9.555$ | $-4.445$ | Yes |
| 2 vs 3 | $30 - 28 = +2$ | $-0.555$ | $4.555$ | No (CI includes 0) |
| 2 vs 4 | $30 - 32 = -2$ | $-4.555$ | $0.555$ | No |
| 3 vs 4 | $28 - 32 = -4$ | $-6.555$ | $-1.445$ | Yes |

**Conclusion**: Treatments 1, 3, 4 form two distinct groups, while Treatment 2 overlaps with both Treatments 1 and 3 ŌĆö i.e., it is not significantly different from 1 or 3 but is significantly lower than 4.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Many students forget to take the **square root** of $2 \cdot MS_E / r$ before multiplying by $t$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in valuation. Also, the CI for a difference $\bar{y}_i - \bar{y}_j$ is **symmetric** about the point estimate ŌĆö students often compute only one side.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Q(a). Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of replication. How is the optimal number of replicates determined?**

**Model Solution**:

**Advantages of Replication** (4 Marks):

1. **Provides an estimate of experimental error** ($\sigma^2$) ŌĆö without replication, $MS_E$ cannot be computed.
2. **Reduces the standard error** of treatment means: $SE = \sigma/\sqrt{r}$.
3. **Increases the precision** of treatment mean estimates (narrower confidence intervals).
4. **Increases statistical power** ŌĆö ability to detect smaller true differences as significant.
5. **Increases the scope of inference** ŌĆö broader validity of conclusions.
6. **Increases error degrees of freedom** ŌĆö more robust F-tests.

**Disadvantages of Replication** (2 Marks):

1. **Increased cost** ŌĆö more materials, time, labor, and equipment required.
2. **Increased experimental error variance** if not properly randomized ŌĆö large experiments are harder to control.
3. **Diminishing returns** ŌĆö due to the square-root law, going from $r=20$ to $r=21$ gives very little precision gain.

**Determination of Optimal $r$** (1 Mark):

The optimal number of replicates is found by balancing **precision** against **cost**:

$$
r_{\text{optimal}} = \arg \min_r \left[ C(r) + \lambda \cdot V(\bar{y}) \right]
$$

where $C(r)$ is the total cost of $r$ replicates and $\lambda$ is the cost per unit of variance.

---

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Q(b). A quality control engineer is testing the effect of 5 catalysts on reaction yield. Currently, 3 replicates are run per catalyst, and the standard error of a treatment mean is $3.0$ g. The engineer wants to reduce the SE to $1.0$ g. (i) How many replicates are required per catalyst? (ii) If each additional replicate costs Ōé╣500, what is the additional cost per catalyst?**

**Model Solution**:

**Given**:
- $r_1 = 3$, $SE_1 = 3.0$ g
- $SE_2 = 1.0$ g (target)
- Cost per additional replicate = Ōé╣500

**Part (i): Required Replicates** (4 Marks)

Using the formula derived in Section 3.2:

$$
r_2 = r_1 \cdot \left(\frac{SE_1}{SE_2}\right)^2
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
r_2 = 3 \cdot \left(\frac{3.0}{1.0}\right)^2
$$

$$
r_2 = 3 \cdot (3)^2
$$

$$
r_2 = 3 \cdot 9 = 27
$$

**Answer**: **27 replicates per catalyst** are required.

**Part (ii): Additional Cost per Catalyst** (3 Marks)

Number of additional replicates needed per catalyst:

$$
\Delta r = r_2 - r_1 = 27 - 3 = 24
$$

Additional cost per catalyst:

$$
\text{Cost} = 24 \times 500 = \text{Ōé╣ } 12{,}000
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often confuse the ratio ŌĆö the formula is $(SE_1 / SE_2)^2$, **not** $(SE_1 / SE_2)$ linearly. The squaring step is the most common error and costs **2 marks** in the board exam.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 1: Replication**

- Ō£ģ **Definition**: Replication = **independent, complete repetition** of an experiment (NOT repetition of measurements).
- Ō£ģ **Three Roles**: (1) Estimate $\sigma^2$, (2) Increase $df_E$, (3) Reduce $SE$ of mean.
- Ō£ģ **Square-Root Law**: $SE \propto 1/\sqrt{r}$ ŌåÆ **4├Ś replicates to halve SE**.
- Ō£ģ **Formula for Required Replicates**: $r_2 = r_1 \cdot (SE_1 / SE_2)^2$.
- Ō£ģ **Degrees of Freedom for Error**: $df_E = a(r-1)$ (in single-factor CRD).
- Ō£ģ **MSE Unbiased Estimator**: $MS_E = SS_E / [a(r-1)]$ estimates $\sigma^2$ ŌĆö **possible only with replication**.
- Ō£ģ **Replication vs Repetition**: Replication = independent runs; Repetition = multiple measurements on the same unit.
- Ō£ģ **Minimum Industry Standard**: Generally $r \geq 3$ replicates per treatment.
- Ō£ģ **CI Width**: Proportional to $1/\sqrt{r}$ ŌĆö so $\sqrt{r}$ times more data narrows CI by factor of $r$.
- Ō£ģ **Power Boost**: Increasing $r$ increases $df_E$, lowers critical F, and increases the chance of detecting true differences.
- Ō£ģ **Trade-off**: Replication costs resources ŌĆö balance precision against budget using $r_{\text{optimal}}$.

> ­¤Ä» **High-Yield Mantra for KTU Boards**: *"Replication reduces variance by $1/r$, but costs money linearly ŌĆö so the square-root law is your best friend."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Randomization and Blocking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Randomization and Blocking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Randomization ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Randomization** is the cornerstone principle of Design of Experiments (DOE), introduced formally by **Sir Ronald A. Fisher (1935)**. It is defined as the process of allocating experimental units to treatments using a **chance mechanism** (such as a random number table, computer-generated random numbers, or drawing lots) such that every unit has an **equal, independent, and unbiased probability** of receiving any treatment.

In mathematical terms, randomization ensures that the treatments are **stochastically independent** of the experimental units and of the order in which the runs are performed. This is what gives the experimenter the right to apply the **F-test, t-test, and confidence intervals** ŌĆö because randomization validates the assumption that the experimental errors are **independently and identically distributed (i.i.d.) Normal random variables**.

### 1.2 Blocking ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blocking** (also called **Local Control** or **Stratification**) is a design strategy in which homogeneous experimental units are grouped together into **blocks**, and within each block, the treatments are assigned **randomly**. The block is treated as a factor in the analysis to **remove** the variability it contributes from the experimental error.

A **block** is a set of experimental units that are as similar as possible with respect to the nuisance variable being controlled. Common examples of blocking variables include: **batches of raw material, operators, days, machines, shifts, ovens, and laboratories**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Classroom Test" Analogy

Imagine you are a professor wanting to compare **3 teaching methods (A, B, C)** on student performance.

- **Without Randomization**: You simply teach Method A in the morning session, Method B in the afternoon, and Method C in the evening. But the morning students may be fresher, the afternoon ones may be tired ŌĆö your comparison is now **contaminated** with the time-of-day effect. This is **BIAS**.

- **With Randomization Only**: You randomly pick students from a single large pool and assign them to A, B, C. This removes the time-of-day bias, but students of vastly different abilities end up together in each group, increasing the **noise (error variance)**.

- **With Randomization + Blocking (Local Control)**: You first group students by **ability** (high, medium, low) ŌĆö these are your **blocks**. Within each block, you randomly assign the three methods. Now the teaching methods are compared under **nearly equal conditions** of student ability. The result: a **sharper, more precise comparison** of the methods.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway**:
> - **Randomization** = eliminates **bias** (systematic error)
> - **Blocking** = reduces **noise** (random error)
> - Together, they enhance the **precision** and **validity** of the experiment.

### 1.4 Why These Principles Matter (KTU Emphasis)

- The **General Linear Model** and **ANOVA (Analysis of Variance)** procedures used in DOE rely entirely on the assumption that the experimental error is a **random, independent, N(0, Žā┬▓)** variable. Only randomization can justify this assumption in a real experiment.
- Blocking allows the analyst to **partition the total sum of squares** into meaningful components (Treatment, Block, Error), thereby **increasing the power** of the F-test used to detect treatment differences.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fisher's Three Principles of Experimental Design

According to **R. A. Fisher**, every well-conducted experiment must satisfy **three fundamental principles**. Together, they form the philosophical foundation of all classical DOE techniques taught in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus.

#### 2.1.1 Replication
- Repeating the **entire experiment** (all treatments) under identical conditions, **r** times.
- Provides an **independent estimate of experimental error (Žā┬▓)**.
- Increases **precision** of treatment mean estimates: Standard Error of mean = $\sigma/\sqrt{r}$.
- **Loss Function**: If r = 1, no error can be estimated ŌĆö the experiment is **invalid** for statistical inference.

#### 2.1.2 Randomization
- Allocation of treatments to experimental units is done by a **random mechanism**.
- Distributes the effect of **uncontrolled nuisance variables** equally across all treatments.
- Ensures that the errors are **independent** and the statistical tests are **valid**.
- Prevents **lurking variables** (hidden systematic effects) from biasing the conclusions.

#### 2.1.3 Blocking (Local Control)
- Grouping experimental units that are **homogeneous** with respect to a known nuisance variable.
- Each block is a **complete replication** of the treatments (in Randomized Block Design).
- Removes the **between-block variability** from the experimental error.
- Increases the **relative precision** of treatment comparisons ŌĆö equivalent to gaining replications "for free" with respect to the within-block variation.

### 2.2 Statistical Linear Models for CRD and RBD

The treatment of the random error $\epsilon$ in these models is what randomization and blocking allow us to **justify** mathematically.

**Completely Randomized Design (CRD) ŌĆö Single Factor:**
$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, t; \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, r$$

**Randomized Block Design (RBD) ŌĆö Two Factors (Treatment + Block):**
$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, t; \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, b$$

Where:
