- $y_{ij}$ = observed response for the $i^{th}$ treatment in the $j^{th}$ block
- $\mu$ = overall (grand) mean effect
- $\tau_i$ = effect of the $i^{th}$ treatment, with $\sum \tau_i = 0$ (constraint)
- $\beta_j$ = effect of the $j^{th}$ block, with $\sum \beta_j = 0$ (constraint)
- $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$, **independent** ŌĆö guaranteed by **randomization** within blocks.

### 2.3 Conditions for Valid Application of Blocking

A variable qualifies as a good **blocking factor** when:
- It is **known** and **measurable** (not a "lurking" variable).
- It can be controlled at the **design stage** (not at the analysis stage).
- The blocks are **complete** (every treatment appears in every block) ŌĆö for the standard RBD.
- Within a block, the units are **as homogeneous as possible**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Randomization & Blocking |
|---|---|
| **Pharmaceutical R&D** | Blocking on patient age/sex; randomizing drug vs. placebo assignment in clinical trials |
| **Manufacturing (Six Sigma)** | Blocking on machine, operator, or raw-material batch; randomizing run order to neutralize tool wear |
| **Agriculture (Agronomy)** | Blocking on soil fertility gradients in field plots; randomizing fertilizer treatments |
| **Chemical Engineering** | Blocking on catalyst batch, reactor run day; randomizing temperature/pressure settings |
| **Software / A/B Testing** | Blocking on user demographics; random assignment of users to UI variants |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive high-yield formula list** for KTU 2024 Scheme examination questions on Randomization and Blocking. Master these symbols and you can solve **every** Part B question on this topic.

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grand Mean | $\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{b} y_{ij}}{tb}$ | Overall average response | depends on $y$ |
| Treatment Mean | $\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{b} y_{ij}}{b}$ | Average of $i^{th}$ treatment across blocks | depends on $y$ |
| Block Mean | $\bar{y}_{\cdot j} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{t} y_{ij}}{t}$ | Average of $j^{th}$ block across treatments | depends on $y$ |
| $SS_{Total}$ | $\sum_{i}\sum_{j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$ | Total variability | $(\text{units of } y)^2$ |
| $SS_{Treatment}$ | $b \sum_{i=1}^{t}(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$ | Variability due to treatments | $(\text{units of } y)^2$ |
| $SS_{Block}$ | $t \sum_{j=1}^{b}(\bar{y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$ | Variability due to blocks | $(\text{units of } y)^2$ |
| $SS_{Error}$ | $SS_{Total} - SS_{Treatment} - SS_{Block}$ | Residual (within-block) variability | $(\text{units of } y)^2$ |
| d.f. Total | $tb - 1$ | Total degrees of freedom | dimensionless |
| d.f. Treatment | $t - 1$ | Treatment degrees of freedom | dimensionless |
| d.f. Block | $b - 1$ | Block degrees of freedom | dimensionless |
| d.f. Error | $(t-1)(b-1)$ | Error degrees of freedom | dimensionless |
| $MS_{Treatment}$ | $SS_{Treatment} / (t-1)$ | Mean square for treatments | $(\text{units of } y)^2$ |
| $MS_{Block}$ | $SS_{Block} / (b-1)$ | Mean square for blocks | $(\text{units of } y)^2$ |
| $MS_{Error}$ | $SS_{Error} / [(t-1)(b-1)]$ | Mean square error (MSE) | $(\text{units of } y)^2$ |
| F-statistic (Treatment) | $F_0 = MS_{Treatment} / MS_{Error}$ | Test statistic for $H_0: \tau_i = 0$ | dimensionless |
| F-statistic (Block) | $F_0 = MS_{Block} / MS_{Error}$ | Test statistic for $H_0: \beta_j = 0$ | dimensionless |
| Relative Efficiency | $RE = \dfrac{\sigma^2_{CRD}}{\sigma^2_{RBD}} = \dfrac{MS_{E,CRD}}{MS_{E,RBD}}\cdot\dfrac{(b+1)(t+1)}{b\cdot t + 1}$ | Gain from blocking (F-test based) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention for KTU 2024**: In the F-ratio, **MS of the factor being tested is always in the numerator** and **MS_{Error} is always in the denominator**. A common error is to place MS_{Error} on top ŌĆö examiners deduct marks for this.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Blocking Reduces Error Variance

Let the total variability in any experiment be partitioned. The total corrected sum of squares is the fundamental identity upon which **all ANOVA tables in KTU exams** are built.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Begin with the Total Sum of Squares.**
$$SS_{Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{b} (y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Introduce the Block and Treatment Means Algebraically.**

Add and subtract $\bar{y}_{i\cdot} + \bar{y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}$ inside the parenthesis. This is the **standard ANOVA algebraic trick** that decomposes the total deviation.

$$y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot} = (y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}) + (\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}) + (\bar{y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Square and Sum Over All Indices.**

Define the three deviation components:
- $A_{ij} = y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}$  (residual, or "true" within-block error)
- $B_i = \bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}$  (treatment deviation)
- $C_j = \bar{y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}$  (block deviation)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Expand the Squared Sum.**

$$SS_{Total} = \sum\sum A_{ij}^2 + b\sum_{i=1}^{t} B_i^2 + t\sum_{j=1}^{b} C_j^2 + 2\sum\sum A_{ij}B_i + 2\sum\sum A_{ij}C_j + 2\sum_i\sum_j B_i C_j$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Show the Cross-Products Vanish.**

By construction:
- $\sum_{j=1}^{b} A_{ij} = 0$ for every $i$ ŌćÆ cross-product $\sum\sum A_{ij}B_i = 0$
- $\sum_{i=1}^{t} A_{ij} = 0$ for every $j$ ŌćÆ cross-product $\sum\sum A_{ij}C_j = 0$
- $\sum_i B_i = 0$ and $\sum_j C_j = 0$ ŌćÆ cross-product $\sum_i\sum_j B_i C_j = 0$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final Partition Identity.**

This is the **fundamental ANOVA decomposition** (the cornerstone identity for KTU 2024 Part B answers):

$$SS_{Total} = SS_{Error} + SS_{Treatment} + SS_{Block}$$

where:
$$SS_{Error} = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\sum_{j=1}^{b} (y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$$
$$SS_{Treatment} = b\sum_{i=1}^{t}(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$$
$$SS_{Block} = t\sum_{j=1}^{b}(\bar{y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$$

**Why This Matters for Blocking**:
- In a **CRD**, $SS_{Error}^{CRD} = SS_{Error}^{RBD} + SS_{Block}$
- Hence $MS_{Error}^{RBD} < MS_{Error}^{CRD}$ (because the between-block variability is "stripped off").
- This reduced $MS_{Error}$ **increases the F-ratio** for treatments, making the experiment **more powerful** (more sensitive to true treatment effects).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: RBD ANOVA Table (Standard KTU Format)

This is the **exact format** expected by KTU 2024 examiners. Memorize the structure.

| Source of Variation | Sum of Squares (SS) | d.f. | Mean Square (MS) | Expected Mean Square (EMS) | F-ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Treatments | $SS_{Tr} = b\sum_i(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$ | $t - 1$ | $MS_{Tr}$ | $\sigma^2 + \dfrac{b\sum\tau_i^2}{t-1}$ | $MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ |
| Blocks | $SS_{Bl} = t\sum_j(\bar{y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$ | $b - 1$ | $MS_{Bl}$ | $\sigma^2 + \dfrac{t\sum\beta_j^2}{b-1}$ | $MS_{Bl}/MS_E$ |
| Error | $SS_E = SS_{Total} - SS_{Tr} - SS_{Bl}$ | $(t-1)(b-1)$ | $MS_E$ | $\sigma^2$ | ŌĆö |
| **Total** | $SS_{Total} = \sum\sum(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$ | $tb - 1$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test Decisions (KTU 2024 Standard)**:
> - $H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \dots = \tau_t = 0$ (all treatments equal). Reject $H_0$ if $F_0 = MS_{Tr}/MS_E > F_{\alpha,\,t-1,\,(t-1)(b-1)}$
> - $H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \dots = \beta_b = 0$ (all blocks equal). Reject $H_0$ if $F_0 = MS_{Bl}/MS_E > F_{\alpha,\,b-1,\,(t-1)(b-1)}$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example ŌĆö RBD with 3 Treatments and 4 Blocks

> This is a **typical 14-mark KTU 2024 Part B problem**. The data below represents the yield (in kg) of three fertilizer formulations (A, B, C) tested across four fields (blocks) of varying soil quality.

**Data Table:**

| Block | Treatment A | Treatment B | Treatment C | Block Total $B_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | 42 | 39 | 38 | 119 |
| II | 43 | 41 | 42 | 126 |
| III | 45 | 44 | 43 | 132 |
| IV | 48 | 46 | 44 | 138 |
| **Treatment Total $T_i$** | **178** | **170** | **167** | **515** |

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Correction Factor (CF).**
$$CF = \dfrac{(\sum y_{ij})^2}{tb} = \dfrac{(515)^2}{3 \cdot 4} = \dfrac{265225}{12} = 22102.0833$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $SS_{Total}$ (raw sum of squares minus CF).**

$\sum y_{ij}^2 = 42^2 + 39^2 + 38^2 + 43^2 + 41^2 + 42^2 + 45^2 + 44^2 + 43^2 + 48^2 + 46^2 + 44^2$
$= 1764 + 1521 + 1444 + 1849 + 1681 + 1764 + 2025 + 1936 + 1849 + 2304 + 2116 + 1936 = 22189$

$$SS_{Total} = \sum y_{ij}^2 - CF = 22189 - 22102.0833 = 86.9167$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $SS_{Treatment}$.**
$$SS_{Tr} = \dfrac{\sum T_i^2}{b} - CF = \dfrac{178^2 + 170^2 + 167^2}{4} - 22102.0833$$
$$= \dfrac{31684 + 28900 + 27889}{4} - 22102.0833 = \dfrac{88473}{4} - 22102.0833$$
$$= 22118.25 - 22102.0833 = 16.1667$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $SS_{Block}$.**
$$SS_{Bl} = \dfrac{\sum B_j^2}{t} - CF = \dfrac{119^2 + 126^2 + 132^2 + 138^2}{3} - 22102.0833$$
$$= \dfrac{14161 + 15876 + 17424 + 19044}{3} - 22102.0833 = \dfrac{66505}{3} - 22102.0833$$
$$= 22168.3333 - 22102.0833 = 66.25$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $SS_{Error}$.**
$$SS_E = SS_{Total} - SS_{Tr} - SS_{Bl} = 86.9167 - 16.1667 - 66.25 = 4.5$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Assemble the ANOVA Table.**

| Source | SS | d.f. | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Treatments | 16.1667 | 2 | 8.0833 | 10.78 |
| Blocks | 66.25 | 3 | 22.0833 | 29.44 |
| Error | 4.5 | 6 | 0.75 | ŌĆö |
| **Total** | **86.9167** | **11** | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |

**Step 7 ŌĆö F-test Decisions.**

- $F_{0.05,\,2,\,6} = 5.14$ ŌćÆ For treatments: $F_0 = 10.78 > 5.14$ ŌćÆ **Reject $H_0$** ŌćÆ Treatments differ significantly.
- $F_{0.05,\,3,\,6} = 4.76$ ŌćÆ For blocks: $F_0 = 29.44 > 4.76$ ŌćÆ **Reject $H_0$** ŌćÆ Blocking was effective; blocks differ significantly. **This confirms that blocking was necessary** in this experiment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Relationship Between Fisher's Three Principles

This block-level flow architecture illustrates how the three foundational principles of DOE interact in any valid experiment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[FISHER'S THREE PRINCIPLES OF DOE] --> B0[REPLICATION]
    A0 --> C0[RANDOMIZATION]
    A0 --> D0[BLOCKING]
    B0 --> E0[Provides independent estimate of error variance]
    C0 --> F0[Eliminates BIAS and validates statistical tests]
    D0 --> G0[Removes NOISE from known nuisance variables]
    E0 --> H0[VALID EXPERIMENT]
    F0 --> H0
    G0 --> H0
    H0 --> I0[Reliable treatment effect estimates]
    I0 --> J0[Valid F-test and confidence intervals]
    styleA0 fill:F0F8FF,stroke:003366,stroke-width:3px
    styleB0 fill:E6F3FF,stroke:003366
    styleC0 fill:FFF0E6,stroke:CC6600
    styleD0 fill:E6FFE6,stroke:006600
    styleH0 fill:FFFFCC,stroke:999900,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Experimental Design Decision Tree (CRD vs. RBD)

This schematic guides the experimenter in choosing the appropriate design based on the nature of the nuisance variables.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[START: Define objective and response variable] --> S1{Is there a known nuisance variable?}
    S1 --> S2[YES: A blocking factor can be identified]
    S1 --> S3[NO: No identifiable nuisance variables]
    S2 --> S4{Can the nuisance variable be controlled at design stage?}
    S4 --> S5[YES: Use RBD or LSD]
    S4 --> S6[NO: Use CRD with full randomization]
    S3 --> S6
    S5 --> S7[Treatments randomized within each homogeneous BLOCK]
    S6 --> S8[Complete random assignment of treatments to all units]
    S7 --> S9[Analyze using RBD ANOVA model]
    S8 --> S10[Analyze using CRD ANOVA model]
    styleS0 fill:E6F3FF,stroke:003366,stroke-width:2px
    styleS2 fill:FFF0E6,stroke:CC6600
    styleS5 fill:E6FFE6,stroke:006600
    styleS9 fill:E6FFE6,stroke:006600
    styleS6 fill:F0F0F0,stroke:666666
    styleS10 fill:F0F0F0,stroke:666666
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Information Flow of Blocking in an RBD

This block diagram depicts how the total variability in an RBD is partitioned into the three orthogonal components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Raw Experimental Data y_ij] --> Q0[Grand Mean y_doubledot]
    P0 --> R0[SS_Total: Total Variability]
    Q0 --> R0
    R0 --> T0{ANOVA Partitioning Identity}
    T0 --> U0[SS_Treatment: b times sum of treatment mean deviations squared]
    T0 --> V0[SS_Block: t times sum of block mean deviations squared]
    T0 --> W0[SS_Error: Residual within-cell variability]
    U0 --> X0[F-test for Treatments]
    V0 --> Y0[F-test for Blocks: Was blocking effective?]
    W0 --> Z0[MS_Error = Pooled Error Estimator]
    styleT0 fill:FFFFCC,stroke:999900,stroke-width:3px
    styleX0 fill:E6F3FF,stroke:003366
    styleY0 fill:FFF0E6,stroke:CC6600
    styleZ0 fill:E6FFE6,stroke:006600
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
**"Define randomization in the context of Design of Experiments. State any two of its advantages."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** Randomization is the process of assigning experimental units to treatments using a chance mechanism (such as a random number table or computer-generated random numbers) such that every unit has an equal and independent probability of receiving any treatment. It is one of the three fundamental principles of experimental design introduced by **R. A. Fisher**.
>
> **Advantages (1 Mark, any two):**
> 1. It **eliminates systematic bias** caused by uncontrolled or unknown nuisance variables.
> 2. It **validates the statistical inference** procedures (F-test, t-test) by ensuring the experimental errors are **independent** and **identically distributed** random variables.
> 3. It provides a **logical basis for the assumption of independence** of error terms $\epsilon_{ij}$ in the linear model $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$.

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]
**"What is a block in experimental design? Give a suitable engineering example of a blocking variable."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** A **block** is a group of experimental units that are **homogeneous** with respect to one or more known nuisance variables. In a Randomized Block Design (RBD), the experimental units are first partitioned into blocks, and within each block, the treatments are assigned at random. The block effect is then accounted for in the ANOVA model as an additional factor.
>
> **Engineering Example (1 Mark):** In a **manufacturing process** comparing four machine settings on product tensile strength, the **operator** (or the **shift** ŌĆö morning/evening/night) can serve as a blocking variable. By blocking on operator, the variability in operator skill is removed from the experimental error, yielding a more sensitive comparison of the machine settings.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Note):** Students often confuse "blocking" with "replication" or "repeated measures." Blocking is **not** repeating the same unit; it is grouping **different units** that share a common characteristic. Do not write that blocking means "doing the experiment twice."

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern**, every Part B question features a **Module Internal Choice**: the student must answer **one out of two** alternatives. The two alternatives below are designed to cover both conceptual and computational mastery of Randomization and Blocking.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** **Explain the three fundamental principles of experimental design as proposed by R. A. Fisher. Discuss, with a suitable example, how randomization and blocking are used together in a Randomized Block Design.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Three Principles of Fisher (4 Marks):**

1. **Replication:** The repetition of an experiment under identical conditions. Replication provides:
   - An estimate of the **experimental error** $\sigma^2$.
   - Increased **precision** of treatment mean estimates (Standard Error decreases as $\sigma/\sqrt{r}$).
   - The ability to assess **variability among replicates**.

2. **Randomization:** The random allocation of treatments to experimental units. It:
   - Eliminates **bias** arising from systematic differences between units.
   - Distributes the effect of **uncontrolled nuisance variables** evenly across all treatments.
   - Justifies the assumption of **independence** of experimental errors, which is essential for the validity of the **F-test**.

3. **Blocking (Local Control):** Grouping homogeneous experimental units into blocks. It:
   - Removes the variability due to the blocking factor from the **error term**.
   - Increases the **relative efficiency** of the experiment.
   - Permits the experimenter to **isolate** the treatment effect from a known source of noise.

**Joint Application ŌĆö RBD Example (3 Marks):**

> A textile engineer wishes to compare **four types of fabric finishes (T1, T2, T3, T4)** for their **water repellency rating**.
>
> - **Replication:** Each finish is tested on **r = 5 fabric swatches** (5 replicates per finish per block).
> - **Blocking:** The experiment is conducted on **5 different days** (blocks), because ambient humidity changes daily. Swatches tested on the same day are more alike in their humidity exposure.
> - **Randomization:** Within each day (block), the **20 swatches** (4 finishes ├Ś 5 replicates) are **randomly assigned** to the testing stations and tested in a randomized order to remove position/time-trend effects.
>
> The resulting data are analyzed by **RBD ANOVA** with the model: $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}$, partitioning the total SS into Treatment, Block, and Error components.

---

**(b)** **The following data represent the breaking strength (in kg) of cloth woven using four different looms (treatments). Three operators (blocks) were used. Perform the RBD ANOVA and test whether the looms differ significantly at $\alpha = 0.05$.** **(7 Marks)**

| Operator (Block) | Loom 1 | Loom 2 | Loom 3 | Loom 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 50 | 47 | 46 | 49 |
| B | 52 | 50 | 48 | 51 |
| C | 45 | 44 | 42 | 46 |
| **Loom Total $T_i$** | **147** | **141** | **136** | **146** |
| **Block Total $B_j$** | **192** | **201** | **177** | ŌĆö |

**Grand Total $G = 570$**, $t = 4$ treatments, $b = 3$ blocks, $N = 12$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Correction Factor.**
$$CF = \frac{G^2}{tb} = \frac{570^2}{12} = \frac{324900}{12} = 27075$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö $SS_{Total}$.**

$\sum y_{ij}^2 = 50^2 + 47^2 + 46^2 + 49^2 + 52^2 + 50^2 + 48^2 + 51^2 + 45^2 + 44^2 + 42^2 + 46^2$
$= 2500 + 2209 + 2116 + 2401 + 2704 + 2500 + 2304 + 2601 + 2025 + 1936 + 1764 + 2116 = 27176$

$$SS_{Total} = 27176 - 27075 = 101$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö $SS_{Treatment}$ (Looms).**
$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{147^2 + 141^2 + 136^2 + 146^2}{3} - 27075 = \frac{21609 + 19881 + 18496 + 21316}{3} - 27075$$
$$= \frac{81302}{3} - 27075 = 27100.667 - 27075 = 25.667$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö $SS_{Block}$ (Operators).**
$$SS_{Bl} = \frac{192^2 + 201^2 + 177^2}{4} - 27075 = \frac{36864 + 40401 + 31329}{4} - 27075$$
$$= \frac{108594}{4} - 27075 = 27148.5 - 27075 = 73.5$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö $SS_{Error}$.**
$$SS_E = 101 - 25.667 - 73.5 = 1.833$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö ANOVA Table.**

| Source | SS | d.f. | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Looms (Treatments) | 25.667 | 3 | 8.556 | 28.04 |
| Operators (Blocks) | 73.5 | 2 | 36.75 | 120.49 |
| Error | 1.833 | 6 | 0.3055 | ŌĆö |
| **Total** | **101** | **11** | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |

**Step 7 ŌĆö F-test Decision.**

- Critical value: $F_{0.05, 3, 6} = 4.76$
- Calculated $F_0$ for Looms = $28.04 > 4.76$ ŌćÆ **Reject $H_0$** ŌćÆ Looms differ significantly in their breaking strength.
- The very large F-value for blocks (120.49) also confirms that **operator differences contributed substantially** to variability ŌĆö justifying the use of blocking in this experiment.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** **Distinguish between Completely Randomized Design (CRD) and Randomized Block Design (RBD). Under what conditions is RBD preferred over CRD? Illustrate with a layout diagram.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Distinguishing Features (5 Marks):**

| Feature | CRD | RBD |
|---|---|---|
| Number of factors | One (treatment) | Two (treatment + block) |
| Randomization | Complete randomization of all units to treatments | Randomization within each block |
| Nuisance variables | Assumed absent or negligible | Explicitly controlled by blocking |
| Error degrees of freedom | $t(r-1)$ | $(t-1)(b-1)$ (smaller) |
| Precision | Lower when nuisance variable exists | Higher when nuisance variable exists |
| Layout | Units are completely homogeneous | Units grouped into homogeneous blocks |
| Assumptions | Homogeneous experimental units | Units homogeneous **within** blocks; blocks may differ |

**When RBD is Preferred (1 Mark):**

RBD is preferred when there is a **known, controllable source of nuisance variation** (e.g., batches, days, operators, machines) that the experimenter can organize into blocks at the design stage.

**Layout Diagram (1 Mark):**

| | Block 1 | Block 2 | Block 3 | Block 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Treatment 1 | A1 | B1 | C1 | D1 |
| Treatment 2 | A2 | B2 | C2 | D2 |
| Treatment 3 | A3 | B3 | C3 | D3 |
| Treatment 4 | A4 | B4 | C4 | D4 |

(Each cell contains **one replicate**; treatments are randomly assigned within each block in the actual experiment.)

---

**(b)** **A pharmaceutical scientist compares three formulations (A, B, C) of an anti-hypertensive drug for their effect on systolic blood pressure reduction (mmHg). Since patients differ in age, she blocks the experiment by age group (Young, Middle, Old), with each patient receiving only one formulation. The data are:**

| Age Block | Formulation A | Formulation B | Formulation C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young | 12 | 15 | 14 |
| Middle | 10 | 13 | 12 |
| Old | 8 | 11 | 10 |

**Perform the RBD ANOVA. Test the hypothesis that the formulations differ at $\alpha = 0.05$. Also test whether blocking on age was effective.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the row/column totals and grand total.**

| Age Block | A | B | C | Block Total $B_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Young | 12 | 15 | 14 | 41 |
| Middle | 10 | 13 | 12 | 35 |
| Old | 8 | 11 | 10 | 29 |
| **Treatment Total $T_i$** | **30** | **39** | **36** | **G = 105** |

$t = 3$, $b = 3$, $N = 9$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Correction Factor.**
$$CF = \frac{G^2}{tb} = \frac{105^2}{9} = \frac{11025}{9} = 1225$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö $SS_{Total}$.**
$\sum y_{ij}^2 = 144 + 225 + 196 + 100 + 169 + 144 + 64 + 121 + 100 = 1263$
$$SS_{Total} = 1263 - 1225 = 38$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö $SS_{Treatment}$ (Formulations).**
$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{30^2 + 39^2 + 36^2}{3} - 1225 = \frac{900 + 1521 + 1296}{3} - 1225 = \frac{3717}{3} - 1225 = 1239 - 1225 = 14$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö $SS_{Block}$ (Age).**
$$SS_{Bl} = \frac{41^2 + 35^2 + 29^2}{3} - 1225 = \frac{1681 + 1225 + 841}{3} - 1225 = \frac{3747}{3} - 1225 = 1249 - 1225 = 24$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö $SS_{Error}$.**
$$SS_E = 38 - 14 - 24 = 0$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö ANOVA Table.**

| Source | SS | d.f. | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Formulations | 14 | 2 | 7.0 | $\infty$ (numerically) |
| Age (Blocks) | 24 | 2 | 12.0 | $\infty$ (numerically) |
| Error | 0 | 4 | 0 | ŌĆö |
| **Total** | **38** | **8** | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |

**Step 8 ŌĆö Interpretation.**

- $F_{0.05, 2, 4} = 6.94$. The calculated F for formulations is **extremely large** (denominator = 0 indicates the model fits perfectly). We **reject $H_0$** ŌĆö the three formulations differ significantly.
- The block F-value is also very large, confirming that **blocking on age was highly effective** ŌĆö patient age strongly influenced the response, and removing it from the error term made the comparison of formulations much sharper.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (RBD Problems)**:
> 1. **Forgetting the Constraint $\sum \tau_i = 0$** in the model statement: a -1 mark penalty is common.
> 2. **Placing $MS_E$ in the numerator of the F-ratio** (instead of $MS_{Treatment}$ or $MS_{Block}$): a -2 mark penalty ŌĆö this is a fundamental error.
> 3. **Skipping the test for block effectiveness**: the KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the block F-ratio. Marks are reserved for the conclusion "blocking was justified" or "blocking was not effective."
> 4. **Unit inconsistency in the final answer**: Always quote the response variable in the original units (e.g., mmHg, kg, ┬░C) and the F-ratios as dimensionless numbers.
> 5. **Confusing the d.f. for Error**: It is $(t-1)(b-1)$, NOT $tb - 1 - (t-1) - (b-1) = (t-1)(b-1)$ ŌĆö students sometimes write $tb - 3$ instead, leading to wrong critical F values.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Fisher's 3 Principles** = Replication + **Randomization** + **Blocking** (Local Control).
- **Randomization** removes **bias**; **Blocking** removes **noise**; together they maximize **precision** of treatment comparisons.
- **Linear Model ŌĆö CRD:** $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$, where $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ i.i.d. (justified by randomization).
- **Linear Model ŌĆö RBD:** $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}$, with constraints $\sum \tau_i = 0$ and $\sum \beta_j = 0$.
- **Fundamental ANOVA Identity (RBD):** $SS_{Total} = SS_{Treatment} + SS_{Block} + SS_{Error}$.
- **Degrees of Freedom (RBD):** $(tb - 1) = (t-1) + (b-1) + (t-1)(b-1)$.
- **Correction Factor (CF):** $CF = G^2 / (tb)$ ŌĆö always compute first, as it underpins all SS formulas.
- **F-ratios:** Always of the form $MS_{Factor} / MS_{Error}$ ŌĆö never reverse.
- **Block effectiveness test:** Reject $H_0$ for blocks ŌćÆ blocking was beneficial; do **not** block on a variable that does not show a significant F-ratio (over-blocking wastes d.f.).
- **Number of replicates per cell in RBD = 1** (one observation per treatment-block combination). If $r > 1$, the d.f. for Error becomes $tb(r-1)$ and there is a separate "interaction" test available.
- **Relative Efficiency (RE) of RBD over CRD:** $RE = \frac{MS_{E,CRD}}{MS_{E,RBD}} \cdot \frac{(b+1)(t+1)}{b \cdot t + 1}$. If $RE > 1$, blocking was worthwhile.
- **Assumption of RBD:** **No interaction** between treatment and block (i.e., the treatment effect is the same in every block). If interaction is suspected, use a **Latin Square Design** or a **Generalized Block Design** instead.
- **When NOT to block:** When the blocking factor is **not controllable** at the design stage, or when the number of blocks is too large relative to the number of treatments (waste of d.f.).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Guidelines for Designing the Experiments
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Guidelines for Designing the Experiments

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design of Experiments (DOE)** is a structured, statistical methodology for planning, conducting, analyzing, and interpreting controlled tests to evaluate the factors that influence the response variable of a process or system. The *guidelines for designing experiments* constitute a systematic framework that ensures experiments yield valid, reliable, and statistically significant conclusions while making efficient use of resources.

According to the classical Montgomery framework (adopted universally in engineering curricula), an experiment is a **test or series of tests** in which purposeful changes are made to the input variables (factors) of a process or system so that we may observe and identify the reasons for changes in the output response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The 2024 Scheme emphasizes that experimental design is *not* merely about collecting data ŌĆö it is about **collecting the right data in the right way** so that valid causal inferences can be drawn.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Formal Definition |
|------|-------------------|
| **Factor (Xi)** | An independent variable under the experimenter's control. |
| **Level** | A specific value or setting of a factor. |
| **Response (Y)** | The dependent (output) variable measured to evaluate the effect of factor changes. |
| **Treatment** | A specific combination of factor levels applied to an experimental unit. |
| **Experimental Unit** | The smallest entity to which a treatment is independently applied. |
| **Replication** | Repetition of an experiment to estimate the experimental error variance. |
| **Randomization** | The random allocation of treatments to experimental units. |
| **Blocking** | Grouping of experimental units that are similar to control nuisance variability. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Chef's Kitchen" Intuition

Imagine you are a chef trying to perfect a new chocolate cake recipe. You suspect three variables matter: **baking temperature**, **baking time**, and **cocoa content**. Without a guideline, you might bake a cake at 180┬░C for 30 min, taste it, then randomly change things. The result? You cannot tell whether the improvement came from the temperature change, the time change, or pure luck.

DOE guidelines instruct you to:

1. **Fix everything else** (oven brand, pan size, flour brand) ŌĆö these are *control variables*.
2. **Change factors systematically** ŌĆö bake at 160, 180, 200 ┬░C while holding time constant; then vary time, and so on.
3. **Repeat each run** ŌĆö bake two identical cakes per condition (replication).
4. **Randomize the order** ŌĆö don't bake all 180 ┬░C cakes first, otherwise oven fatigue contaminates results.
5. **Block by similarity** ŌĆö run all "morning" cakes in the morning batch (blocking).

Just as this recipe logic produces a robust cake, the guidelines of DOE produce robust engineering conclusions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Experimental design is the "scientific cooking method" of engineering ŌĆö controlling noise, varying inputs deliberately, and measuring outcomes with statistical discipline.

## 1.4 Why Are These Guidelines Necessary?

Without structured guidelines, experimenters face three classic pitfalls:

- **Confounding** ŌĆö the effect of one factor cannot be separated from another.
- **Bias** ŌĆö systematic errors from uncontrolled nuisance variables.
- **Inefficiency** ŌĆö too many runs for too little information, or vice versa.

The guidelines solve these by mandating replication, randomization, and blocking ŌĆö the **three foundational principles** of DOE.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (KTU 2024 ŌĆö OECHT727):**
> After studying this topic, students should be able to:
> - **CO1:** Recall and explain the terminology and principles of experimental design.
> - **CO2:** Apply the basic guidelines to plan a single-factor experiment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Step-by-Step Logical Framework of Experimental Design

A complete experimental design, as prescribed by KTU and standard DOE texts, follows **eight sequential stages**. Each stage has a clear deliverable and a clear *why*.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Recognition of the Problem and Statement of the Objective

Every experiment begins with a precise, quantitative objective statement.

- *Bad:* "I want to improve the product."
- *Good:* "I want to determine whether increasing temperature from 300 ┬░C to 350 ┬░C will reduce surface roughness (Ra) below **0.8 ┬Ąm**, while keeping production cost under Ōé╣50/unit."

The objective must be **measurable**, with a clearly defined response variable $Y$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Selection of the Response Variable

Choose $Y$ such that it:
- Is **objectively measurable** (quantitative, not subjective).
- Reflects the engineering objective directly.
- Has adequate precision (signal-to-noise ratio).

Examples: tensile strength (MPa), cycle time (seconds), yield (%).

### Step 3 ŌĆö Choice of Factors, Levels, and Ranges

This is the *heart* of design planning.

**Factors** are classified into:

| Factor Type | Definition | Example |
|-------------|------------|---------|
| **Design factor** | Deliberately varied to study its effect | Temperature |
| **Held-constant factor** | Held at a fixed level to reduce noise | Humidity |
| **Nuisance / Blocking factor** | Varied but accounted for via blocking | Shift (day/night) |
| **Noise factor** | Uncontrollable variability | Raw material lot |

The **range** of each factor must be wide enough to detect meaningful effects but narrow enough to remain operationally feasible.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners often ask, "Differentiate between control factors and nuisance factors." Memorize the table above verbatim.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Choice of Experimental Design

The selection rule:

| If your experiment hasŌĆ” | Recommended Design |
|------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 factor, several levels | **Completely Randomized Design (CRD)** |
| 1 factor + nuisance factor | **Randomized Block Design (RBD)** |
| 2 or more factors | **Factorial Design** ($2^k$ or $3^k$) |
| Hard-to-change factors | **Split-Plot / Repeated Measures** |
| Many factors, few runs desired | **Fractional Factorial ($2^{k-p}$)** |

### Step 5 ŌĆö Determination of Sample Size (Replication)

The number of replicates $n$ is set so that the test can detect a meaningful difference with high probability.

### Step 6 ŌĆö Randomization of the Experimental Runs

To eliminate bias from unknown or uncontrollable nuisance variables, the order of all experimental runs must be **randomized**.

### Step 7 ŌĆö Conducting the Experiment

Meticulous data collection, with all observed (planned and unplanned) effects recorded in a lab logbook.

### Step 8 ŌĆö Statistical Analysis and Conclusions

Use ANOVA, regression, or graphical tools to test hypotheses, estimate effects, and draw engineering conclusions.

## 2.2 The Three Foundational Principles (Hallmarks of a Good Experiment)

Every well-designed experiment must satisfy:

1. **Randomization** ŌĆö random allocation of treatments eliminates systematic bias.
2. **Replication** ŌĆö independent repetition enables estimation of experimental error $\sigma^2$ and increases precision.
3. **Blocking** ŌĆö when a nuisance variable is known, grouping similar units into blocks removes its confounding effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule (Montgomery):** "A good experimental design is one that yields the maximum amount of information with the minimum use of resources." The three principles are the engine that delivers this efficiency.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | When to Use |
|---|-------------------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$ | Linear model for CRD (single factor) | ANOVA for one-factor experiments |
| 2 | $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ i.i.d. | Model assumption | Validity check |
| 3 | $SS_T = SS_{\text{Treatments}} + SS_E$ | Total Sum of Squares decomposition | ANOVA table |
| 4 | $SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{n}(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$ | Total variation | Computing ANOVA |
| 5 | $SS_{\text{Treat}} = \sum_{i=1}^{a} n(\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$ | Between-treatment variation | Treatment effect test |
| 6 | $SS_E = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{n}(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i.})^2$ | Within-treatment (error) variation | Denominator of F |
| 7 | $MS_E = \dfrac{SS_E}{N - a}$ | Mean squared error | Estimating $\sigma^2$ |
| 8 | $MS_{\text{Treat}} = \dfrac{SS_{\text{Treat}}}{a - 1}$ | Mean square treatments | Numerator of F |
| 9 | $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_{\text{Treat}}}{MS_E}$ | Test statistic | Compare with $F_{\alpha, a-1, N-a}$ |
| 10 | $n = \dfrac{2(z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^2 \sigma^2}{\Delta^2}$ | Sample size (one-sided comparison) | Replication planning |
| 11 | $n = \dfrac{2(z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^2 \sigma^2}{\delta^2}$ | Sample size for two means | Two-treatment CRD |
| 12 | $\Phi^2 = \dfrac{n \Delta^2}{2a \sigma^2}$ | Non-centrality parameter | Operating characteristic curves |
| 13 | $\text{Power} = 1 - \beta$ | Probability of detecting true effect | Sample-size justification |
| 14 | $\text{Range} = Y_{\max} - Y_{\min}$ | Spread of data | Quick variability check |
| 15 | $Y = f(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_k) + \epsilon$ | General response model | Multi-factor designs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used universally in manufacturing (Six Sigma DMAIC), pharmaceuticals (clinical trials), agriculture (field trials), and software engineering (A/B testing). A mechanical engineer uses $F_0$ to compare mean tool wear across spindle speeds; a chemical engineer uses $n$ to plan the number of batches needed to validate a new catalyst.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Sample Size for a Two-Treatment Comparison (CRD)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module 1.

### Setup

Suppose we have two treatments (means $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$) and we want to detect a difference $\Delta = \mu_1 - \mu_2$ with:
- Significance level $\alpha$ (Type I error probability)
- Power $1 - \beta$ (where $\beta$ is Type II error probability)
- Known variance $\sigma^2$

We use a two-sided test:

$$
H_0 : \mu_1 = \mu_2 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1 : \mu_1 \neq \mu_2
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Standardize the test statistic.**

Under $H_0$, the difference $\bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2$ has expectation $0$ and variance $\dfrac{2\sigma^2}{n}$ (assuming equal sample sizes $n$ per treatment).

$$
Z = \dfrac{(\bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2) - 0}{\sqrt{2\sigma^2/n}}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Reject $H_0$ if $\vert Z \vert > z_{\alpha/2}$.**

So the rejection region is:

$$
\vert \bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2 \vert > z_{\alpha/2} \sqrt{\dfrac{2\sigma^2}{n}}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Type II error $\beta$ under the true alternative $\Delta \neq 0$.**

Under $H_1$, $\bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2 \sim N\left(\Delta, \dfrac{2\sigma^2}{n}\right)$.

Define the standardized shift:

$$
d = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{2\sigma^2/n}}
$$

$$
\beta = P\left(-z_{\alpha/2} < \dfrac{\bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2 - \Delta}{\sqrt{2\sigma^2/n}} < z_{\alpha/2} \;\middle|\; H_1\right)
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute $u = \bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2$.**

$$
\beta = P\left(\Delta - z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\sigma^2}{n}} < u < \Delta + z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\sigma^2}{n}}\right)
$$

Standardizing:

$$
\beta = \Phi\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{2\sigma^2/n}} - z_{\alpha/2}\right) - \Phi\left(\dfrac{-\Delta}{\sqrt{2\sigma^2/n}} - z_{\alpha/2}\right)
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Note that $\Phi(-x) = 1 - \Phi(x)$, and for large positive $\Delta$ the second term is negligible.**

$$
\beta \approx \Phi\left(z_{\alpha/2} - \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{2\sigma^2/n}}\right)
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö For given $\alpha$ and $\beta$, solve for $n$.**

$$
z_{\beta} = z_{\alpha/2} - \dfrac{\Delta \sqrt{n}}{\sqrt{2\sigma^2}}
$$

$$
\dfrac{\Delta \sqrt{n}}{\sqrt{2\sigma^2}} = z_{\alpha/2} - z_{\beta}
$$

$$
\sqrt{n} = \dfrac{(z_{\alpha/2} - z_{\beta})\sqrt{2\sigma^2}}{\Delta}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Square both sides to obtain the final sample-size formula.**

$$
\boxed{n = \dfrac{2(z_{\alpha/2} - z_{\beta})^2 \sigma^2}{\Delta^2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **one-sided test**, replace $z_{\alpha/2}$ with $z_{\alpha}$:
> $$n = \dfrac{2(z_{\alpha} + z_{\beta})^2 \sigma^2}{\Delta^2}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Sum-of-Squares Decomposition for CRD

We must show that $SS_T = SS_{\text{Treat}} + SS_E$.

**Total variation:**

$$
SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{n}(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2
$$

**Add and subtract $\bar{Y}_{i.}$ inside the square:**

$$
SS_T = \sum_{i}\sum_{j}\left[(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i.}) + (\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})\right]^2
$$

**Expand using $(A+B)^2 = A^2 + 2AB + B^2$:**

$$
SS_T = \sum_i \sum_j (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i.})^2 + 2\sum_i \sum_j (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i.})(\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..}) + \sum_i \sum_j (\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2
$$

**Examine the cross-term.** Since $\bar{Y}_{i.}$ is constant over $j$:

$$
\sum_j (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i.}) = n\bar{Y}_{i.} - n\bar{Y}_{i.} = 0
$$

Therefore the cross-term vanishes. We obtain:

$$
SS_T = \underbrace{\sum_i \sum_j (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i.})^2}_{SS_E} + \underbrace{n \sum_i (\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2}_{SS_{\text{Treat}}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** This derivation is the *mathematical foundation of ANOVA*. Examiners often ask: "Prove that the total sum of squares decomposes into treatment and error components." Memorize the "add-and-subtract the group mean" trick.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Sample Size Planning

**Problem.** A polymer engineer wants to compare two catalyst formulations for tensile strength. Pilot data give $\sigma = 5$ MPa. The smallest difference considered practically meaningful is $\Delta = 4$ MPa. Using $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-sided) and power $1 - \beta = 0.90$ (so $\beta = 0.10$):

Find the required number of replicates $n$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Look up the standard normal quantiles.**

For $\alpha = 0.05$ two-sided: $z_{\alpha/2} = z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

For $\beta = 0.10$: $z_{\beta} = z_{0.10} = 1.2816$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute into the formula.**

$$
n = \dfrac{2(z_{\alpha/2} - z_{\beta})^2 \sigma^2}{\Delta^2}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{2(1.96 - 1.2816)^2 (5)^2}{(4)^2}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{2(0.6784)^2 (25)}{16}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{2 \times 0.4602 \times 25}{16}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{23.012}{16} \approx 1.438
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Round up to the next integer.**

$$
\boxed{n = 2 \text{ replicates per treatment (total } N = 4\text{ runs)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Catch:** Statistical computation may give a fractional answer, but you must always **round up** (ceiling) the sample size. Also, accounting for potential dropouts, KTU examiners expect $n = 3$ in practice to ensure robustness.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö Choosing the Right Experimental Design

| Number of Factors | Homogeneous Experimental Units? | Nuisance Variable? | Recommended Design |
|------------------|--------------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Yes | None | **CRD** |
| 1 | Yes | One | **RBD** |
| Ōēź 2 | Yes | None | **Full Factorial $2^k$** |
| Ōēź 2 | Yes | Several | **Factorial in Blocks** |
| Ōēź 4 | Yes, runs are limited | None | **Fractional Factorial $2^{k-p}$** |
| Ōēź 2 | Hard-to-change factors | Mixed | **Split-Plot** |
| 1 | Units differ a lot | One | **Latin Square / Graeco-Latin** |
| Sequential optimization needed | Yes | Yes | **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram ŌĆö The Eight Sequential Steps of Experimental Design

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the complete KTU-prescribed procedure for designing any experiment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Recognize Problem and Define Objective] --> B[Step 2: Select Response Variable Y]
    B --> C[Step 3: Choose Factors, Levels, and Ranges]
    C --> D[Step 4: Select Experimental Design]
    D --> E[Step 5: Determine Number of Replications n]
    E --> F[Step 6: Randomize the Experimental Runs]
    F --> G[Step 7: Conduct the Experiment and Record Data]
    G --> H[Step 8: Statistical Analysis ANOVA and Conclusions]
    H --> I{Objective Achieved?}
    I -- No --> J[Refine Factors or Ranges] --> C
    I -- Yes --> K[Final Report and Recommendations]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö The Three Foundational Principles and Their Roles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FOUND["Three Foundational Principles of DOE"]
        R[Randomization]
        REP[Replication]
        BL[Blocking]
    end

    subgraph EFF["Effects Achieved"]
        E1[Eliminates Systematic Bias]
        E2[Estimates Experimental Error Variance sigma squared]
        E3[Removes Nuisance Variability]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Engineering Outcome"]
        VALID[Valid Causal Inference]
        EFFIC[Maximum Information per Rupee and per Hour]
        RELI[Reliable Reproducible Conclusions]
    end

    R --> E1 --> VALID
    REP --> E2 --> RELI
    BL --> E3 --> EFFIC
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram ŌĆö Mapping Variable Types in an Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT["Input Variables"]
        X1[Design Factor X1: Temperature]
        X2[Design Factor X2: Pressure]
        X3[Held Constant X3: Humidity]
        X4[Nuisance Factor X4: Shift]
    end

    PROCESS["PROCESS UNDER STUDY"]

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Variable"]
        Y[Response Y: Yield percent]
    end

    X1 --> PROCESS
    X2 --> PROCESS
    X3 --> PROCESS
    X4 --> PROCESS
    PROCESS --> Y
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree ŌĆö Choosing Between CRD, RBD, and Factorial

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: How many factors?] --> ONE{One factor only?}
    ONE -- Yes --> NOISY{Nuisance variable present?}
    NOISY -- No --> CRD[Use CRD]
    NOISY -- Yes --> RBD[Use RBD]
    ONE -- No --> TWO{Two or more factors?}
    TWO -- Yes --> FACT[Use Full Factorial]
    TWO -- No --> MANY{Many factors, few runs?}
    MANY -- Yes --> FF[Use Fractional Factorial]
    MANY -- No --> RS[Use Response Surface Methodology]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**
**List and briefly explain any three guidelines for designing an experiment.**

**Model Answer:**

The three most important guidelines for designing an experiment are:

1. **Randomization** ŌĆö The experimental runs must be performed in a random order. This ensures that the effect of any uncontrolled nuisance variable is averaged out and does not bias the estimated treatment effects.

2. **Replication** ŌĆö Each treatment combination must be repeated independently multiple times. Replication provides an estimate of the experimental error $\sigma^2$ and increases the precision of treatment-mean estimates.

3. **Blocking** ŌĆö When a nuisance variable is known and controllable (such as day, batch, or operator), grouping similar experimental units into blocks removes the variability due to that factor and reduces error variance.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a design factor, a nuisance factor, and a noise factor with one engineering example each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Design Factor:** A variable deliberately varied by the experimenter to study its effect. *Example:* Curing temperature in a rubber vulcanization study.

- **Nuisance Factor:** A variable that influences the response but is of no direct interest; controlled via blocking. *Example:* Machine operator in a CNC machining experiment.

- **Noise Factor:** A variable that is uncontrollable and contributes to response variability. *Example:* Ambient humidity during a coating process.

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the step-by-step procedure for designing a single-factor experiment, with reference to an industrial example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the formula for the number of replications $n$ required to detect a difference $\Delta$ between two treatment means with significance level $\alpha$ and power $1-\beta$.

### Question B ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Supplementary 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three basic principles of experimental design. State how each principle contributes to validity and efficiency.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a study comparing two rocket propellants, the standard deviation of burning rate is $\sigma = 3$ cm/s. The engineer wishes to detect a difference of $\Delta = 5$ cm/s with $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-sided) and power $0.90$. Determine the number of replicates per propellant.

---

## Complete Model Solutions

### Solution A(a) ŌĆö Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1 ŌĆö Statement of the problem.**  
A pharmaceutical company wishes to determine whether a new drug delivery method (Treatment B) produces a longer average pain-relief duration than the standard method (Treatment A). The response variable is *duration of relief* in hours.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Selection of response variable.**  
$Y$ = duration of relief (hours), measured by a calibrated stopwatch protocol.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Choice of factors, levels, and ranges.**  
- *Design factor:* Delivery method at two levels (A, B).  
- *Nuisance factor:* Patient age group ŌĆö blocked into "young" and "elderly."  
- *Held constant:* Dosage, room temperature, evaluator.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Choice of experimental design.**  
Randomized Block Design (RBD) with 2 treatments and 2 age blocks.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Determine replications.**  
Using the sample-size formula (see Solution A(b)) with $\sigma = 1.5$ h, $\Delta = 1$ h, $\alpha = 0.05$, power $= 0.90$: $n \approx 24$ patients per block.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Randomization.**  
Within each block, allocate patients to A or B using a random number table.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Conduct the experiment.**  
Record duration of relief for each patient.

**Step 8 ŌĆö Statistical analysis.**  
Compute ANOVA table, test $H_0 : \mu_A = \mu_B$ using the F-test at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Conclusion.** If $F_0 > F_{0.05, 1, 46}$, reject $H_0$ and conclude the new delivery method is significantly more effective.

---

### Solution A(b) ŌĆö Derivation of Sample-Size Formula

*(Full derivation already given in Section 3.1 above; reproduced here in compact form for completeness.)*

Starting from the test statistic:

$$
Z = \dfrac{\bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2}{\sqrt{2\sigma^2/n}}
$$

Under $H_0$, $|Z| > z_{\alpha/2}$ rejects $H_0$. Under $H_1$ with true difference $\Delta$, the Type II error is:

$$
\beta = \Phi\left(z_{\alpha/2} - \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{2\sigma^2/n}}\right) - \Phi\left(-z_{\alpha/2} - \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{2\sigma^2/n}}\right)
$$

For meaningful $\Delta$ the second term is negligible, giving:

$$
\beta \approx \Phi\left(z_{\alpha/2} - \dfrac{\Delta\sqrt{n}}{\sqrt{2\sigma^2}}\right)
$$

Solving for $n$:

$$
\boxed{n = \dfrac{2(z_{\alpha/2} - z_{\beta})^2 \sigma^2}{\Delta^2}}
$$

---

### Solution B(a) ŌĆö Three Principles of Experimental Design

**1. Randomization.**  
Random allocation of treatments to experimental units ensures that the effect of any uncontrolled or unknown nuisance variable is distributed evenly across all treatments. This eliminates systematic bias, so the observed differences in $Y$ can be attributed to the treatments themselves. Statistically, randomization validates the assumption that residuals $\epsilon_{ij}$ are independent.

**2. Replication.**  
Replication is the independent repetition of each treatment. It serves two purposes:  
- It provides an estimate of experimental error variance $\sigma^2$ (the denominator of the F-test).  
- It reduces the variance of treatment-mean estimates by a factor of $1/n$, increasing precision.  
Without replication, the experimenter cannot separate treatment effect from noise.

**3. Blocking.**  
Blocking groups experimental units that are *similar* with respect to a nuisance variable. Within each block, all treatments are applied. This isolates and removes the variation due to the nuisance factor from the error term, sharpening the test of treatment differences. Common blocking variables include day, batch, operator, or machine.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** **"R-R-B"** ŌåÆ Randomization, Replication, Blocking. Examiners often frame questions like, "Why is replication necessary in DOE?" ŌĆö answer in terms of *error estimation* and *precision improvement*.

---

### Solution B(b) ŌĆö Sample-Size Numerical Problem

**Given:** $\sigma = 3$ cm/s, $\Delta = 5$ cm/s, $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-sided), power $= 0.90$ so $\beta = 0.10$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Standard normal quantiles.**

From the Z-table:
- $z_{\alpha/2} = z_{0.025} = 1.96$
- $z_{\beta} = z_{0.10} = 1.2816$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute.**

$$
n = \dfrac{2(z_{\alpha/2} - z_{\beta})^2 \sigma^2}{\Delta^2}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{2(1.96 - 1.2816)^2 \times (3)^2}{(5)^2}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{2 \times (0.6784)^2 \times 9}{25}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{2 \times 0.4602 \times 9}{25} = \dfrac{8.2836}{25}
$$

$$
n \approx 0.331
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Round up to the next integer.**

$$
\boxed{n = 1 \text{ replicate per propellant}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Practical adjustment.**

Although statistically $n = 1$ suffices, the engineer should use at least $n = 5$ replicates per propellant to obtain a more reliable estimate of $\sigma$ and to guard against outliers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to *square* $z_{\alpha/2} - z_{\beta}$. Loss: 2 marks.
> 2. Students use $z_\alpha$ instead of $z_{\alpha/2}$ for a two-sided test. Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. Students forget to *round up* the sample size and report fractional answers. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. Students omit the practical recommendation for additional replicates due to dropout / outlier risk. Loss: 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **DOE = planning, conducting, and analyzing experiments** to draw valid causal conclusions about factor effects on a response.
- [ ] **Eight sequential steps** must be followed: objective ŌåÆ response ŌåÆ factors ŌåÆ design ŌåÆ replication ŌåÆ randomization ŌåÆ conduct ŌåÆ analysis.
- [ ] **Three foundational principles** are Randomization, Replication, and Blocking ŌĆö collectively called the *RRB* of experimental design.
- [ ] **Replication** is mandatory to estimate the experimental error variance $\sigma^2$.
- [ ] **Randomization** validates the i.i.d. assumption on residuals and removes bias from unknown nuisance sources.
- [ ] **Blocking** is used when a controllable nuisance variable is known and can be partitioned.
- [ ] **Sample-size formula (two-treatment CRD, two-sided test):**
$$
n = \dfrac{2(z_{\alpha/2} - z_{\beta})^2 \sigma^2}{\Delta^2}
$$
For one-sided test, replace $z_{\alpha/2}$ with $z_\alpha$ and use $+$ sign.
- [ ] **ANOVA decomposition:**
$$
SS_T = SS_{\text{Treatments}} + SS_E
$$
- [ ] **F-test statistic:**
$$
F_0 = \dfrac{MS_{\text{Treat}}}{MS_E} \sim F_{(a-1,\, N-a)}
$$
- [ ] **Decision rules for design choice:**
  - 1 factor, no nuisance ŌåÆ **CRD**
  - 1 factor, 1 nuisance ŌåÆ **RBD**
  - Ōēź 2 factors ŌåÆ **Factorial**
  - Hard-to-change factors ŌåÆ **Split-Plot**
- [ ] Always **round up** the sample size to the next integer.
- [ ] **Pilot studies** are essential for estimating $\sigma$ before planning the full experiment.
- [ ] The **response variable $Y$** must be measurable, quantitative, and directly tied to the engineering objective.
- [ ] The **operating characteristic curve** can be used as an alternative to the formula for sample-size determination when exact computation is cumbersome.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Statistical Concepts: Concept of Random Variable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Statistical Concepts: Concept of Random Variable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous framework of probability theory (which forms the bedrock of **Design of Experiments**), a **Random Variable (RV)** is formally defined as a **measurable function** that assigns a unique real number to every elementary outcome in a sample space of a random experiment.

Mathematically, if $S$ is the sample space of a random experiment and $\mathbb{R}$ is the set of real numbers, then a random variable $X$ is a function:

$$X: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

such that for every real number $x \in \mathbb{R}$, the set $\{s \in S : X(s) \leq x\}$ belongs to the $\sigma$-algebra (collection of measurable events) of $S$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Definition (Verbatim-Recommended):**
> *"A random variable is a real-valued function defined on the sample space of a random experiment. It is not 'variable' in the algebraic sense, but rather a function that assigns numerical values to each outcome of a probabilistic experiment."*

### 1.2 Classification of Random Variables

Random variables are classified into two principal categories:

**A. Discrete Random Variable**
A random variable $X$ is **discrete** if it can assume only a **finite** or **countably infinite** number of distinct values. The probability of each value is given by the **Probability Mass Function (PMF)**, denoted $P(X = x_i)$.

*Examples:* Number of defective items in a batch, count of customer arrivals per hour, roll of a fair die.

**B. Continuous Random Variable**
A random variable $X$ is **continuous** if it can assume **any value within one or more intervals** of the real line. The probability distribution is described by a **Probability Density Function (PDF)**, $f(x)$, such that:

$$P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx$$

with the necessary condition:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$$

*Examples:* Tensile strength of a steel rod, reaction time in a chemical process, height of students in a class.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Number Translator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Postal Sorting Machine**
>
> Imagine a postal sorting machine at a regional logistics hub. Each incoming parcel carries an unpredictable destination city (the *outcome* from the sample space), but the machine assigns each parcel a unique **tracking number** (the *real number*). The random variable is precisely this tracking-numbering rule. The parcels themselves are random; the **number they receive is deterministic once the parcel arrives**. Similarly, a coin toss outcome is random ("H" or "T"), but the random variable $X$ might map "H" $\rightarrow 1$ and "T" $\rightarrow 0$. The randomness lies in the experiment, **not** in the algebraic symbol $X$.

### 1.4 Real-World Significance in Design of Experiments

In **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, the response variable $Y$ (e.g., yield of a chemical reaction, fatigue life of a component, conversion efficiency of a catalyst) is **always modelled as a random variable** because it is influenced by uncontrollable noise factors. Understanding random variables allows the experimenter to:
- Quantify process variability
- Construct confidence intervals
- Validate statistical significance of treatment effects via ANOVA
- Establish tolerance bounds in Six-Sigma quality engineering

The statistical constants most often encountered are:

- **Mean / Expected Value:** $\mu = E(X)$, dimensionless or process-specific units.
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = Var(X)$, always in **(units)┬▓**.
- **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{Var(X)}$, same units as $X$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Architecture of a Random Variable

The complete statistical description of a random variable rests on **three foundational pillars**, each serving a distinct mathematical role:

**Pillar 1: Probability Mass Function (PMF) ŌĆö For Discrete RVs**
The PMF, $P(X = x_i)$ or $p(x_i)$, assigns a non-negative probability to each possible discrete outcome such that:

$$p(x_i) = P(X = x_i), \quad \forall i$$

with the two **axiomatic constraints**:

$$\text{(i)} \quad p(x_i) \geq 0 \quad \forall \, i$$

$$\text{(ii)} \quad \sum_{i} p(x_i) = 1$$

**Pillar 2: Probability Density Function (PDF) ŌĆö For Continuous RVs**
The PDF, $f(x)$, does **not** directly give probabilities (since $P(X = x) = 0$ for any single point in continuous space). Instead, probabilities are obtained over intervals:

$$P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx$$

with constraints:

$$f(x) \geq 0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \quad \text{and} \quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$$

**Pillar 3: Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) ŌĆö Universal**
The CDF, $F_X(x)$, unifies both discrete and continuous cases:

$$F_X(x) = P(X \leq x), \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$

Key properties of the CDF:

- $0 \leq F_X(x) \leq 1$
- $F_X(x)$ is a **non-decreasing** (monotonically increasing) function
- $\lim_{x \to -\infty} F_X(x) = 0$ and $\lim_{x \to +\infty} F_X(x) = 1$
- $P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$
- For continuous RV: $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$, hence $f(x) = \dfrac{dF_X(x)}{dx}$

### 2.2 Numerical Characteristics of Random Variables

**Expected Value (Mean):**

$$\mu = E(X) = \begin{cases} \sum_{i} x_i \, p(x_i) & \text{(Discrete)} \\ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f(x) \, dx & \text{(Continuous)} \end{cases}$$

**Variance:**

$$\sigma^2 = Var(X) = E[(X - \mu)^2] = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$$

$$Var(X) = \begin{cases} \sum_{i} (x_i - \mu)^2 \, p(x_i) & \text{(Discrete)} \\ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x - \mu)^2 \, f(x) \, dx & \text{(Continuous)} \end{cases}$$

**Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Discrete RV** | **Continuous RV** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Probability Statement | $P(X = x_i) = p(x_i)$ | $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx$ | Dimensionless |
| Normalization | $\sum_i p(x_i) = 1$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$ | Dimensionless |
| Mean $\mu$ | $\sum_i x_i \, p(x_i)$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f(x)\,dx$ | Units of $X$ |
| $E(X^2)$ | $\sum_i x_i^2 \, p(x_i)$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^2 \, f(x)\,dx$ | Units of $X^2$ |
| Variance $\sigma^2$ | $\sum_i (x_i - \mu)^2 \, p(x_i)$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x - \mu)^2 \, f(x)\,dx$ | Units of $X^2$ |
| CDF $F(x)$ | $\sum_{x_i \leq x} p(x_i)$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\,dt$ | Dimensionless |
| Median | $F(x_{med}) = 0.5$ | $F(x_{med}) = 0.5$ | Units of $X$ |
| Mode | Max $p(x_i)$ | Max $f(x)$ | Units of $X$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear Transformation Property (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
>
> If $Y = aX + b$, where $a, b$ are real constants, then:
>
> $$E(Y) = aE(X) + b$$
>
> $$Var(Y) = a^2 \, Var(X)$$
>
> $$\sigma_Y = \vert a \vert \, \sigma_X$$

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

In engineering practice, the random variable framework provides:
- **Reliability Engineering:** Modeling time-to-failure of mechanical components.
- **Quality Control:** Monitoring process parameters within control charts (X-bar, R-charts).
- **Signal Processing:** Characterizing noise distributions in communication channels.
- **Structural Engineering:** Quantifying load and resistance variability using LRFD (Load and Resistance Factor Design).
- **Six-Sigma Manufacturing:** Reducing process standard deviation to meet defect-rate targets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Expected Value of a Fair Six-Sided Die

**Problem Statement:** A fair die is rolled. Let $X$ denote the number that appears on the top face. Derive the expected value $E(X)$ and variance $Var(X)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Enumerate the Sample Space and PMF:**

The sample space is $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$. Since the die is fair, each outcome has equal probability:

$$p(x_i) = P(X = x_i) = \frac{1}{6}, \quad \forall \, i \in \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Verify the Normalization Condition:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{6} p(x_i) = \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{6}{6} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Discrete Expected Value Formula:**

$$\mu = E(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{6} x_i \, p(x_i)$$

$$E(X) = (1)\left(\frac{1}{6}\right) + (2)\left(\frac{1}{6}\right) + (3)\left(\frac{1}{6}\right) + (4)\left(\frac{1}{6}\right) + (5)\left(\frac{1}{6}\right) + (6)\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)$$

$$E(X) = \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6}{6} = \frac{21}{6} = 3.5$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $E(X^2)$ for the Variance Formula:**

$$E(X^2) = \sum_{i=1}^{6} x_i^2 \, p(x_i) = \frac{1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 + 4^2 + 5^2 + 6^2}{6}$$

$$E(X^2) = \frac{1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 36}{6} = \frac{91}{6} \approx 15.1667$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the Computational Variance Formula $Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$:**

$$Var(X) = \frac{91}{6} - (3.5)^2 = \frac{91}{6} - 12.25$$

$$Var(X) = \frac{91}{6} - \frac{49}{4} = \frac{182 - 147}{12} = \frac{35}{12} \approx 2.9167$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute the Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{Var(X)} = \sqrt{\frac{35}{12}} \approx 1.7078$$

**Final Result:** $\mu = 3.5$, $\sigma^2 = 35/12$, $\sigma \approx 1.7078$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Property $E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b$

**Theorem Statement:** For any random variable $X$ and real constants $a, b$, the expected value of the linear transformation $Y = aX + b$ equals $aE(X) + b$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express the Transformation:**

Let $Y = aX + b$. For discrete $X$ with PMF $p(x_i)$:

$$E(Y) = E(aX + b) = \sum_{i} (a x_i + b) \, p(x_i)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Distribute the Summation Using Linearity:**

$$E(Y) = \sum_{i} (a x_i \, p(x_i) + b \, p(x_i))$$

$$E(Y) = a \sum_{i} x_i \, p(x_i) + b \sum_{i} p(x_i)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Definition of Expected Value and Normalization:**

The first summation is $E(X)$ by definition; the second summation equals **1** by the normalization axiom:

$$E(Y) = a \, E(X) + b(1) = aE(X) + b$$

$$\boxed{E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Adding a constant $b$ to every measurement shifts the mean by $b$ but does **not** change its variability. Multiplying by $a$ scales both the mean and variability.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Property $Var(aX + b) = a^2 Var(X)$

**Theorem Statement:** The variance is invariant under translation and scales quadratically under linear scaling.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start from the Definitional Form:**

$$Var(aX + b) = E\left[(aX + b - E(aX+b))^2\right]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute $E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b$:**

$$Var(aX + b) = E\left[(aX + b - aE(X) - b)^2\right]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify the Inner Expression:**

$$aX + b - aE(X) - b = aX - aE(X) = a(X - E(X))$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Square the Simplified Expression:**

$$Var(aX + b) = E\left[a^2 (X - E(X))^2\right] = a^2 \, E\left[(X - E(X))^2\right]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Recognize the Original Variance:**

$$E\left[(X - E(X))^2\right] = Var(X)$$

$$\boxed{Var(aX + b) = a^2 \, Var(X)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Critical Insight for KTU:** The constant $b$ **vanishes** because variance measures spread about the mean, and translation does not alter spread. Note that $\sigma_{aX+b} = \vert a \vert \, \sigma_X$ (the absolute value appears since standard deviation must be non-negative).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: CDF from PDF for a Continuous Uniform RV

**Problem:** Let $X \sim U(a, b)$ have PDF $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{b-a}$ for $a \leq x \leq b$. Derive the CDF $F(x)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the Definition for $x < a$:**

For $x < a$, no probability mass has accumulated:

$$F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\,dt = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Definition for $a \leq x \leq b$:**

$$F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{a} 0\,dt + \int_{a}^{x} \frac{1}{b-a}\,dt = \frac{x - a}{b - a}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Definition for $x > b$:**

$$F(x) = \int_{a}^{b} \frac{1}{b-a}\,dt = \frac{b - a}{b - a} = 1$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Combine into the Piecewise CDF:**

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < a \\ \dfrac{x - a}{b - a}, & a \leq x \leq b \\ 1, & x > b \end{cases}$$

**Verification:** $F(a) = 0$, $F(b) = 1$, monotonically non-decreasing, and $\dfrac{dF}{dx} = \dfrac{1}{b-a} = f(x)$ in the interior. $\checkmark$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Discrete vs Continuous Quick Reference

| **Feature** | **Discrete RV** | **Continuous RV** |
|---|---|---|
| Possible Values | Countable set $\{x_1, x_2, \dots\}$ | Uncountable (intervals) |
| Defining Function | PMF $p(x_i)$ | PDF $f(x)$ |
| $P(X = x)$ | May be $> 0$ | Always $= 0$ |
| CDF Behaviour | Jump discontinuities at $x_i$ | Continuous, differentiable almost everywhere |
| Mean Formula | $\sum x_i \, p(x_i)$ | $\int x \, f(x)\,dx$ |
| Variance Formula | $\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 p(x_i)$ | $\int (x-\mu)^2 f(x)\,dx$ |
| Example | Binomial $B(n, p)$, Poisson $P(\lambda)$ | Normal $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, Exponential $E(\lambda)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Mapping from Sample Space to Random Variable

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a random variable $X$ acts as a **measurement rule** that maps each outcome of a probabilistic experiment (sample space $S$) to a numerical value in $\mathbb{R}$, and how the resulting RV is then characterized by PMF/PDF and CDF.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["EXPERIMENT: Rolling a Die Twice"] --> B["SAMPLE SPACE S: 36 Ordered Pairs"]
    B --> C["RANDOM VARIABLE X: Sum of Two Faces"]
    C --> D["RANGE OF X: 2 to 12"]
    D --> E["PROBABILITY FUNCTION"]
    E --> F1["PMF p(x) for Discrete"]
    E --> F2["PDF f(x) for Continuous"]
    F1 --> G1["Cumulative Sum F x"]
    F2 --> G2["Integral of PDF F x"]
    G1 --> H["NUMERICAL CHARACTERISTICS"]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I["Mean Mu"]
    H --> J["Variance Sigma Squared"]
    H --> K["Std Deviation Sigma"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow ŌĆö Descriptive Pipeline of Random Variable Analysis

The diagram below presents a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** depicting the analytical pipeline used by statisticians and DOE practitioners when characterizing a random variable.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input["INPUT: Random Experiment with Uncertain Outcomes"] --> stepA["Step A: Define Sample Space S"]
    stepA --> stepB["Step B: Construct Random Variable X Mapping S to Real Numbers"]
    stepB --> stepC{"Step C: Classify RV Type"}
    stepC -- Discrete --> stepD["Step D1: Derive PMF p of x via Counting or Enumeration"]
    stepC -- Continuous --> stepE["Step D2: Derive PDF f of x via Differentiation of CDF or Limit Definition"]
    stepD --> stepF["Step E: Build CDF by Summation"]
    stepE --> stepG["Step E: Build CDF by Integration"]
    stepF --> stepH["Step F: Compute Mean Variance Std Deviation"]
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI["Step G: Apply to DOE for Hypothesis Testing ANOVA Control Charts"]
    stepI --> output["OUTPUT: Statistical Inferences about Process Behaviour"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic ŌĆö CDF Properties Visualization (Block-Level)

The following schematic captures the **structural properties of a CDF** as a functional block, useful when interpreting cumulative probability in KTU numerical problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["Property 1: F minus infinity equals 0"] --> p2["Property 2: F is Non Decreasing"]
    p2 --> p3["Property 3: F positive infinity equals 1"]
    p3 --> p4["Property 4: P a less than X less than or equal b equals F of b minus F of a"]
    p4 --> p5["Property 5: Right Continuous"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Diagrams:**
> - **Section 4.1** demonstrates that a random variable is a *bridge* between abstract outcomes and concrete numerical analysis.
> - **Section 4.2** models the actual analytical workflow used in a Design of Experiments study.
> - **Section 4.3** captures the *axiomatic skeleton* of the CDF that examiners frequently test via short-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**
Define a random variable. Distinguish clearly between a discrete random variable and a continuous random variable, giving one engineering example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks ŌĆö Board Key Pattern):**

A **random variable** $X$ is a real-valued function defined on the sample space $S$ of a random experiment. It assigns a unique real number $X(s)$ to each elementary outcome $s \in S$.

| Aspect | Discrete RV | Continuous RV |
|---|---|---|
| Value Set | Finite or countably infinite | Uncountable (intervals) |
| Probability Function | PMF $p(x_i) = P(X = x_i)$ | PDF $f(x)$ with $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int f(x)\,dx$ |
| Engineering Example | Number of defective bolts in a batch of 50 | Tensile strength (in MPa) of a steel specimen |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinction table, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**
State any **six properties** of the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) $F_X(x)$ of a random variable $X$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks ŌĆö Board Key Pattern):**

1. $0 \leq F_X(x) \leq 1$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.
2. $F_X(x)$ is a **non-decreasing** function of $x$.
3. $\lim_{x \to -\infty} F_X(x) = 0$.
4. $\lim_{x \to +\infty} F_X(x) = 1$.
5. $F_X(x)$ is **right-continuous** at every point.
6. $P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$.
7. For continuous RV: $f(x) = \dfrac{dF_X(x)}{dx}$.
8. For discrete RV: $F_X(x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} p(x_i)$ (a step function).

*(Any six properties ŌĆö ┬Į mark each.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Every Part B question in the End Semester Examination (ESE) provides an **internal choice**. Two completely independent alternatives are presented below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Define a random variable. For a discrete random variable $X$ with PMF $p(x)$, derive the expressions for the **mean** and **variance** in terms of the PMF. State the axioms that the PMF must satisfy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fair coin is tossed **three** times. Let $X$ denote the number of heads obtained. **(i)** Construct the probability distribution of $X$. **(ii)** Compute $E(X)$ and $Var(X)$. **(iii)** Find $P(X \geq 2)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A random variable $X$ is a real-valued function $X: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined on the sample space $S$ of a random experiment such that for every real $x$, the set $\{s \in S : X(s) \leq x\}$ is an event.

**PMF Axioms (2 marks):**
$$p(x_i) \geq 0 \quad \forall \, i \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i} p(x_i) = 1$$

**Mean Derivation (1.5 marks):**
$$\mu = E(X) = \sum_{i} x_i \, p(x_i)$$

This is a weighted average of all possible values, weighted by their probabilities.

**Variance Derivation (1.5 marks):**
$$Var(X) = E[(X - \mu)^2] = \sum_{i} (x_i - \mu)^2 \, p(x_i)$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**(i) Probability Distribution (3 marks):**

The sample space has $2^3 = 8$ equally likely outcomes. $X$ can take values $0, 1, 2, 3$.

Using the Binomial $B(3, 0.5)$ formula $P(X = k) = \binom{3}{k} (0.5)^3$:

| $X = x$ | Outcomes | $P(X = x)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | TTT | $1/8$ |
| 1 | HTT, THT, TTH | $3/8$ |
| 2 | HHT, HTH, THH | $3/8$ |
| 3 | HHH | $1/8$ |

Verification: $\frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{1}{8} = \frac{8}{8} = 1 \checkmark$

**(ii) Mean and Variance (3 marks):**

$$E(X) = 0 \cdot \frac{1}{8} + 1 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 3 \cdot \frac{1}{8} = \frac{0 + 3 + 6 + 3}{8} = \frac{12}{8} = 1.5$$

$$E(X^2) = 0 \cdot \frac{1}{8} + 1 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 4 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 9 \cdot \frac{1}{8} = \frac{0 + 3 + 12 + 9}{8} = \frac{24}{8} = 3$$

$$Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = 3 - (1.5)^2 = 3 - 2.25 = 0.75$$

**(iii) Probability $P(X \geq 2)$ (1 mark):**

$$P(X \geq 2) = P(X = 2) + P(X = 3) = \frac{3}{8} + \frac{1}{8} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Define the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of a random variable $X$. List **six** properties of the CDF. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A continuous random variable $X$ has the PDF:

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} kx, & 0 \leq x \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Find: **(i)** The value of the constant $k$. **(ii)** The CDF $F(x)$ for all $x$. **(iii)** The mean $\mu = E(X)$. **(iv)** $P(1 \leq X \leq 1.5)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The CDF of a random variable $X$ is defined as:

$$F_X(x) = P(X \leq x), \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$

**Six Properties (1 mark each = 6 marks):**

1. $0 \leq F_X(x) \leq 1$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.
2. $F_X(x)$ is a **monotonically non-decreasing** function.
3. $\lim_{x \to -\infty} F_X(x) = 0$.
4. $\lim_{x \to +\infty} F_X(x) = 1$.
5. $F_X(x)$ is **right-continuous** for all $x$.
6. $P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$.

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**(i) Find $k$ (2 marks):**

Apply the normalization condition $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$:

$$\int_{0}^{2} kx \, dx = 1$$

$$k \left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{2} = 1$$

$$k \cdot \frac{4}{2} = 1 \implies 2k = 1 \implies k = 0.5$$

**(ii) Construct the CDF (2 marks):**

For $x < 0$: $\quad F(x) = 0$

For $0 \leq x \leq 2$:

$$F(x) = \int_{0}^{x} 0.5 \, t \, dt = 0.5 \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} = 0.25 x^2$$

For $x > 2$: $\quad F(x) = 1$

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ 0.25 x^2, & 0 \leq x \leq 2 \\ 1, & x > 2 \end{cases}$$

**(iii) Compute the Mean (2 marks):**

$$\mu = E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f(x) \, dx = \int_{0}^{2} x \cdot 0.5x \, dx = 0.5 \int_{0}^{2} x^2 \, dx$$

$$\mu = 0.5 \left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{0}^{2} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{8}{3} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.3333$$

**(iv) Compute the Probability (1 mark):**

$$P(1 \leq X \leq 1.5) = F(1.5) - F(1) = 0.25(1.5)^2 - 0.25(1)^2$$

$$P(1 \leq X \leq 1.5) = 0.25 \cdot 2.25 - 0.25 \cdot 1 = 0.5625 - 0.25 = 0.3125$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PMF with PDF:** Students often write $f(x) = P(X = x)$ for continuous RVs. This is mathematically **wrong** ŌĆö for continuous RVs, $P(X = x) = 0$ always; the PDF only yields probabilities via integration.
> 2. **Forgetting the Normalization Constraint:** In Q.B(b)(i), if a student writes "By inspection $k = 1$" without integrating, full credit is denied. Always **derive** the constant using the unity area condition.
> 3. **Sign Error in CDF Inequality:** When computing $P(a \leq X \leq b)$, students sometimes write $F(a) - F(b)$. The correct order is **always** $F(b) - F(a)$ since $F$ is non-decreasing.
> 4. **Linear Transformation Mistake:** When asked for variance of $aX + b$, students frequently write $a \cdot Var(X) + b$ instead of $a^2 \cdot Var(X)$. The constant $b$ does **not** contribute to variance.
> 5. **Squaring Without Square Bracket:** When using $Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$, the square **must** be applied to the bracket ŌĆö not just to $E(X)$ alone.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Concept of Random Variable**
>
> - A **random variable** is a *function* (not a variable in the algebraic sense) mapping each outcome of a random experiment to a real number.
> - **Discrete RV** $\Rightarrow$ **PMF** $p(x_i)$; valid values: $p(x_i) \geq 0$ and $\sum p(x_i) = 1$.
> - **Continuous RV** $\Rightarrow$ **PDF** $f(x)$; valid conditions: $f(x) \geq 0$ and $\int f(x)\,dx = 1$.
> - **CDF** $F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$ ŌĆö defined for **both** discrete and continuous RVs.
> - For continuous RVs: $f(x) = \dfrac{dF_X(x)}{dx}$ and $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\,dt$.
