> - **Mean**: $E(X) = \sum x_i p(x_i)$ (discrete) or $E(X) = \int x f(x)\,dx$ (continuous).
> - **Variance**: $Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$ (use this computational shortcut ŌĆö saves time in exams).
> - **Standard Deviation**: $\sigma = \sqrt{Var(X)}$.
> - **Linear Transformation**: $E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b$; $\quad Var(aX + b) = a^2 Var(X)$.
> - **Probability from CDF**: $P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$.
> - **Median** $M$: satisfies $F(M) = 0.5$; **Mode**: value where PMF/PDF is maximum.
> - In **DOE**, the response variable is always modelled as a random variable to account for noise factors.
> - **Memory Tip:** Variance measures *spread* (translation-invariant); Mean measures *location* (translation-sensitive).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Probability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Probability

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probability** is the mathematical measure of the *likelihood* of occurrence of a random experiment's outcome. It is a real-valued function $P : \mathcal{F} \to \mathbb{R}$ defined on a $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{F}$ of events of a sample space $\Omega$, satisfying the **Kolmogorov Axioms** (1933), which serve as the axiomatic foundation for the modern theory of probability.

In the context of **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, probability is the backbone that allows us to:
- Quantify the **chance of error** in experimental conclusions.
- Model the **random variation** inherent in all observed data.
- Construct **confidence intervals** and perform **hypothesis tests** on treatment effects.

### 1.2 Key Terminology ŌĆö A Glossary for B.Tech Students

| Term | Symbol | Meaning | Engineering Example |
|------|--------|---------|---------------------|
| **Random Experiment** | ŌĆö | A repeatable procedure with uncertain outcomes | Tensile test on a steel specimen |
| **Sample Space** | $\Omega$ | The set of ALL possible outcomes | $\Omega = \{$Failure, Success$\}$ |
| **Event** | $A, B, \ldots$ | A subset of the sample space | $A = $ "Strength $>$ 400 MPa" |
| **Sure Event** | $\Omega$ | The event that always occurs | Sample itself |
| **Impossible Event** | $\emptyset$ | The event that never occurs | Diameter $<$ 0 |
| **Mutually Exclusive** | $A \cap B = \emptyset$ | Two events that cannot occur together | Pass and Fail simultaneously |
| **Exhaustive** | $A \cup B = \Omega$ | Events that cover all possibilities | $A, A^{c}$ |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö "The Weather Forecast":**
> Imagine a meteorologist saying *"There is a 70% chance of rain tomorrow."* They are not saying it WILL rain 70% of the time ŌĆö they are saying that **out of 100 similar atmospheric conditions in the past, it rained on approximately 70 of those days.**
>
> In the same way, when a DOE analyst states that "*the probability of a Type-I error is $\alpha = 0.05$*", they mean that **if the experiment were repeated 100 times under identical conditions, the analyst would falsely reject the true null hypothesis about 5 times.** Probability, therefore, is a *long-run frequency* of occurrence, anchored in repeated trials.

### 1.4 Classical vs. Frequency vs. Subjective Definitions

The **KTU 2024 syllabus** recognises three complementary interpretations of probability:

1. **Classical (a priori) Definition** ŌĆö Applicable when all outcomes are equally likely:
$$P(A) = \frac{n(A)}{n(\Omega)} = \frac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Total number of outcomes}}$$

2. **Frequency (Empirical / Statistical) Definition** ŌĆö Based on observed relative frequencies:
$$P(A) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n(A)}{n}$$
where $n(A)$ is the number of times event $A$ occurs in $n$ trials. This is the **most relevant definition for DOE**, since experimental data is empirical.

3. **Subjective (Bayesian) Definition** ŌĆö A personal degree of belief. Used in **Bayesian DOE** approaches.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Kolmogorov Axioms (The Three Pillars)

Every legitimate probability assignment **must** satisfy these three axioms:

**Axiom 1 ŌĆö Non-Negativity:**
$$P(A) \geq 0 \quad \forall \, A \in \mathcal{F}$$

**Axiom 2 ŌĆö Normalisation (Certainty of the Whole):**
$$P(\Omega) = 1$$

**Axiom 3 ŌĆö Countable Additivity (For Mutually Exclusive Events $A_1, A_2, \ldots$):**
$$P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in DOE:** When you run a factorial experiment, the **total probability** of all possible outcomes (effect exists, no effect exists, error in measurement, etc.) must sum to **exactly 1**. Any statistical test you apply (ANOVA, t-test, F-test) implicitly assumes these axioms hold.

### 2.2 Core Theorems Derived from the Axioms

| # | Theorem | Mathematical Statement | Practical Use in DOE |
|---|---------|-----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Complement Rule | $P(A^{c}) = 1 - P(A)$ | Computing "fail-to-reject" probabilities |
| 2 | Monotonicity | If $A \subseteq B$, then $P(A) \leq P(B)$ | Comparing event likelihoods |
| 3 | Addition Rule (General) | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ | Probability of "either-or" outcomes |
| 4 | Addition (Mutually Exclusive) | If $A \cap B = \emptyset$, then $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ | Partitioning outcome space |
| 5 | Total Probability | $P(A) = \sum_{i} P(A \vert B_i) P(B_i)$ | Combining information from partitions |
| 6 | **Bayes' Theorem** | $P(B_j \vert A) = \dfrac{P(A \vert B_j) P(B_j)}{\sum_i P(A \vert B_i) P(B_i)}$ | Updating beliefs given new evidence |

### 2.3 Conditional Probability and Independence

**Conditional Probability** ŌĆö The probability of event $A$ given that $B$ has occurred:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad P(B) > 0$$

**Statistical Independence** ŌĆö Two events are independent if and only if:
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$
Equivalently, $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$ ŌĆö knowing $B$ provides *no information* about $A$.

**Multiplication Rule (General):**
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \mid \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula | Boundary / Range | Used In DOE Context |
|---------|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| Probability of event | $0 \leq P(A) \leq 1$ | Always bounded by **0 and 1** | All hypothesis testing |
| Conditional prob. | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ | $P(B) > 0$ | Sequential experimentation |
| Bayes' Theorem | $P(H \vert E) = \dfrac{P(E \vert H) P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Posterior $\propto$ Likelihood $\times$ Prior | Bayesian DOE |
| Total Probability | $P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \vert B_i) P(B_i)$ | $\{B_i\}$ partitions $\Omega$ | Stratified sampling |
| Independence | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$ | Holds iff $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$ | Randomisation in design |
| Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^{2}] - \mu^{2}$ | $\text{Var}(X) \geq 0$ | ANOVA, error estimation |
| Expected Value | $E[X] = \sum x_i P(x_i)$ | Real-valued | Treatment mean estimation |
| Std. Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}$ | $\sigma \geq 0$ | Control chart limits |

### 2.5 Distributions Most Relevant to DOE

> [!NOTE]
> DOE analysis relies on three families of **sampling distributions** that follow from the Central Limit Theorem. You are expected to recognise when each applies:

| Distribution | Symbol | Typical Application in DOE |
|--------------|--------|----------------------------|
| **Normal Distribution** | $N(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ | Underlying assumption for measurement errors; CLT for large $n$ |
| **Student's t-Distribution** | $t_{\nu}$ | Comparing two treatment means (small sample, unknown $\sigma$) |
| **F-Distribution** | $F_{\nu_1, \nu_2}$ | ANOVA ŌĆö comparing multiple treatment variances simultaneously |
| **Chi-Square** | $\chi^{2}_{\nu}$ | Goodness-of-fit, test of independence in contingency tables |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bayes' Theorem from First Principles

**Starting Point:** The definition of conditional probability applied to two events $A$ and $B$:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}
$$

Rearranging gives the **Multiplication Rule**:

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)
$$

Because intersection is **commutative** ($A \cap B = B \cap A$), we can also write:

$$
P(B \cap A) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

Since the two left-hand sides are equal, the right-hand sides are equal:

$$
P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

Dividing both sides by $P(B)$ (provided $P(B) > 0$):

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

This is **Bayes' Theorem for two events**. To generalise to a partition $\{B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\}$ of $\Omega$, we substitute $P(B)$ using the **Law of Total Probability**:

$$
P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \, P(A_i)
$$

Substituting yields the **Generalised Bayes' Theorem**:

$$
\boxed{P(A_j \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_j) \, P(A_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \, P(A_i)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **DOE Interpretation:** $P(A_j)$ is the **prior** probability (belief before experiment), $P(B \mid A_j)$ is the **likelihood** (data observed), and $P(A_j \mid B)$ is the **posterior** (updated belief after experiment). This is the engine of **Bayesian Optimal Design**.

### 3.2 Derivation: The Addition Rule for Two Events

Start from the partition of the union into disjoint regions:
$$A \cup B = (A \cap B^{c}) \cup (A \cap B) \cup (A^{c} \cap B)$$

The three pieces are mutually exclusive, so by Axiom 3:
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A \cap B^{c}) + P(A \cap B) + P(A^{c} \cap B)$$

Now observe:
$$A = (A \cap B^{c}) \cup (A \cap B) \implies P(A) = P(A \cap B^{c}) + P(A \cap B)$$
$$B = (A^{c} \cap B) \cup (A \cap B) \implies P(B) = P(A^{c} \cap B) + P(A \cap B)$$

Adding the last two equations:
$$P(A) + P(B) = P(A \cap B^{c}) + 2 P(A \cap B) + P(A^{c} \cap B)$$

But we know $P(A \cup B) = P(A \cap B^{c}) + P(A \cap B) + P(A^{c} \cap B)$, so:
$$P(A) + P(B) = P(A \cup B) + P(A \cap B)$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)}
$$

**Sanity check:** If $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive, $P(A \cap B) = 0$, and the formula reduces to Axiom 3's pairwise form. Ō£ō

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Exam Pattern)

#### **Example 1 ŌĆö Conditional Probability in a Quality Control DOE**

> A factory has two machines, $M_1$ and $M_2$, producing bolts. $M_1$ produces **60%** of the output with a defect rate of **2%**, while $M_2$ produces **40%** with a defect rate of **3%**.
>
> **(a)** Find the probability that a randomly selected bolt is defective.
> **(b)** Given that a bolt is defective, find the probability it came from $M_1$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Total Probability:**

Define:
- $D$: bolt is defective
- $M_1$: from machine 1, with $P(M_1) = 0.60$
- $M_2$: from machine 2, with $P(M_2) = 0.40$
- $P(D \mid M_1) = 0.02$
- $P(D \mid M_2) = 0.03$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(D) &= P(D \mid M_1) \cdot P(M_1) + P(D \mid M_2) \cdot P(M_2) \\
&= (0.02)(0.60) + (0.03)(0.40) \\
&= 0.012 + 0.012 \\
&= \mathbf{0.024} \;\; \text{or} \;\; \mathbf{2.4\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Bayes' Theorem:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(M_1 \mid D) &= \frac{P(D \mid M_1) \cdot P(M_1)}{P(D)} \\
&= \frac{(0.02)(0.60)}{0.024} \\
&= \frac{0.012}{0.024} \\
&= \mathbf{0.50} \;\; \text{or} \;\; \mathbf{50\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Even though $M_1$ produces 60% of bolts and has a *lower* defect rate than $M_2$, the posterior probability that a defective bolt came from $M_1$ is exactly 50% ŌĆö because the absolute number of defects from each machine is the same ($0.012$).

#### **Example 2 ŌĆö Independence and Multiplication**

> An experiment has three independent components $C_1, C_2, C_3$ with reliabilities $0.95, 0.90, 0.85$ respectively. Find the probability that the **system fails** if all three are connected in **series**.

**Solution:**

For **series connection**, the system works only if ALL components work. By independence:

$$
P(\text{System Works}) = P(C_1) \cdot P(C_2) \cdot P(C_3) = (0.95)(0.90)(0.85)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{System Works}) &= 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85 \\
&= 0.855 \times 0.85 \\
&= 0.72675
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(\text{System Fails}) = 1 - 0.72675 = \mathbf{0.27325} \;\; \text{or} \;\; \mathbf{27.325\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DOE Connection:** This is precisely the logic behind **system reliability analysis** when you design experiments involving multiple process steps. Each step introduces its own failure probability, and the *overall* probability compounds multiplicatively ŌĆö motivating the use of **factorial designs** to identify which step contributes most variance.

#### **Example 3 ŌĆö Counting Sample Points (Classical Probability)**

> Three treatment levels are applied to 6 randomly assigned experimental units. How many distinct ordered arrangements are possible?

**Solution:** This is a permutation problem.

$$
P(n, r) = \frac{n!}{(n - r)!} = \frac{6!}{(6 - 3)!} = \frac{720}{6} = \mathbf{120}
$$

If order does **not** matter (combinatorial arrangement), it would be:

$$
C(n, r) = \binom{6}{3} = \frac{6!}{3! \cdot 3!} = \frac{720}{36} = \mathbf{20}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the three axioms of probability. Why are they essential in Design of Experiments?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three Kolmogorov axioms of probability are:

1. **Non-negativity:** $P(A) \geq 0$ for every event $A$.
2. **Normalisation:** $P(\Omega) = 1$, where $\Omega$ is the sample space.
3. **Countable additivity:** For mutually exclusive events $A_1, A_2, \ldots$, 
$$P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$$

In DOE, these axioms guarantee that the **significance level $\alpha$** (e.g., 0.05) is a mathematically valid probability, ensuring the Type-I error rate is bounded and that hypothesis-testing procedures (ANOVA, t-test, F-test) yield *reliable* conclusions. **(1 mark for each axiom + 0 mark deduction reserved for missing the DOE link)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Distinguish between mutually exclusive events and independent events with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mutually Exclusive | Independent |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|
| Definition | $A \cap B = \emptyset$ | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ |
| Meaning | Cannot occur together | Occurrence of one doesn't affect the other |
| Example | Coin shows Head AND Tail simultaneously | Tossing two different coins |

**Mutually Exclusive Example:** In an experiment with two treatments $T_1$ and $T_2$, the event "treatment $T_1$ gives the highest yield" and "treatment $T_2$ gives the highest yield" are mutually exclusive.

**Independent Example:** The outcome of die-roll 1 and die-roll 2 are independent ŌĆö knowing one gives no information about the other.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Adapted for KTU 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** State and prove Bayes' Theorem of probability for two events. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** In a manufacturing process, Machine A produces 30% of items with 4% defective, Machine B produces 50% with 3% defective, and Machine C produces 20% with 5% defective. **(i)** Find the overall probability of a defective item. **(ii)** Given that an item is defective, find the probability it came from Machine B. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Statement & Proof:**

*Statement:* If $A$ and $B$ are two events with $P(B) > 0$, then

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

*Proof:* By definition of conditional probability:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \quad \text{and} \quad P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$$

From the second equation:
$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

Substituting into the first equation:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Application:**

Given:
- $P(A) = 0.30, \; P(B) = 0.50, \; P(C) = 0.20$
- $P(D \mid A) = 0.04, \; P(D \mid B) = 0.03, \; P(D \mid C) = 0.05$

**(i) Total probability:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(D) &= P(D \mid A) P(A) + P(D \mid B) P(B) + P(D \mid C) P(C) \\
&= (0.04)(0.30) + (0.03)(0.50) + (0.05)(0.20) \\
&= 0.012 + 0.015 + 0.010 \\
&= \mathbf{0.037}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Posterior probability via Bayes:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(B \mid D) &= \frac{P(D \mid B) \cdot P(B)}{P(D)} \\
&= \frac{(0.03)(0.50)}{0.037} \\
&= \frac{0.015}{0.037} \\
&\approx \mathbf{0.4054} \;\; \text{or} \;\; \mathbf{40.54\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** Define conditional probability. Show that for two independent events $A$ and $B$, $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The probability that a student passes a statistics test is 0.7. Given that the student has studied, the probability of passing becomes 0.9. The probability that a randomly selected student has studied is 0.6. Find: **(i)** The probability that a randomly selected student both studies AND passes. **(ii)** The probability that a student passes the test. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

*Definition:* Conditional probability of event $A$ given event $B$ (with $P(B) > 0$) is:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

*Proof of Independence Implication:*
If $A$ and $B$ are independent, by definition:
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

Substituting into the conditional probability formula:
$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A) \cdot P(B)}{P(B)} = P(A) \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Part (b):**

Let $S$ = "student studies", $P$ = "student passes".
- $P(P \mid S^{c}) = 0.7 \implies P(S^{c}) \cdot 0.7$ contributes to pass without study
- $P(P \mid S) = 0.9$, $P(S) = 0.6$

**(i) Probability of studying AND passing:**
$$
P(S \cap P) = P(P \mid S) \cdot P(S) = (0.9)(0.6) = \mathbf{0.54}
$$

**(ii) Total probability of passing:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(P) &= P(P \mid S) P(S) + P(P \mid S^{c}) P(S^{c}) \\
&= (0.9)(0.6) + (0.7)(0.4) \\
&= 0.54 + 0.28 \\
&= \mathbf{0.82}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the assumption** $P(B) > 0$ when writing conditional probability formulas. **(ŌłÆ1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing mutually exclusive with independent** in conceptual questions. These are NOT the same! Mutually exclusive events with non-zero probability are *negatively dependent*. **(ŌłÆ2 marks)**
> 3. **Skipping the units / interpretation step** in numerical answers. KTU examiners expect students to write the final answer as both a decimal AND a percentage (e.g., "0.024 or 2.4%"). **(ŌłÆ0.5 mark)**
> 4. **In Bayes' Theorem questions**, students often compute the numerator correctly but forget the **denominator** (total probability) ŌĆö leading to a posterior probability greater than 1. Always sanity-check! **(ŌłÆ2 marks)**
> 5. **In Part B 14-mark questions**, examiners look for **structured sub-parts**. Do not write one long paragraph ŌĆö use bullet points and explicit algebraic steps. **(ŌłÆ2 to ŌłÆ3 marks for unstructured answers)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Probability for DOE**

- Ō£ģ **Three Kolmogorov Axioms** (non-negativity, normalisation, additivity) are the foundation of every probability calculation in DOE.
- Ō£ģ **Sample space** $\Omega$ contains **all** possible outcomes; an **event** $A$ is any subset of $\Omega$.
- Ō£ģ **Complement rule:** $P(A^{c}) = 1 - P(A)$ ŌĆö used constantly when computing "fail to reject" probabilities.
- Ō£ģ **Addition rule:** $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ ŌĆö critical for combining event probabilities.
- Ō£ģ **Multiplication rule:** $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$ ŌĆö used in sequential experiments.
- Ō£ģ **Independence** means $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ ŌĆö the **basis of randomisation** in DOE.
- Ō£ģ **Total probability theorem** is the key pre-requisite for applying **Bayes' Theorem**.
- Ō£ģ **Bayes' Theorem:** Posterior $\propto$ Likelihood $\times$ Prior. Essential for Bayesian DOE and updating beliefs after observing data.
- Ō£ģ **Three definitions of probability** (Classical, Frequency, Subjective) ŌĆö know when each applies. DOE uses mostly the **frequency** interpretation.
- Ō£ģ **Distributions you must recognise in DOE:**
  - **Normal** $N(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ ŌåÆ measurement errors, residuals
  - **t-distribution** ŌåÆ comparing 2 means, small samples
  - **F-distribution** ŌåÆ ANOVA (comparing multiple means)
  - **Chi-square** $\chi^{2}$ ŌåÆ goodness-of-fit tests
- Ō£ģ **Always check** that your final probability lies in $[0, 1]$ ŌĆö if not, you have a logical or arithmetic error.
- Ō£ģ **For 14-mark questions**, structure your answer as: *Statement ŌåÆ Proof/Formula ŌåÆ Numerical substitution ŌåÆ Final answer with interpretation*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Density Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Density Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, a **Density Function** most commonly refers to the **Probability Density Function (PDF)**. It is a statistical function that describes the relative likelihood that a continuous random variable $X$ (representing an experimental observation, measurement, or response) takes on a given value within a specified interval.

Mathematically, a density function $f(x)$ is any non-negative, integrable function such that the total area under its curve equals **1 (unity)**.

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x)\, dx = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In DOE, density functions are the foundation of statistical inference. They govern how we model **experimental error**, perform **ANOVA (Analysis of Variance)**, and conduct **hypothesis testing (F-test, t-test)**. The Normal density function is the most frequently used assumption in classical DOE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you drop **1000 marbles** from a fixed height onto a long, bumpy wooden plank. Most marbles will cluster near the centre because the plank's shape naturally guides them there, but some roll to the edges. If you draw a graph where the **horizontal axis** is the final resting position of a marble, and the **vertical axis** is how many marbles stopped at each position ŌĆö that shape is essentially a **density function**.

**In simple terms:**
- The **height** of the curve at any point = how *likely* an outcome is near that value.
- **Tall peaks** = high probability (common outcomes).
- **Flat valleys** = low probability (rare outcomes).
- The **total area** under the curve is always **1** (100% of all possible outcomes are accounted for).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in DOE:** Every measurement you take in an experiment (e.g., yield of a chemical reaction, tensile strength of a metal) is influenced by uncontrollable random noise. That noise is mathematically modelled by a density function. Without understanding density functions, you cannot separate *real treatment effects* from *random noise*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Normal Mean:** $\mu = 0$
- **Standard Normal Variance:** $\sigma^2 = 1$
- **Total Probability Mass:** $\int f(x)\, dx = 1$
- **Probability within interval $[a,b]$:** $P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f(x)\, dx$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown: How a Density Function Operates

A density function operates on three fundamental axioms (Kolmogorov Axioms), which DOE relies upon implicitly:

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Non-Negativity:** For every $x$ in the sample space, $f(x) \ge 0$. Probability can never be negative.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Normalization:** The total area under the density curve equals exactly **1**. This ensures *all* possibilities are accounted for.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Additivity:** For two disjoint intervals $[a,b]$ and $[c,d]$, the probability that $X$ falls in either is the **sum** of their individual probabilities.

### 2.2 Properties of Density Functions (KTU High-Yield)

| Property | Formula | Engineering Utility in DOE |
|---|---|---|
| Total Area | $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x)\, dx = 1$ | Ensures exhaustive probability coverage |
| Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) | $F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt$ | Used to find $P(X \le x)$ in tolerance testing |
| Mean (Expected Value) | $E[X] = \mu = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x \cdot f(x)\, dx$ | Represents the *true* average response |
| Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} (x - \mu)^2 \cdot f(x)\, dx$ | Measures experimental error / noise |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}$ | Square root of mean squared deviation |
| Skewness | $\gamma_1 = E\!\left[\left(\tfrac{X-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^3\right]$ | Detects asymmetry in experimental data |
| Kurtosis | $\gamma_2 = E\!\left[\left(\tfrac{X-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^4\right] - 3$ | Measures tail-heaviness of error distribution |
| Mode | Value of $x$ where $f(x)$ is maximum | Most likely observed response |
| Median | $x$ such that $\int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt = 0.5$ | Robust central tendency indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** Never confuse a **Probability Density Function (PDF)** with a **Probability Mass Function (PMF)**. PDF applies to **continuous** random variables (e.g., tensile strength in MPa), while PMF applies to **discrete** random variables (e.g., number of defective items in a lot).

### 2.3 Common Density Functions Used in DOE

#### (a) Normal (Gaussian) Density Function
$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

- **Used in:** ANOVA, regression analysis, control charts, t-tests, F-tests.
- **Justification in DOE:** By the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**, the sampling distribution of the mean of many independent random errors tends toward Normal, regardless of the original error distribution.

#### (b) t-Distribution Density Function
$$f(t) = \frac{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{\nu+1}{2}\right)}{\sqrt{\nu\pi}\,\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{\nu}{2}\right)} \left(1 + \tfrac{t^2}{\nu}\right)^{-(\nu+1)/2}$$

- **Used in:** Small-sample hypothesis testing when $\sigma^2$ is unknown.
- **Key parameter:** Degrees of freedom $\nu$ (often $\nu = n-1$ for one-sample tests).

#### (c) F-Distribution Density Function
$$f(F) = \frac{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{d_1+d_2}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{d_1}{2}\right)\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{d_2}{2}\right)} \left(\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^{d_1/2} \frac{F^{(d_1/2)-1}}{\left[1 + \tfrac{d_1}{d_2}F\right]^{(d_1+d_2)/2}}$$

- **Used in:** ANOVA F-tests to compare variances between treatments.
- **Key parameters:** Numerator degrees of freedom $d_1$, denominator degrees of freedom $d_2$.

#### (d) Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Density Function
$$f(\chi^2) = \frac{1}{2^{k/2}\Gamma(k/2)} (\chi^2)^{(k/2)-1} e^{-\chi^2/2}$$

- **Used in:** Goodness-of-fit tests, confidence intervals for variance.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Manufacturing:** Modelling dimensional tolerances and surface roughness.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Modelling reaction yields, concentration fluctuations.
- **Civil Engineering:** Modelling material strength variability (concrete compressive strength).
- **Electronics:** Modelling signal noise in communication experiments.
- **Quality Control:** Setting control limits on Shewhart charts.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Normal Density Dominance in DOE:** The **Central Limit Theorem** states that the sum (or average) of a large number of independent random variables ŌĆö regardless of their individual distributions ŌĆö tends toward a Normal distribution. Since experimental errors are typically the cumulative effect of many small, independent disturbances, they are well approximated by a Normal density.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mean of a Density Function

**Given:** A continuous random variable $X$ with density $f(x)$.

**Goal:** Derive the expression for the expected value $E[X]$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Discretize the continuous variable.**
Partition the real line into infinitesimally small intervals of width $\Delta x$. The probability that $X$ lies in the interval $[x_i, x_i + \Delta x]$ is approximately $f(x_i) \cdot \Delta x$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the contribution to the average.**
The contribution of values near $x_i$ to the expected value is approximately $x_i \cdot f(x_i) \cdot \Delta x$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sum over all intervals.**
The total expected value is the sum of all such contributions across the entire real line:

$$E[X] \approx \sum_{i} x_i \cdot f(x_i) \cdot \Delta x$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Take the limit as $\Delta x \to 0$.**
As the partition becomes infinitely fine, the Riemann sum converges to a definite integral:

$$\mu = E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x \cdot f(x)\, dx$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Variance of a Density Function

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define deviation from the mean.**
Let $Y = X - \mu$. The variance of $X$ equals the expected value of $Y^2$.

$$\text{Var}(X) = E\!\left[(X - \mu)^2\right]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute into the integral definition of expectation.**

$$\sigma^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} (x - \mu)^2 \cdot f(x)\, dx$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Expand the squared term.**

$$\sigma^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \left(x^2 - 2\mu x + \mu^2\right) f(x)\, dx$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Split into three integrals.**

$$\sigma^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x^2 f(x)\, dx - 2\mu \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x\, f(x)\, dx + \mu^2 \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x)\, dx$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply known results.**

By definition, $\int x\, f(x)\, dx = \mu$, and $\int f(x)\, dx = 1$. Let $E[X^2] = \int x^2 f(x)\, dx$.

$$\sigma^2 = E[X^2] - 2\mu^2 + \mu^2 \cdot 1$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final simplified form.**

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = E[X^2] - \mu^2}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Probability in an Interval

**Given:** A density function $f(x)$ and an interval $[a, b]$.

**Goal:** Show that $P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f(x)\, dx$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recognize the CDF as an integral.**
By definition, the cumulative distribution function is:

$$F(x) = P(X \le x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use the difference of CDFs.**

$$P(a \le X \le b) = P(X \le b) - P(X < a) = F(b) - F(a)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the integral form.**

$$P(a \le X \le b) = \int_{-\infty}^{b} f(t)\, dt - \int_{-\infty}^{a} f(t)\, dt$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the additive property of integrals.**

$$P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f(t)\, dt$$

### 3.4 Worked Example: Normal Density Properties

For the Standard Normal density $f(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}$:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verify normalization.**
It is a classical result (proved using Gaussian integrals and polar coordinates) that:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}\, dz = 1$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the mean.**

$$\mu = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} z \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}\, dz$$

The integrand is an **odd function** (since $z \cdot e^{-z^2/2}$ is odd), and the limits are symmetric about zero. Therefore:

$$\mu = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the variance.**

$$\sigma^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} z^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}\, dz$$

Let $u = z^2/2$, so $du = z\, dz$. Using the standard moment result for Gaussian integrals:

$$\sigma^2 = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Standard Normal density is *unitless* because it has been standardised by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation. This allows engineers to compare experimental responses measured in different units (e.g., MPa vs. ┬░C) on a common scale.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Probability Density Function in DOE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Experimental Observation X] --> B{Is X Continuous?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Probability Density Function f of x]
    B -- No --> D[Probability Mass Function P of X equals x]
    C --> E[Normalization Check Integral equals 1]
    C --> F[Compute Mean mu]
    C --> G[Compute Variance sigma squared]
    C --> H[Compute Skewness and Kurtosis]
    E --> I{Valid Density Function?}
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -- Yes --> J[Apply to DOE Inference]
    I -- No --> K[Re-specify Model]
    J --> L[Hypothesis Testing]
    J --> M[Confidence Intervals]
    J --> N[ANOVA and F Test]
    J --> O[Regression Analysis]
    L --> P[Decision on Treatment Effect]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Mapping Density Function Families to DOE Applications

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Norm[Normal Density Family]
        N1[Standard Normal Z]
        N2[General Normal N mu sigma]
    end
    subgraph Sampling[Sampling Distribution Family]
        S1[Chi Square Distribution]
        S2[t Distribution]
        S3[F Distribution]
    end
    N1 --> S2
    N1 --> S1
    N1 --> S3
    N2 --> A1[Z Test for Large Samples]
    S1 --> A2[Goodness of Fit Test]
    S2 --> A3[t Test for Small Samples]
    S3 --> A4[ANOVA F Test]
```

### 4.3 Workflow: From Raw Experimental Data to Density Function Inference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Raw Experimental Data] --> collect[Collect n Observations]
    collect --> est[Estimate Sample Mean and Variance]
    est --> select{Choose Density Function}
    select -- Symmetric and bell shaped --> npath[Assume Normal Density]
    select -- Small sample size --> tpath[Use t Distribution]
    select -- Compare two variances --> fpath[Use F Distribution]
    npath --> valid[Validate via QQ Plot or KS Test]
    tpath --> valid
    fpath --> valid
    valid --> infer[Perform Statistical Inference]
    infer --> out[Conclude Treatment Significance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each node represents a logical stage in the DOE pipeline. The arrows depict information flow. The choice of density function is dictated by the experimental design constraints (sample size, equal/unequal variances, normality assumptions).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**Define Probability Density Function. State its two essential properties.**

**Model Answer:**
A Probability Density Function (PDF) is a non-negative function $f(x)$ that describes the relative likelihood of a continuous random variable $X$ taking a value within a given interval.

**Two essential properties:**
1. **Non-negativity:** $f(x) \ge 0$ for all $x$.
2. **Normalization:** $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x)\, dx = 1$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Distinguish between a Probability Density Function (PDF) and a Probability Mass Function (PMF).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PDF | PMF |
|---|---|---|
| Variable type | Continuous | Discrete |
| Represents | Density (per unit length) | Exact probability |
| Total sums to | Integral equals 1 | Sum equals 1 |
| Value at a point | $f(x)$ can exceed 1 | $0 \le P(X=x) \le 1$ |
| Example in DOE | Tensile strength | Number of defectives |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the **mean** and **variance** of a continuous random variable from its probability density function. State clearly each axiom used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A continuous random variable $X$ has density:

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} kx^2 & \text{for } 0 \le x \le 3 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Find:
1. The value of the constant $k$.
2. The mean $E[X]$.
3. The variance $\text{Var}(X)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

*Mean derivation:* Consider partitioning the real line into infinitesimally small intervals of width $\Delta x$. The probability that $X$ lies in $[x_i, x_i + \Delta x]$ is $f(x_i) \cdot \Delta x$. The contribution to the average from this interval is $x_i \cdot f(x_i) \cdot \Delta x$. Summing and taking the limit $\Delta x \to 0$:

$$\mu = E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x \cdot f(x)\, dx$$

*Variance derivation:* Using $E[(X-\mu)^2]$:

$$\sigma^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} (x-\mu)^2 f(x)\, dx = E[X^2] - \mu^2$$

where $E[X^2] = \int x^2 f(x)\, dx$.

*Axioms used:* Non-negativity and normalization of $f(x)$.

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply normalization to find $k$.**

$$\int_0^3 kx^2\, dx = 1$$

$$\left[\frac{kx^3}{3}\right]_0^3 = \frac{27k}{3} = 9k = 1$$

$$k = \frac{1}{9}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the mean.**

$$\mu = \int_0^3 x \cdot \frac{x^2}{9}\, dx = \frac{1}{9}\int_0^3 x^3\, dx = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{81}{4} = \frac{9}{4} = 2.25$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $E[X^2]$.**

$$E[X^2] = \int_0^3 x^2 \cdot \frac{x^2}{9}\, dx = \frac{1}{9}\int_0^3 x^4\, dx = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{243}{5} = \frac{27}{5} = 5.4$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the variance.**

$$\sigma^2 = E[X^2] - \mu^2 = 5.4 - (2.25)^2 = 5.4 - 5.0625 = 0.3375$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the significance of the **Normal density function** in Design of Experiments. State its PDF and derive its normalization property. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The lifetime (in hours) of a certain electronic component follows a Normal distribution with $\mu = 1000$ and $\sigma = 100$. Find:
1. $P(X > 1100)$
2. $P(900 < X < 1100)$ **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Significance of Normal Density in DOE:**

The Normal density function is the cornerstone of classical experimental design because:

1. The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** guarantees that averages of independent random errors tend toward Normality, justifying its widespread use.
2. It underlies **ANOVA, regression, t-tests, and F-tests**.
3. It is fully described by only **two parameters** ($\mu$ and $\sigma^2$), simplifying inference.

**PDF:**

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Normalization proof sketch:** Evaluate $I = \int e^{-x^2/2}\, dx$. Then $I^2 = \int\!\!\int e^{-(x^2+y^2)/2}\, dx\, dy$. Switching to polar coordinates and integrating over $[0, 2\pi] \times [0, \infty)$ yields $I^2 = 2\pi$, so $I = \sqrt{2\pi}$. Applying the substitution $z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$ confirms the integral equals **1**.

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Standardize to $Z = (X-\mu)/\sigma$.**
Here $\sigma = 100$, $\mu = 1000$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $P(X > 1100)$.**

$$P(X > 1100) = P\!\left(Z > \frac{1100 - 1000}{100}\right) = P(Z > 1.00)$$

From standard normal tables: $P(Z > 1.00) = 1 - \Phi(1.00) = 1 - 0.8413 = 0.1587$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $P(900 < X < 1100)$.**

$$P(900 < X < 1100) = P(-1.00 < Z < 1.00) = \Phi(1.00) - \Phi(-1.00)$$

Using symmetry $\Phi(-1.00) = 1 - \Phi(1.00) = 0.1587$:

$$P(900 < X < 1100) = 0.8413 - 0.1587 = 0.6826$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to standardize:** Many students substitute raw values directly into the Normal PDF and integrate ŌĆö this is mathematically tedious and *wrong* in approach. Always convert to the standard normal $Z$ and use tables.
> 2. **Confusing $\le$ with $<$:** For continuous distributions, $P(X < a) = P(X \le a)$. There is no point mass at a single value.
> 3. **Skipping the constant $k$ in piecewise PDFs:** Examiners award a separate mark for *finding* $k$ from the normalization condition. Skipping it leads to cascading errors.
> 4. **Missing the units of $\sigma$:** Variance has units of $X^2$, while standard deviation has the same units as $X$. Be precise.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A PDF is a non-negative function whose total integral over the real line equals **1**.
- **Continuous vs Discrete:** Use PDF for continuous random variables (e.g., strength, time); use PMF for discrete variables (e.g., defect counts).
- **Mean (Expected Value):** $E[X] = \int x \cdot f(x)\, dx$.
- **Variance:** $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$.
- **CDF:** $F(x) = P(X \le x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt$.
- **Probability in interval:** $P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f(x)\, dx$.
- **Normal PDF is dominant in DOE** due to the Central Limit Theorem.
- **Standardization:** Convert any Normal to $Z \sim N(0,1)$ using $Z = (X-\mu)/\sigma$.
- **Symmetry:** Odd integrands over symmetric intervals evaluate to zero (e.g., mean of Standard Normal is 0).
- **Skewness/Kurtosis:** Higher moments help detect deviations from Normality ŌĆö essential before applying DOE tests.
- **KTU Tip:** Always state both properties (non-negativity and normalization) when defining a PDF in exams.
- **Quick Sanity Check:** If your computed probability lies outside $[0, 1]$ or your variance is negative, revisit your integration limits and algebra.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cumulative Distribution Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cumulative Distribution Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** of a random variable $X$ is defined as the function $F_X(x)$ that gives, for every real number $x$, the probability that the random variable $X$ takes a value less than or equal to $x$.

Mathematically, for any random variable $X$ (discrete, continuous, or mixed):

$$
F_X(x) = P(X \leq x), \quad x \in \mathbb{R}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Convention (KTU Board Standard):** The function is universally denoted as $F_X(x)$ or simply $F(x)$. The subscript $X$ identifies the random variable to which the CDF belongs. This is the formal KTU 2024 Scheme definition expected in Part A and Part B examinations.

The CDF is a **fundamental unifying tool** in probability theory because the same definition works for both discrete and continuous random variables, making it the most general way to describe a probability distribution.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tank filling up from the bottom**.

- The water level represents the cumulative probability accumulated.
- At the very bottom of the tank (very small $x$), almost no water has accumulated, so $F(x) \approx 0$.
- At the very top (very large $x$), the tank is full, so $F(x) = 1$.
- As we move up the tank, the water level (probability) can only **rise or stay the same** ŌĆö it never falls. This is the **non-decreasing** property.

So, the CDF $F(x)$ tells you: *"Up to this point $x$, how much total probability mass has accumulated?"* ŌĆö much like reading a running total on a billing meter.

Another intuitive view: Think of $F(x)$ as the **"credit score" of probability** ŌĆö it sums up all probability contributions from $-\infty$ up to the point $x$.

## 1.3 Why the CDF is Central to Design of Experiments

In **Design of Experiments (DoE)**, we frequently deal with:

- **Test statistics** (F-test, t-test, Chi-square) whose distributions are defined entirely through their CDFs.
- **Critical regions** such as $P(X > x_\alpha) = \alpha$, which are evaluated using $1 - F(x_\alpha)$.
- **P-values** in ANOVA tables, which are computed as $1 - F(X_{obs})$.

Thus, mastering the CDF is essential for interpreting every hypothesis-testing scenario in DoE.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Probability Values:** The KTU syllabus emphasizes the two boundary limits $\lim_{x \to -\infty} F(x) = \mathbf{0}$ and $\lim_{x \to +\infty} F(x) = \mathbf{1}$. These are considered compulsory points in short-answer questions.

## 1.4 Categorization of CDF by Random Variable Type

| Random Variable Type | CDF Form | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **Discrete** | $F_X(x) = \sum_{t \leq x} P(X = t)$ | High (3ŌĆō7 marks) |
| **Continuous** | $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt$ | Very High (7ŌĆō14 marks) |
| **Mixed** | Combines jump discontinuities and continuous segments | Moderate |

This taxonomy will recur throughout Module 1 and subsequent modules of OECHT727.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Axiomatic Properties of a CDF

For $F_X(x)$ to qualify as a valid Cumulative Distribution Function, it must satisfy the following four **Kolmogorov axioms** (these are routinely asked as 3-mark questions):

### Property 1 ŌĆö Non-Decreasing (Monotonically Increasing)
$$
\text{If } x_1 < x_2 \text{ then } F_X(x_1) \leq F_X(x_2)
$$
**Why:** Probability of a larger set cannot be smaller than that of a smaller set. As $x$ grows, we accumulate more outcomes, so $F(x)$ cannot fall.

### Property 2 ŌĆö Bounded Between 0 and 1
$$
0 \leq F_X(x) \leq 1, \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}
$$
**Why:** Probabilities are inherently bounded. The CDF never leaves the unit interval.

### Property 3 ŌĆö Left Boundary Limit
$$
\lim_{x \to -\infty} F_X(x) = 0
$$
**Why:** The probability that $X$ is less than or equal to an extremely small number tends to zero.

### Property 4 ŌĆö Right Boundary Limit
$$
\lim_{x \to +\infty} F_X(x) = 1
$$
**Why:** The probability that $X$ is less than or equal to an extremely large number is the entire sample space ŌĆö certainty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bonus Property (Continuous Case Only):** A CDF is **right-continuous** at every point $x$: $\lim_{h \to 0^+} F_X(x + h) = F_X(x)$. This is the standard convention used by KTU examiners.

## 2.2 The Core Probability Extraction Formula

The single most important operational formula in CDF theory is:

$$
P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)
$$

**Derivation Logic:**
- $F_X(b)$ = probability of all outcomes $\leq b$
- $F_X(a)$ = probability of all outcomes $\leq a$
- Their difference = probability of outcomes strictly between $a$ and $b$ (inclusive of $b$, exclusive of $a$).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-day reference** for any CDF-based problem. Memorize these identities before every test.

| # | Formula / Identity | Applicability | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$ | Universal definition | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $F_X(x) = \sum_{t \leq x} P(X=t)$ | Discrete RVs | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt$ | Continuous RVs | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $f_X(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F_X(x)$ | Continuous RVs (where differentiable) | $\text{unit}^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$ | Universal | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $P(X > a) = 1 - F_X(a)$ | Universal | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $P(X \geq a) = 1 - F_X(a^-)$ | Universal (careful at jump points) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\lim_{x \to -\infty} F_X(x) = 0$ | Universal axiom | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\lim_{x \to +\infty} F_X(x) = 1$ | Universal axiom | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $0 \leq F_X(x) \leq 1$ | Universal axiom | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $F_X(x^-) \leq F_X(x) \leq F_X(x^+)$ | Jump continuity rule | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $E[X] = \int_{0}^{\infty} \big(1 - F_X(x)\big)\, dx - \int_{-\infty}^{0} F_X(x)\, dx$ | Continuous non-negative RV | Original units of $X$ |

> **Critical Exam Tip:** Identity 12 is rarely tested but extremely powerful. The KTU board often awards **bonus marks** to students who connect CDFs to expected values via survival functions.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In engineering applications rooted in Design of Experiments:

- **Quality Control:** The CDF of a defect-rate variable gives the fraction of products whose defect rate is below a tolerance threshold.
- **Reliability Engineering:** $1 - F_X(t)$ is the **survival function** $R(t)$, denoting the probability a component is still operational at time $t$.
- **Signal Processing:** Cumulative energy distributions of noisy signals.
- **Manufacturing Tolerances:** Probability that a machined shaft diameter lies within specification limits $\big[F_X(\text{USL}) - F_X(\text{LSL})\big]$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CDF from a Discrete Probability Mass Function

### Problem Setup
Consider a discrete random variable $X$ taking values $\{x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots\}$ with PMF $P(X = x_i) = p_i$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the definition.**

$$
F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Decompose the event $\{X \leq x\}$ into the union of elementary events.**

$$
\{X \leq x\} = \bigcup_{i \,:\, x_i \leq x} \{X = x_i\}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Since the elementary events are mutually exclusive, apply the additive rule of probability.**

$$
F_X(x) = \sum_{i \,:\, x_i \leq x} P(X = x_i) = \sum_{i \,:\, x_i \leq x} p_i
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Recognize that this is a step function.**

For $x$ values that fall **between** two support points, the CDF remains constant (it "steps up" only at each $x_i$).

$$
F_X(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i \quad \text{when } x_k \leq x < x_{k+1}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> Let $X$ take values $0, 1, 2$ with $P(X=0)=0.2$, $P(X=1)=0.5$, $P(X=2)=0.3$. Compute $F_X(1.5)$.

**Solution:**

$$
F_X(1.5) = P(X \leq 1.5) = P(X=0) + P(X=1) = 0.2 + 0.5 = 0.7
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: CDF from a Continuous Probability Density Function

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the definition.**

$$
F_X(x) = P(X \leq x) = P(-\infty < X \leq x)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use the interpretation of the PDF as a probability density.**

The PDF $f_X(t)$ satisfies $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f_X(t)\, dt$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply this integral formula over the interval $(-\infty, x]$.**

$$
F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$ using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.**

$$
\frac{d}{dx} F_X(x) = f_X(x)
$$

This is the inverse relationship: **the PDF is the derivative of the CDF** (at points where the derivative exists).

### Worked Numerical Example (Standard Normal CDF)

> If $Z \sim N(0, 1)$, find $P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1)$ using the standard normal CDF $\Phi(z)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the probability extraction identity.**

$$
P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) = F_Z(1) - F_Z(-1) = \Phi(1) - \Phi(-1)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use the symmetry property of the standard normal.**

$$
\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)
$$

$$
\Phi(-1) = 1 - \Phi(1)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute back.**

$$
P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) = \Phi(1) - (1 - \Phi(1)) = 2\Phi(1) - 1
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Read from the standard normal table $\Phi(1) = 0.8413$.**

$$
P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) = 2(0.8413) - 1 = 0.6826
$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **68.26%** of observations from a standard normal distribution fall within one standard deviation of the mean. This is the famous "68-95-99.7 rule" anchored at $\pm 1\sigma$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Discrete to Continuous CDF Behavior

| Property | Discrete CDF | Continuous CDF |
|---|---|---|
| Nature of function | Step function (staircase) | Smooth, continuous |
| Differentiable everywhere | No (only between jumps) | Yes (where PDF is continuous) |
| Jumps at support points | Yes, jump = $p_i$ | No |
| Probability at single point | $P(X=x) = F(x) - F(x^-)$ | $P(X=x) = 0$ |
| Recovery of distribution | From PMF via summation | From PDF via differentiation |

## 3.4 Derivation of the Expectation via the CDF (Identity 12)

For a non-negative continuous random variable $X$:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the standard expectation formula.**

$$
E[X] = \int_{0}^{\infty} x \, f_X(x)\, dx
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply integration by parts with $u = x$ and $dv = f_X(x) dx$.**

Let $u = x \Rightarrow du = dx$ and $v = F_X(x)$.

$$
E[X] = \big[x \, F_X(x)\big]_0^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} F_X(x)\, dx
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate the boundary term.**

Since $F_X(0) = 0$ and $\lim_{x \to \infty} x \, F_X(x) = 0$ (under mild integrability conditions):

$$
E[X] = 0 - \int_{0}^{\infty} F_X(x)\, dx
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rewrite using the survival function.**

$$
E[X] = \int_{0}^{\infty} \big(1 - F_X(x)\big)\, dx
$$

This elegant identity allows computation of expected values **directly from the CDF** without ever computing the PDF ŌĆö a powerful trick in DoE for quick sanity checks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CDF Computation Workflow Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the operational logic for computing probabilities using the CDF, mapped as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Identify Random Variable X and Its Type"] --> nodeB{"Is X Discrete or Continuous?"}
    nodeB -- Discrete --> nodeC["Use PMF P of X equals x"]
    nodeB -- Continuous --> nodeD["Use PDF f of x"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Compute CDF via Summation F equals Sum of p_i"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Compute CDF via Integration F equals Integral of f t dt"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Apply Probability Extraction Formula"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["P of a less than X less than or equal b equals F of b minus F of a"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Validate Against 4 Axiomatic Properties"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Final Numerical Probability Answer"]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Relationship Between PDF, CDF, and PMF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Probability Mass Function P of X equals x"] -- Summation --> node2["CDF F of x via Discrete Formula"]
    node3["Probability Density Function f of x"] -- Integration --> node4["CDF F of x via Continuous Formula"]
    node2 -- Difference F of x minus F of x minus --> node1
    node4 -- Differentiation dF by dx --> node3
    node2 -- Survival Function 1 minus F of x --> node5["Reliability R of t"]
    node4 -- Survival Function 1 minus F of x --> node5
```

## 4.3 Step-by-Step Decision Flow for Solving CDF Problems in the Exam Hall

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Read the Problem Statement"] --> stepB["Identify the Random Variable and Its Support"]
    stepB --> stepC["Recognize the Distribution Type Discrete or Continuous"]
    stepC --> stepD["Select the Appropriate CDF Formula"]
    stepD --> stepE{"Problem Asks for P between a and b?"}
    stepE -- Yes --> stepF["Apply P of a less than X less than or equal b equals F of b minus F of a"]
    stepE -- No --> stepG["Directly Evaluate F at the Given Point"]
    stepF --> stepH["Compute Numerical Value Using Tables or Integration"]
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI["Cross Check With Boundary Limits 0 and 1"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]
**Define the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of a random variable $X$ and state any three of its properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of a random variable $X$ is defined as:

$$
F_X(x) = P(X \leq x), \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}
$$

Three properties are:

1. **Bounded:** $0 \leq F_X(x) \leq 1$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.
2. **Non-decreasing:** If $x_1 < x_2$, then $F_X(x_1) \leq F_X(x_2)$.
3. **Boundary limits:** $\lim_{x \to -\infty} F_X(x) = 0$ and $\lim_{x \to +\infty} F_X(x) = 1$.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for any two properties + 0.5 mark bonus for stating the third cleanly.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between the CDF of a discrete random variable and the CDF of a continuous random variable with appropriate formulas.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a **discrete** random variable with PMF $P(X = x_i) = p_i$:

$$
F_X(x) = \sum_{i \,:\, x_i \leq x} p_i
$$

This produces a **step function** that jumps at each support point by an amount $p_i$.

For a **continuous** random variable with PDF $f_X(x)$:

$$
F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt
$$

This produces a **smooth, continuous, differentiable** function whose derivative equals the PDF.

*(1.5 marks each for both formulas + their distinguishing characteristics.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]

**(a)** The probability mass function of a discrete random variable $X$ is given by:

| $x$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P(X=x)$ | 0.10 | 0.25 | 0.40 | 0.25 |

Find $F_X(x)$ for all $x$. Also compute $P(0.5 < X \leq 2)$ and $P(X > 2)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If $X$ is a continuous random variable with PDF $f_X(x) = 2x$ for $0 \leq x \leq 1$ and $0$ elsewhere, derive the CDF $F_X(x)$, plot its nature, and find $P(0.3 \leq X \leq 0.7)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute CDF at each support point and in between.**

For $x < 0$: $F_X(x) = 0$.

For $0 \leq x < 1$:

$$
F_X(x) = P(X = 0) = 0.10
$$

For $1 \leq x < 2$:

$$
F_X(x) = P(X=0) + P(X=1) = 0.10 + 0.25 = 0.35
$$

For $2 \leq x < 3$:

$$
F_X(x) = 0.10 + 0.25 + 0.40 = 0.75
$$

For $x \geq 3$:

$$
F_X(x) = 0.10 + 0.25 + 0.40 + 0.25 = 1.00
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $P(0.5 < X \leq 2)$.**

Using the identity $P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$:

$$
P(0.5 < X \leq 2) = F_X(2) - F_X(0.5) = 0.75 - 0.10 = 0.65
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $P(X > 2)$.**

$$
P(X > 2) = 1 - P(X \leq 2) = 1 - F_X(2) = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25
$$

*(This 7-mark question awards: 3 marks for CDF construction, 2 marks for $P(0.5 < X \leq 2)$, and 2 marks for $P(X > 2)$.)*

---

#### Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verify the PDF integrates to 1.**

$$
\int_{0}^{1} 2t\, dt = \big[t^2\big]_0^1 = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Derive the CDF by integration.**

For $x < 0$:

$$
F_X(x) = 0
$$

For $0 \leq x \leq 1$:

$$
F_X(x) = \int_{0}^{x} 2t\, dt = \big[t^2\big]_0^x = x^2
$$

For $x > 1$:

$$
F_X(x) = \int_{0}^{1} 2t\, dt = 1
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Nature of the CDF.** $F_X(x)$ is a smooth, monotonically increasing quadratic curve on $[0, 1]$ going from $0$ to $1$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $P(0.3 \leq X \leq 0.7)$.**

$$
P(0.3 \leq X \leq 0.7) = F_X(0.7) - F_X(0.3) = (0.7)^2 - (0.3)^2 = 0.49 - 0.09 = 0.40
$$

*(Award allocation: 2 marks for PDF verification, 3 marks for deriving the CDF piecewise, 2 marks for the final probability.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model ŌĆö Alternative Choice]

**(a)** The lifetime $X$ (in hours) of an electronic component has CDF:

$$
F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & x < 0 \\ 1 - e^{-0.002 x} & x \geq 0 \end{cases}
$$

Find the PDF, compute $P(X > 1000)$, and find the median lifetime. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a discrete random variable $Y$ with CDF:

$$
F_Y(y) = \begin{cases} 0 & y < 1 \\ 0.2 & 1 \leq y < 2 \\ 0.5 & 2 \leq y < 3 \\ 0.9 & 3 \leq y < 4 \\ 1.0 & y \geq 4 \end{cases}
$$

Determine the PMF, compute $E[Y]$, and verify whether $F_Y(y)$ is a valid CDF. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find the PDF by differentiation.**

For $x \geq 0$:

$$
f_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx} F_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\big(1 - e^{-0.002 x}\big) = 0.002\, e^{-0.002 x}
$$

For $x < 0$: $f_X(x) = 0$. This is the **exponential distribution** with rate $\lambda = 0.002$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $P(X > 1000)$.**

$$
P(X > 1000) = 1 - F_X(1000) = 1 - (1 - e^{-2}) = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Find the median lifetime $m$ such that $F_X(m) = 0.5$.**

$$
1 - e^{-0.002 m} = 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-0.002 m} = 0.5
$$

$$
-0.002 m = \ln(0.5) = -0.6931
$$

$$
m = \frac{0.6931}{0.002} \approx 346.57 \text{ hours}
$$

---

#### Solution to B(b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Determine the PMF from the jump discontinuities.**

The PMF values are the jump heights at $y = 1, 2, 3, 4$:

$$
P(Y = 1) = 0.2 - 0 = 0.2
$$

$$
P(Y = 2) = 0.5 - 0.2 = 0.3
$$

$$
P(Y = 3) = 0.9 - 0.5 = 0.4
$$

$$
P(Y = 4) = 1.0 - 0.9 = 0.1
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $E[Y]$.**

$$
E[Y] = (1)(0.2) + (2)(0.3) + (3)(0.4) + (4)(0.1)
$$

$$
E[Y] = 0.2 + 0.6 + 1.2 + 0.4 = 2.4
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verify validity of the CDF.**

- All probabilities $\geq 0$ Ō£ō
- All probabilities sum to $1$: $0.2 + 0.3 + 0.4 + 0.1 = 1.0$ Ō£ō
- CDF is non-decreasing Ō£ō
- Boundary limits $F(-\infty) = 0$, $F(\infty) = 1$ Ō£ō

Hence, $F_Y(y)$ is a valid CDF.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing strict vs. non-strict inequalities:** Students frequently write $P(a \leq X \leq b)$ as $F(b) - F(a)$. For discrete RVs, this equals $F(b) - F(a^-)$ (the left limit at $a$). Use $F(b) - F(a)$ only when the lower bound is **strict**.
> 2. **Forgetting piecewise definitions:** Many students write the CDF formula without specifying the regions ($x < 0$, $0 \leq x \leq 1$, $x > 1$). This loses **2 marks** consistently.
> 3. **Misapplying derivative to discrete CDFs:** A discrete CDF is **not differentiable** at jump points. Do not write $f(x) = F'(x)$ for discrete RVs.
> 4. **Boundary check omission:** Failing to verify $F(-\infty) = 0$ and $F(+\infty) = 1$ costs a half-mark penalty under strict KTU 2024 valuation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Memorize Before Every KTU Exam**

- Ō£ģ CDF is **defined universally** as $F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$ ŌĆö works for all RV types.
- Ō£ģ The **four axioms** are: non-decreasing, bounded in $[0, 1]$, $F(-\infty) = 0$, $F(+\infty) = 1$.
- Ō£ģ For **discrete RVs**: $F_X(x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} p_i$ (step function).
- Ō£ģ For **continuous RVs**: $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt$ (smooth curve).
- Ō£ģ **Inverse relationship (continuous only):** $f_X(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F_X(x)$.
- Ō£ģ **Probability extraction:** $P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$.
- Ō£ģ **Survival function:** $P(X > a) = 1 - F(a)$.
- Ō£ģ For discrete RVs, **jump height** = $P(X = x_i) = F(x_i) - F(x_i^-)$.
- Ō£ģ CDF is **right-continuous** by convention.
- Ō£ģ CDF can be used to compute $E[X]$ without the PDF via $E[X] = \int_0^\infty (1 - F(x))\, dx$ for non-negative RVs.
- Ō£ģ In DoE, the CDF underpins the **F-distribution, t-distribution, and Chi-square distribution** ŌĆö all critical for ANOVA tests.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measure of Central Tendency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measure of Central Tendency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Measure of Central Tendency (MCT):** A statistical measure that identifies a single value as representative of an entire distribution of data. It aims to provide an accurate description of the **centre** of the data set by identifying the central position around which the observations are clustered.

In the context of **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, the measure of central tendency is the **first descriptive statistic** computed after data collection. It summarises replicate observations, helps in detecting outliers when combined with dispersion measures, and forms the foundation for **ANOVA (Analysis of Variance)**, **regression analysis**, and **hypothesis testing**.

The three classical measures of central tendency, as per KTU 2024 syllabus, are:

1. **Arithmetic Mean (AM)** ŌĆö the most widely used average in engineering statistics.
2. **Median** ŌĆö the positional middle value, robust to extreme values.
3. **Mode** ŌĆö the most frequently occurring value, useful in categorical and quality-control data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DOE requires Central Tendency:** In any experimental run, the response $Y$ is observed multiple times. The central tendency summarises the "true" effect of a factor level, while dispersion quantifies noise. Together they form the building blocks of **variance decomposition**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine **100 students** writing a KTU end-semester exam. Their marks range from 35 to 98. Instead of reporting every individual mark, the examiner says: *"The class average is 72."* That single number **72** is a **measure of central tendency** ŌĆö a representative value that captures where most students cluster.

- The **arithmetic mean (72)** is like the *balance point* of a see-saw where every mark is placed at equal distance.
- The **median (71)** is the *student standing exactly in the middle* of the line arranged by height (marks).
- The **mode (70)** is the *most common height* in the class ŌĆö the height shared by the largest group.

## 1.3 Types of Data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **choice of central tendency measure** strictly depends on the **data type**:

| Data Type | Best Measure | Reason |
|-----------|--------------|--------|
| Numerical (Continuous) | **Arithmetic Mean** | Uses all data values |
| Skewed / Ordinal | **Median** | Not affected by extremes |
| Categorical / Discrete | **Mode** | Identifies the most frequent class |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Arithmetic Mean (AM)

### 2.1.1 Simple Arithmetic Mean (Ungrouped Data)

The arithmetic mean of $n$ observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ is defined as the sum of all observations divided by the number of observations.

**Logic Steps:**
- **Step 1:** Sum all observations: $S = x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n$.
- **Step 2:** Count the total number of observations: $n$.
- **Step 3:** Divide sum by count: $\bar{x} = S / n$.

### 2.1.2 Weighted Arithmetic Mean

When observations carry different importance (e.g., different number of replicates per treatment in DOE), a **weighted mean** is used:

$$
\bar{x}_w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}
$$

where $w_i$ is the weight assigned to $x_i$.

### 2.1.3 Grouped Data Mean

For frequency-distributed data with class marks $x_i$ and frequencies $f_i$:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}
$$

### 2.1.4 Properties of Arithmetic Mean

- It is **rigidly defined** ŌĆö unique value for a given data set.
- It uses **every observation**.
- **Algebraic property:** $\sum (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$ (sum of deviations is zero).
- **Squaring property:** $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ is **minimum** (least squares principle ŌĆö this is the foundation of OLS regression in DOE).
- Affected by **outliers** and **extreme values** (not robust).

## 2.2 Median

### 2.2.1 Median for Ungrouped Data

- **Step 1:** Arrange the $n$ observations in **ascending or descending** order.
- **Step 2:** If $n$ is **odd**, median is the $\left(\dfrac{n+1}{2}\right)$-th value.
- **Step 3:** If $n$ is **even**, median is the **average** of the $\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right)$-th and $\left(\dfrac{n}{2} + 1\right)$-th values.

$$
\text{Median} = 
\begin{cases}
x_{\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)} & \text{if } n \text{ is odd} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{x_{\left(\frac{n}{2}\right)} + x_{\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)}}{2} & \text{if } n \text{ is even}
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.2.2 Median for Grouped Data

$$
\text{Median} = L + \left( \frac{\frac{N}{2} - F}{f} \right) \cdot h
$$

where:
- $L$ = lower class boundary of the median class
- $N$ = total frequency ($\sum f_i$)
- $F$ = cumulative frequency **before** the median class
- $f$ = frequency of the median class
- $h$ = class width

The **median class** is the first class whose cumulative frequency is $\geq N/2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Median is robust** ŌĆö it ignores the magnitude of extreme values, considering only their position. This makes it ideal for **skewed experimental data** in DOE.

## 2.3 Mode

### 2.3.1 Mode for Ungrouped Data

The value that occurs **most frequently**. A data set may have **no mode**, **one mode** (unimodal), **two modes** (bimodal), or **more than two modes** (multimodal).

### 2.3.2 Mode for Grouped Data

$$
\text{Mode} = L + \left( \frac{f_1 - f_0}{2 f_1 - f_0 - f_2} \right) \cdot h
$$

where:
- $L$ = lower class boundary of the modal class
- $f_1$ = frequency of the modal class
- $f_0$ = frequency of the class **preceding** the modal class
- $f_2$ = frequency of the class **succeeding** the modal class
- $h$ = class width

The **modal class** is the class with the **highest frequency**.

## 2.4 Empirical Relationship Between Mean, Median, and Mode

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **moderately skewed, unimodal distribution**, Karl Pearson established the empirical formula:

$$
\text{Mode} = 3 \cdot \text{Median} - 2 \cdot \text{Mean}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\text{Mean} - \text{Mode} = 3 \,(\text{Mean} - \text{Median})
$$

This is a high-yield KTU question. The right-hand side is $3 \times$ (Pearson's coefficient of skewness base).

## 2.5 Other Important Means (Reference)

| Mean Type | Formula | Application in DOE / Engineering |
|-----------|---------|----------------------------------|
| **Geometric Mean (GM)** | $GM = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{n} x_i \right)^{1/n}$ | Averaging **ratios, growth rates, signal-to-noise ratios** in factorial design |
| **Harmonic Mean (HM)** | $HM = \dfrac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{x_i}}$ | Averaging **rates** (e.g., average speed) |
| **Quadratic Mean / RMS** | $RMS = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{n}}$ | **Noise analysis, vibration, electrical engineering** |

> [!NOTE]
> For positive numbers: $HM \leq GM \leq AM \leq RMS$. This is the **AM-GM-HM inequality**, occasionally asked in KTU.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Measure | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 | Simple AM (ungrouped) | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | Raw data |
| 2 | Weighted AM | $\bar{x}_w = \dfrac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}$ | Observations with different importance |
| 3 | Grouped AM | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}$ | Frequency table |
| 4 | Median (odd $n$) | $x_{\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)}$ | Sorted data |
| 5 | Median (even $n$) | $\dfrac{x_{(n/2)} + x_{(n/2+1)}}{2}$ | Sorted data |
| 6 | Grouped Median | $L + \left(\dfrac{N/2 - F}{f}\right) h$ | Class-interval data |
| 7 | Grouped Mode | $L + \left(\dfrac{f_1 - f_0}{2 f_1 - f_0 - f_2}\right) h$ | Class-interval data |
| 8 | Empirical Relation | $\text{Mode} = 3 \cdot \text{Median} - 2 \cdot \text{Mean}$ | Skewed unimodal data |
| 9 | Geometric Mean | $GM = \left(\prod x_i\right)^{1/n}$ | Ratios, S/N ratios |
| 10 | Harmonic Mean | $HM = \dfrac{n}{\sum (1/x_i)}$ | Rates, speeds |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in DOE:** In a $2^k$ factorial design, the response mean is the *grand average* of all $2^k$ treatment combinations, and the treatment effect is computed as the difference between level means. Mastery of central tendency is therefore non-negotiable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Algebraic Properties of Arithmetic Mean

### 3.1.1 Property 1: Sum of Deviations is Zero

By definition:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $n$:

$$
n \bar{x} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - n \bar{x} = 0
$$

Factoring the LHS as a sum of deviations $d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0
$$

**Interpretation:** The deviations of observations *above* the mean exactly balance the deviations *below* the mean. This is the **"balance-point"** property of the mean ŌĆö it is the centre of mass of the data points when equal weights are assigned.

### 3.1.2 Property 2: Sum of Squared Deviations is Minimum

Let $\bar{x}$ be the arithmetic mean and let $a$ be any other constant (a candidate for the "centre"). Consider the sum of squared deviations about $a$:

$$
S(a) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - a)^2
$$

Expanding the square:

$$
S(a) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i^2 - 2 a x_i + a^2) = \sum x_i^2 - 2a \sum x_i + n a^2
$$

To minimise $S(a)$, differentiate with respect to $a$ and set the derivative to zero:

$$
\frac{d S(a)}{d a} = -2 \sum x_i + 2 n a = 0
$$

Solving for $a$:

$$
2 n a = 2 \sum x_i \quad \Longrightarrow \quad a = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \bar{x}
$$

The second derivative is $\dfrac{d^2 S(a)}{d a^2} = 2n > 0$, confirming that $a = \bar{x}$ is a **minimum**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** This is the **Least Squares Principle**, the foundation of all regression and ANOVA in DOE. The arithmetic mean minimises total squared error, making it the optimal estimator in normal-distribution-based experimental analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation: Grouped Mean via Change of Origin and Scale

When class marks $x_i$ are large or spread out, the **step-deviation method** is used to simplify calculation. Choose an arbitrary origin $A$ (typically a central class mark) and a class width $h$ (assumed uniform).

Define a transformed variable:

$$
u_i = \frac{x_i - A}{h}
$$

Then the mean in terms of $u$ is:

$$
\bar{u} = \frac{\sum f_i u_i}{\sum f_i}
$$

Recovering the original mean:

$$
\bar{x} = A + h \cdot \bar{u}
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Step 1: From $u_i = (x_i - A)/h$, we have $x_i = A + h u_i$.

Step 2: Substituting into the grouped mean formula:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i} = \frac{\sum f_i (A + h u_i)}{\sum f_i}
$$

Step 3: Split the sum:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{A \sum f_i + h \sum f_i u_i}{\sum f_i} = A + h \cdot \frac{\sum f_i u_i}{\sum f_i}
$$

Step 4: Recognising the second term as $\bar{u}$:

$$
\bar{x} = A + h \bar{u}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is directly testable in KTU 2024. It is also the basis of the **coding technique** used in $2^k$ factorial design to compute effect sums of squares.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem (Full Solution)

### Problem: Compute Mean, Median, and Mode

The marks obtained by 10 students in a KTU DOE course are:

$$
\{ 45, 52, 60, 60, 65, 70, 70, 70, 80, 95 \}
$$

### 3.3.1 Compute the Arithmetic Mean

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{45 + 52 + 60 + 60 + 65 + 70 + 70 + 70 + 80 + 95}{10}
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{667}{10} = 66.7
$$

**Verification of Property 1:** Sum of deviations must be zero.

$$
(45 - 66.7) + (52 - 66.7) + 2(60 - 66.7) + (65 - 66.7) + 3(70 - 66.7) + (80 - 66.7) + (95 - 66.7)
$$

$$
= -21.7 - 14.7 - 13.4 - 1.7 + 9.9 + 13.3 + 28.3 = 0.0
$$

### 3.3.2 Compute the Median

Since $n = 10$ (even), median is the average of the 5th and 6th values.

Sorted data: $45, 52, 60, 60, \mathbf{65}, \mathbf{70}, 70, 70, 80, 95$.

$$
\text{Median} = \frac{65 + 70}{2} = 67.5
$$

### 3.3.3 Compute the Mode

The value **70** appears three times ŌĆö the highest frequency.

$$
\text{Mode} = 70
$$

### 3.3.4 Verification of Empirical Relation

$$
\text{Mean} - \text{Mode} = 66.7 - 70 = -3.3
$$

$$
3 (\text{Mean} - \text{Median}) = 3(66.7 - 67.5) = 3(-0.8) = -2.4
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The empirical relation is only **approximate**; small deviations (like $-3.3$ vs $-2.4$ here) are expected for real data. The relation becomes **exact** for a perfect Pearsonian (moderately skewed) distribution.

## 3.4 Worked Grouped Data Problem

### Problem: From the Frequency Distribution Below, Compute Mean, Median, and Mode.

| Class Interval | Frequency $f_i$ |
|----------------|-----------------|
| 0 ŌĆō 10 | 4 |
| 10 ŌĆō 20 | 8 |
| 20 ŌĆō 30 | 12 |
| 30 ŌĆō 40 | 10 |
| 40 ŌĆō 50 | 6 |
| **Total** | **40** |

### 3.4.1 Grouped Mean

| Class | $f_i$ | $x_i$ (mid-point) | $f_i x_i$ |
|-------|-------|--------------------|------------|
| 0ŌĆō10 | 4 | 5 | 20 |
| 10ŌĆō20 | 8 | 15 | 120 |
| 20ŌĆō30 | 12 | 25 | 300 |
| 30ŌĆō40 | 10 | 35 | 350 |
| 40ŌĆō50 | 6 | 45 | 270 |
| **Sum** | **40** | | **1060** |

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1060}{40} = 26.5
$$

### 3.4.2 Grouped Median

Cumulative frequencies: $4, 12, 24, 34, 40$.

$N/2 = 20$. The first cumulative frequency $\geq 20$ is $24$ ŌĆö the **median class is 20ŌĆō30**.

- $L = 20$ (lower boundary of median class)
- $F = 12$ (cumulative frequency of class before median class)
- $f = 12$ (frequency of median class)
- $h = 10$ (class width)

$$
\text{Median} = 20 + \left( \frac{20 - 12}{12} \right) \times 10 = 20 + \frac{8}{12} \times 10 = 20 + 6.667 = 26.667
$$

### 3.4.3 Grouped Mode

Modal class is **20ŌĆō30** (highest frequency = 12).

- $L = 20$
- $f_1 = 12$ (modal class frequency)
- $f_0 = 8$ (preceding class)
- $f_2 = 10$ (succeeding class)
- $h = 10$

$$
\text{Mode} = 20 + \left( \frac{12 - 8}{2(12) - 8 - 10} \right) \times 10
$$

$$
\text{Mode} = 20 + \left( \frac{4}{24 - 18} \right) \times 10 = 20 + \frac{4}{6} \times 10 = 20 + 6.667 = 26.667
$$

### 3.4.4 Empirical Check

$$
3 \times \text{Median} - 2 \times \text{Mean} = 3(26.667) - 2(26.5) = 80.0 - 53.0 = 27.0
$$

Computed Mode $= 26.667$, close to the empirical estimate of $27.0$ ŌĆö a satisfactory match.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Measures of Central Tendency

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["MEASURES OF CENTRAL TENDENCY"] --> B["ARITHMETIC MEAN"]
    A --> C["MEDIAN"]
    A --> D["MODE"]
    A --> E["OTHER MEANS"]

    B --> B1["Simple AM Ungrouped Data"]
    B --> B2["Weighted AM"]
    B --> B3["Grouped AM Frequency Data"]
    B --> B4["Combined AM Multiple Groups"]

    C --> C1["Ungrouped Median Odd n"]
    C --> C2["Ungrouped Median Even n"]
    C --> C3["Grouped Median Cumulative Frequency"]

    D --> D1["Ungrouped Mode Most Frequent Value"]
    D --> D2["Grouped Mode Modal Class Formula"]

    E --> E1["Geometric Mean GM"]
    E --> E2["Harmonic Mean HM"]
    E --> E3["Quadratic Mean RMS"]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow: Which Measure to Use

```mermaid
graph TD
    S["START Data Set"] --> Q1{"Data Type"}
    Q1 -- Categorical Nominal --> M1["Use MODE"]
    Q1 -- Ordinal --> M2["Use MEDIAN"]
    Q1 -- Numerical Continuous --> Q2{"Distribution Shape"}
    Q2 -- Symmetric No Outliers --> M3["Use ARITHMETIC MEAN"]
    Q2 -- Skewed or Outliers Present --> M4["Use MEDIAN"]
    Q3{"Need Geometric Progression Average"} -- Yes --> M5["Use GEOMETRIC MEAN"]
    Q3 -- No --> M4
    Q3{"Need Average Rate"} -- Yes --> M6["Use HARMONIC MEAN"]
    Q3 -- No --> M4
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (DOE Context)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["EXPERIMENTAL RUNS"] --> B["REPLICATE OBSERVATIONS Y1 Y2 ... Yn"]
    B --> C["COMPUTE CENTRAL TENDENCY"]
    C --> D1["Arithmetic Mean GRAND AVERAGE"]
    C --> D2["Median"]
    C --> D3["Mode"]
    D1 --> E["ANOVA TABLE"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> F["QUALITY CONTROL CHARTS"]
    E --> G["FACTOR EFFECT ESTIMATION"]
    F --> H["PROCESS MONITORING"]
    G --> I["OPTIMAL FACTOR LEVELS"]
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In a DOE workflow, the **arithmetic mean** of replicates becomes the treatment response; **medians** are used in non-parametric ANOVA (Kruskal-Wallis) for skewed residuals; **modes** are tracked in control charts to identify the most common defect type.

---
