# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]

**Define the arithmetic mean. State any two of its important properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** The arithmetic mean of $n$ observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ is the sum of all observations divided by the number of observations:
> 
> $$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$
> 
> **Properties (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
> 
> 1. **Rigidly defined:** The arithmetic mean is a unique value for a given data set ŌĆö there is no ambiguity.
> 2. **Uses all observations:** It is computed from every data point, making it a comprehensive representative.
> 3. **Sum of deviations is zero:** $\sum (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$.
> 4. **Least squares property:** $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ is minimum.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

**Distinguish between mean, median, and mode. Under what condition is the median preferred over the mean?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mean | Median | Mode |
|--------|------|--------|------|
| Definition | Sum/n | Middle value | Most frequent value |
| Data Type | Numerical | Ordinal/Interval | Categorical/Discrete |
| Outlier Effect | Highly affected | Not affected | Not affected |
| Computation | Requires all values | Position-based | Frequency-based |

**Condition for median preference (1 mark):**
The median is preferred when the data is **skewed** (asymmetric) or contains **extreme outliers**, because the mean gets pulled toward the tail. Example: Income distribution where a few billionaires inflate the mean ŌĆö the median is more representative.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Pattern:** Answer **any one full question** from the choice. Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split across sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Adapted]

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

The following data represent the tensile strength (in MPa) of 12 steel specimens tested in a DOE study:

$$
\{ 410, 425, 430, 435, 440, 445, 450, 455, 460, 470, 485, 495 \}
$$

**Compute the arithmetic mean, median, and mode. Also verify the empirical relationship $\text{Mode} = 3 \cdot \text{Median} - 2 \cdot \text{Mean}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Arithmetic Mean (2 marks)**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{410 + 425 + 430 + 435 + 440 + 445 + 450 + 455 + 460 + 470 + 485 + 495}{12}
$$

Sum:

$$
410 + 425 = 835,\; 835 + 430 = 1265,\; 1265 + 435 = 1700,\; 1700 + 440 = 2140
$$

$$
2140 + 445 = 2585,\; 2585 + 450 = 3035,\; 3035 + 455 = 3490,\; 3490 + 460 = 3950
$$

$$
3950 + 470 = 4420,\; 4420 + 485 = 4905,\; 4905 + 495 = 5400
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{5400}{12} = 450 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Median (2 marks)**

$n = 12$ (even). Median is the average of the 6th and 7th values.

Sorted data: 410, 425, 430, 435, 440, **445**, **450**, 455, 460, 470, 485, 495.

$$
\text{Median} = \frac{445 + 450}{2} = 447.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Mode (1 mark)**

All values appear exactly once ŌĆö the data is **uniformly distributed with no mode** (or all values are modes; technically a multimodal distribution with $k = 12$ modes). For a continuous experimental variable like tensile strength, mode is rarely meaningful.

**Step 4: Empirical Relation Verification (2 marks)**

$$
3 \times \text{Median} - 2 \times \text{Mean} = 3(447.5) - 2(450) = 1342.5 - 900 = 442.5
$$

The empirical formula predicts Mode $\approx 442.5$ MPa, but the actual data has no unique mode. The discrepancy illustrates that the empirical relation holds only for **moderately skewed, unimodal** data ŌĆö not for symmetric or uniform data.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Define a weighted arithmetic mean. In a $2^3$ factorial experiment, three replicates of a treatment yield the responses 45, 48, 51. The weight assigned to this treatment is 3. Compute the weighted contribution of this treatment to the grand mean, given that the sum of all weights is 24.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition (2 marks)**

The weighted arithmetic mean assigns different levels of importance to observations:

$$
\bar{x}_w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}
$$

where $w_i$ is the weight of the $i$-th observation.

**Step 2: Treatment Average (2 marks)**

$$
\bar{x}_{\text{trt}} = \frac{45 + 48 + 51}{3} = \frac{144}{3} = 48
$$

**Step 3: Weighted Contribution (3 marks)**

Contribution of this treatment to the weighted grand mean:

$$
C = w \cdot \bar{x}_{\text{trt}} = 3 \times 48 = 144
$$

Grand weighted mean:

$$
\bar{x}_w = \frac{C}{\sum w_i} = \frac{144}{24} = 6
$$

> The treatment's contribution $C = 144$ represents its weighted share in the numerator of the grand mean.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**The following grouped data represents the lifetime (in hours) of 50 electric bulbs tested during a reliability experiment. Compute the arithmetic mean using:**
**(i) Direct method**
**(ii) Step-deviation method**
**Verify that both methods yield the same result.**

| Lifetime (hours) | Number of Bulbs $f_i$ |
|------------------|------------------------|
| 300 ŌĆō 400 | 6 |
| 400 ŌĆō 500 | 12 |
| 500 ŌĆō 600 | 18 |
| 600 ŌĆō 700 | 10 |
| 700 ŌĆō 800 | 4 |
| **Total** | **50** |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Construct the computation table (3 marks)**

| Class | $f_i$ | $x_i$ (mid-point) | $f_i x_i$ | $u_i = (x_i - 550)/100$ | $f_i u_i$ |
|-------|-------|--------------------|-----------|--------------------------|-----------|
| 300ŌĆō400 | 6 | 350 | 2100 | -2 | -12 |
| 400ŌĆō500 | 12 | 450 | 5400 | -1 | -12 |
| 500ŌĆō600 | 18 | 550 | 9900 | 0 | 0 |
| 600ŌĆō700 | 10 | 650 | 6500 | 1 | 10 |
| 700ŌĆō800 | 4 | 750 | 3000 | 2 | 8 |
| **Sum** | **50** | | **26900** | | **-6** |

**Step 2: Direct Method (2 marks)**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i} = \frac{26900}{50} = 538 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3: Step-Deviation Method (2 marks)**

With $A = 550$ (chosen as a central value) and $h = 100$:

$$
\bar{u} = \frac{\sum f_i u_i}{\sum f_i} = \frac{-6}{50} = -0.12
$$

$$
\bar{x} = A + h \bar{u} = 550 + 100(-0.12) = 550 - 12 = 538 \text{ hours}
$$

**Verification:** Both methods give $\bar{x} = 538$ hours. Ō£ō

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**From the same data, compute the median and mode. Comment on the skewness of the distribution.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Cumulative Frequencies (1 mark)**

Cumulative frequencies: $6, 18, 36, 46, 50$.

$N/2 = 25$. First $cf \geq 25$ is $36$ ŌĆö median class is **500ŌĆō600**.

**Step 2: Median (3 marks)**

- $L = 500$
- $F = 18$ (cumulative frequency before median class)
- $f = 18$ (median class frequency)
- $h = 100$

$$
\text{Median} = 500 + \left(\frac{25 - 18}{18}\right) \times 100 = 500 + \frac{7}{18} \times 100 = 500 + 38.89 = 538.89 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3: Mode (2 marks)**

Modal class is **500ŌĆō600** (highest frequency = 18).

- $L = 500$
- $f_1 = 18, \; f_0 = 12, \; f_2 = 10$
- $h = 100$

$$
\text{Mode} = 500 + \left( \frac{18 - 12}{2(18) - 12 - 10} \right) \times 100 = 500 + \frac{6}{14} \times 100 = 500 + 42.86 = 542.86 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4: Skewness Comment (1 mark)**

Mean $= 538 <$ Median $= 538.89 <$ Mode $= 542.86$.

Since **Mean < Median < Mode**, the distribution is **negatively skewed (left-skewed)** ŌĆö the tail extends toward lower values. The Pearsonian coefficient:

$$
Sk = \frac{\text{Mean} - \text{Mode}}{\text{Standard Deviation}} < 0 \quad \text{(skewness is negative)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Measure of Central Tendency:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing class boundary with class limit:** For grouped median and mode, students often use the *lower class limit* (e.g., 500) instead of the *lower class boundary* (e.g., 500 itself for inclusive classes, but 499.5 for exclusive continuous classes). **Marks lost: 1ŌĆō2 per question.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to sort data for median:** Median of ungrouped data is computed only on **sorted** data. Computing it on unsorted data is a common mistake.
> 
> 3. **Wrong median class identification:** Median class is the **first** class with $cf \geq N/2$, not the class containing the $N/2$-th observation. The two definitions coincide, but students must use cumulative frequency correctly.
> 
> 4. **Empirical relation misuse:** The formula $\text{Mode} = 3 \cdot \text{Median} - 2 \cdot \text{Mean}$ is **empirical and approximate**. It should not be used to "verify" a wrong answer ŌĆö it is itself an estimate.
> 
> 5. **Missing units:** Always report the unit (MPa, hours, etc.). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 
> 6. **Step-deviation errors:** Forgetting to convert back via $\bar{x} = A + h \bar{u}$ is a critical error. The transformed mean $\bar{u}$ is **not** the actual mean.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Measure of Central Tendency**

### Quick-Reference Bullet Summary

- **Central tendency** = single value representing the centre of a data distribution.
- **Three classical measures:** Mean (AM), Median, Mode.
- **Arithmetic Mean** $\bar{x} = \sum x_i / n$ ŌĆö most common, uses all data, affected by outliers.
- **Weighted Mean** $\bar{x}_w = \sum w_i x_i / \sum w_i$ ŌĆö used when observations have unequal importance (e.g., unequal replication in DOE).
- **Median** = middle value after sorting; robust to outliers; preferred for **skewed** data.
- **Median formula (ungrouped, even $n$):** average of $n/2$-th and $(n/2+1)$-th values.
- **Median formula (grouped):** $L + \left(\dfrac{N/2 - F}{f}\right) h$.
- **Mode** = most frequent value; useful for categorical data; may be **non-unique** (bimodal/multimodal).
- **Mode formula (grouped):** $L + \left(\dfrac{f_1 - f_0}{2 f_1 - f_0 - f_2}\right) h$.
- **Empirical relationship:** $\text{Mode} = 3 \times \text{Median} - 2 \times \text{Mean}$ (for moderately skewed, unimodal data).
- **Geometric Mean:** $GM = (\prod x_i)^{1/n}$ ŌĆö used for ratios and S/N ratios in Taguchi DOE.
- **Harmonic Mean:** $HM = n / \sum (1/x_i)$ ŌĆö used for rates.
- **AM-GM-HM inequality:** $HM \leq GM \leq AM \leq RMS$ (for positive numbers).
- **Key property of mean:** $\sum (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$ and $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ is **minimum** (least-squares principle).
- **Step-deviation transformation:** $u_i = (x_i - A)/h \implies \bar{x} = A + h \bar{u}$ ŌĆö reduces computational effort in grouped data.
- **Skewness interpretation:** If Mean < Median < Mode ŌåÆ **negatively skewed**; if Mean > Median > Mode ŌåÆ **positively skewed**.
- **In DOE:** Treatment means are computed across replicates; the **grand mean** is the average of all observations, and **factor effects** are differences of level means.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Mantra:** *"Central tendency measures the centre; dispersion measures the spread. Together they form the mean-square-error framework of every experimental design."* ŌĆö Master these before moving to Module 2 (ANOVA and Test Statistics).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling and Sampling Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Sampling and Sampling Distribution # SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Sampling: The Mathematical Foundation of Inference > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)** > **Sampling** is the statistical process of selecting a finite subset of individuals, items, or observations (the *sample*) from a larger group (the *population*) with the objective of estimating properties of the entire population without conducting an exhaustive census. In the language of **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, every measurement we make is a *sample observation*. The experimentalist never has access to the entire population of possible experimental outcomes, so they must *infer* characteristics of the population from a finite, randomly drawn collection of data points. ### 1.2 Population vs. Sample ŌĆö The Foundational Distinction | Term | Symbol | Description | Example (Industrial Context) | |---|---|---|---| | **Population** | $N$ | The entire set of all possible measurements or units of interest | All 50,000 ball-bearings manufactured in a month | | **Sample** | $n$ | A finite subset drawn from the population | 100 ball-bearings randomly inspected | | **Parameter** | $\mu, \sigma^2, p$ | A *true* numerical descriptor of the population (fixed but unknown) | True mean tensile strength $\mu = 520 \text{ MPa}$ | | **Statistic** | $\bar{X}, S^2, \hat{p}$ | A numerical descriptor computed *from the sample* (random variable) | Sample mean $\bar{x} = 515 \text{ MPa}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Paradigm Shift:** A *parameter* is a **constant** (even though unknown), while a *statistic* is a **random variable** whose value changes from sample to sample. This distinction is the bedrock of all sampling theory. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Soup Tasting" Intuition Imagine a chef preparing **500 liters of tomato soup** in an industrial kettle. To verify seasoning, the chef cannot taste the entire kettle. Instead, they stir thoroughly and draw **10 small ladles** from different depths and locations. - The **entire kettle of soup** = Population $N = 500$ L - The **10 ladle-spoons combined** = Sample $n = 10 \times 50 \text{ mL} = 500 \text{ mL}$ - The **true saltiness of the kettle** = Parameter $\mu$ (unknown) - The **average saltiness of the 10 ladles** = Statistic $\bar{X}$ (varies if repeated) Just as stirring randomizes the soup so each ladle is representative, **randomization in sampling** ensures each subset has an equal chance of reflecting the population. ### 1.4 Why Sampling Distribution Matters A single sample gives one estimate. But if we were to **draw infinitely many samples** and plot the values of the statistic (say, $\bar{X}$) from each, we obtain a *probability distribution* ŌĆö this is the **Sampling Distribution**. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition** > The **Sampling Distribution of a Statistic** is the probability distribution of that statistic computed from all possible samples of size $n$ drawn from the same population. It describes how the statistic *varies* across repeated sampling. This concept is the *bridge* between descriptive statistics (the sample we have) and inferential statistics (the conclusions we draw about the population). # SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Sampling Concepts The DOE framework treats sampling theory in a layered structure. Master this hierarchy before tackling any numerical problem: 1. **Population Identification** ŌĆö What is $N$? What are we trying to estimate? 2. **Sampling Technique Selection** ŌĆö How is $n$ drawn from $N$? 3. **Statistic Computation** ŌĆö Calculate $\bar{X}$, $S^2$, $\hat{p}$ from the drawn $n$. 4. **Sampling Distribution Characterization** ŌĆö What is the distribution of the statistic across repeated sampling? 5. **Standard Error Evaluation** ŌĆö Quantify the variability of the statistic. 6. **Confidence Interval / Hypothesis Test** ŌĆö Use the sampling distribution to make inferences. ## 2.2 Probability vs. Non-Probability Sampling Techniques ### A. Probability Sampling (Each unit has a *known, non-zero* chance of selection) - **Simple Random Sampling (SRS):** Every possible sample of size $n$ has an equal probability of being chosen. Used when the population is **homogeneous**. - **Stratified Sampling:** Population divided into *strata* (homogeneous sub-groups); random sample drawn from each stratum. Used when the population is **heterogeneous**. - **Systematic Sampling:** Every $k^{th}$ unit is selected after a random start. Used for **ordered populations** (assembly lines). - **Cluster Sampling:** Population divided into *clusters* (heterogeneous groups); entire clusters are randomly selected. Used for **geographically dispersed** populations. ### B. Non-Probability Sampling (Selection based on judgment or convenience) - **Convenience Sampling:** Selecting units that are easiest to reach. - **Judgmental / Purposive Sampling:** Researcher uses expertise to select "typical" units. - **Snowball Sampling:** Existing subjects recruit future subjects (used in rare-population studies). > [!WARNING] > Non-probability sampling **cannot** support valid probability-based inference. KTU exam questions almost always assume **probability sampling**. ## 2.3 The Big Three Sampling Distributions In Module 1, the focus is on three pivotal distributions of statistics: ### 2.3.1 Sampling Distribution of the Mean ($\bar{X}$) When sampling from a population with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$: $$\text{Mean of } \bar{X}: \quad E(\bar{X}) = \mu$$ $$\text{Standard Error of } \bar{X}: \quad \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$ For **finite populations** with sampling fraction $n/N > 0.05$, the **Finite Population Correction (FPC)** factor applies: $$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{N - n}{N - 1}}$$ ### 2.3.2 Sampling Distribution of the Proportion ($\hat{p}$) When sampling from a Bernoulli population with true proportion $p$: $$\text{Mean of } \hat{p}: \quad E(\hat{p}) = p$$ $$\text{Standard Error of } \hat{p}: \quad \sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$ ### 2.3.3 Sampling Distribution of the Difference ($\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2$) For two independent samples of sizes $n_1$ and $n_2$: $$\sigma_{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is your high-yield reference for every numerical problem in Module 1.** | # | Concept | Formula | Key Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Mean of sample mean | $E(\bar{X}) = \mu$ | Always (unbiased estimator) | | 2 | Standard error of mean (infinite pop.) | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma \div \sqrt{n}$ | $\sigma$ known | | 3 | Standard error of mean (finite pop.) | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = (\sigma \div \sqrt{n}) \cdot \sqrt{(N-n)\div(N-1)}$ | $n/N > 0.05$ | | 4 | Sample variance (unbiased) | $S^2 = \sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \div (n-1)$ | Use $n-1$ denominator | | 5 | Mean of sample variance | $E(S^2) = \sigma^2$ | Always unbiased | | 6 | Mean of sample proportion | $E(\hat{p}) = p$ | Always | | 7 | Standard error of proportion | $\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{p(1-p)\div n}$ | $np \geq 5$, $n(1-p) \geq 5$ | | 8 | SE of difference of means | $\sigma_{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2\div n_1 + \sigma_2^2\div n_2}$ | Independent samples | | 9 | Z-statistic (large sample) | $Z = (\bar{X} - \mu) \div (\sigma \div \sqrt{n})$ | $n \geq 30$ | | 10 | t-statistic (small sample) | $t = (\bar{X} - \mu) \div (S \div \sqrt{n})$ | $n < 30$, $\sigma$ unknown | | 11 | CLT approximation | $\bar{X} \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2 \div n)$ | $n \geq 30$ | | 12 | Required sample size (mean) | $n = (Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma \div E)^2$ | $E$ = margin of error | | 13 | Required sample size (proportion) | $n = Z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot p(1-p) \div E^2$ | Prior $p$ estimate | | 14 | Required sample size (diff. of means) | $n = (Z_{\alpha/2})^2 (\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2) \div E^2$ | Equal $n_1 = n_2 = n$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Notation Alert:** In KTU exam scripts, $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ is also written as $\sigma_{\bar{x}}$ or $\text{SE}(\bar{X})$ ŌĆö all three notations refer to the **Standard Error of the Sample Mean**. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The sampling distribution underpins virtually every modern quality-control decision: - **Six-Sigma Manufacturing:** Process capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) rely on $\bar{X}$ distributions to determine defect rates per million opportunities. - **Clinical Trials (Pharmaceutical):** Comparing mean recovery times between a drug and placebo group uses the SE of the difference of means. - **Load Testing in Civil Engineering:** Sampling distribution of concrete compressive strength allows engineers to predict the probability that a batch meets design specifications (e.g., M25 grade). - **A/B Testing in Software:** Sampling distribution of conversion-rate differences validates which UI design performs better. ## 2.6 Central Limit Theorem (CLT) ŌĆö The Crown Jewel > [!NOTE] > **Theorem (CLT)** > If $\bar{X}$ is the sample mean of a random sample of size $n$ drawn from *any* population with finite mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$, then as $n \to \infty$, the standardized variable > $$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$ > converges in distribution to the **standard normal** $N(0, 1)$. **Practical Rule:** The CLT approximation is considered acceptable for engineering applications when $n \geq 30$, even if the parent population is skewed or non-normal. For $n < 30$ and unknown $\sigma$, we instead use **Student's t-distribution** with $\nu = n - 1$ degrees of freedom. # SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean and Variance of the Sample Mean ($\bar{X}$) ### Setup Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be a random sample of size $n$ drawn independently from a population with: $$E(X_i) = \mu, \quad \text{Var}(X_i) = \sigma^2 \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$ The sample mean is defined as: $$\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$$ ### Step 1 ŌĆö Derive the Expected Value of $\bar{X}$ We begin by applying the **linearity of expectation** (the most powerful expectation identity): $$\begin{aligned} E(\bar{X}) &= E\left[\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] \\ &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i) \quad \text{(linearity of expectation)} \end{aligned}$$ Since every $X_i$ has the same expectation $E(X_i) = \mu$: $$\begin{aligned} E(\bar{X}) &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu \\ &= \frac{1}{n} \cdot n \cdot \mu \\ &= \mu \end{aligned}$$ This proves that $\bar{X}$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $\mu$. ### Step 2 ŌĆö Derive the Variance of $\bar{X}$ Because the $X_i$ are *independent*, we use the **additivity of variance for independent random variables**: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Var}(\bar{X}) &= \text{Var}\left[\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] \\ &= \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i) \quad \text{(scaling property + independence)} \end{aligned}$$ Since each variance equals $\sigma^2$: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Var}(\bar{X}) &= \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma^2 \\ &= \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n \cdot \sigma^2 \\ &= \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \end{aligned}$$ Taking the square root gives the **Standard Error**: $$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sqrt{\text{Var}(\bar{X})} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$ ### Interpretation The variance of $\bar{X}$ shrinks as $n$ increases. Specifically, to **halve** the standard error, we must **quadruple** the sample size ŌĆö a fundamental trade-off in experimental design. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Finite Population Correction (FPC) Factor ### Setup When sampling **without replacement** from a finite population of size $N$, the draws are no longer independent. The covariance between any two distinct draws is: $$\text{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = -\frac{\sigma^2}{N - 1} \quad \text{for } i \neq j$$ ### Step 1 ŌĆö Recompute the Variance with Dependence $$\begin{aligned} \text{Var}(\bar{X}) &= \frac{1}{n^2} \text{Var}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] \\ &= \frac{1}{n^2}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i) + 2 \sum_{i [!IMPORTANT] > When $n \ll N$ (sampling fraction $< 5\%$), the term $(N-n)/(N-1) \approx 1$ and the FPC factor can be safely ignored. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Standard Error of the Sample Proportion ($\hat{p}$) ### Setup Let $X_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$, so $X_i = 1$ if the unit has the attribute and $0$ otherwise. The sample proportion is: $$\hat{p} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$$ ### Step 1 ŌĆö Population Parameters of Bernoulli $$\begin{aligned} E(X_i) &= p \\ \text{Var}(X_i) &= p(1-p) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Sampling Distribution of the Mean Formula Substituting $\mu = p$ and $\sigma^2 = p(1-p)$ into the earlier derivation: $$\begin{aligned} E(\hat{p}) &= p \\ \text{Var}(\hat{p}) &= \frac{p(1-p)}{n} \\ \sigma_{\hat{p}} &= \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 ŌĆö Normal Approximation (CLT for Proportions) For large $n$, $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal: $$Z = \frac{\hat{p} - p}{\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}} \sim N(0, 1)$$ Validity requires $np \geq 5$ and $n(1-p) \geq 5$ (rule of thumb). --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A process engineer suspects the mean diameter of turned shafts is $25.00$ mm. A random sample of $n = 36$ shafts yields $\bar{x} = 24.85$ mm with known $\sigma = 0.60$ mm. Compute the standard error, the $Z$-statistic, and the two-sided $p$-value. ### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Standard Error $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{\bar{X}} &= \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \\ &= \frac{0.60}{\sqrt{36}} \\ &= \frac{0.60}{6} \\ &= 0.10 \text{ mm} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the Z-Statistic $$\begin{aligned} Z &= \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma_{\bar{X}}} \\ &= \frac{24.85 - 25.00}{0.10} \\ &= \frac{-0.15}{0.10} \\ &= -1.50 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Two-Sided $p$-Value Using the standard normal table: $$\begin{aligned} P(Z \leq -1.50) &= 0.0668 \\ p\text{-value} &= 2 \times 0.0668 = 0.1336 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 ŌĆö Decision At $\alpha = 0.05$, since $0.1336 > 0.05$, we **fail to reject** $H_0: \mu = 25.00$ mm. The observed deviation is statistically consistent with random sampling variation. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | $\bar{X}$ (Mean) | $\hat{p}$ (Proportion) | |---|---|---| | Underlying distribution of single obs. | Continuous (e.g., Normal, Exponential) | Bernoulli (Binary) | | Population parameter | $\mu$ (continuous) | $p$ (between 0 and 1) | | Estimator formula | $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i$ | $\hat{p} = \frac{X}{n}$ (where $X$ = # successes) | | Standard error | $\sigma \div \sqrt{n}$ | $\sqrt{p(1-p)\div n}$ | | CLT requirement | $n \geq 30$ | $np \geq 5$ and $n(1-p) \geq 5$ | | When $\sigma$ unknown | Use $t$-distribution, $\nu = n-1$ | Use $\hat{p}$ in SE formula | # SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart: From Population to Inference ```mermaid flowchart TD A["POPULATION N
True Parameters mu sigma p"] --> B["SAMPLING TECHNIQUE
SRS Stratified Systematic Cluster"] B --> C["SAMPLE n
Observations x1 x2 xn"] C --> D["SAMPLE STATISTICS
bar X S squared hat p"] D --> E["SAMPLING DISTRIBUTION
Distribution of Statistic over all samples"] E --> F["STANDARD ERROR
Variability of Statistic"] F --> G["INFERENCE
Confidence Interval Hypothesis Test"] G --> H["POPULATION CONCLUSION
Estimate mu sigma p"] H -.->|"Feeds back"| A ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Sampling Distribution Construction ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph P1["Population Universe mu sigma squared"] U1["Unit 1"] U2["Unit 2"] U3["Unit 3"] U4["Unit N"] end subgraph S1["Sample 1 n units"] X11["bar X1"] end subgraph S2["Sample 2 n units"] X22["bar X2"] end subgraph S3["Sample k n units"] X33["bar Xk"] end U1 --> S1 U2 --> S2 U3 --> S3 U4 --> S1 S1 --> D["Sampling Distribution of bar X
Mean mu Variance sigma squared by n"] S2 --> D S3 --> D ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree: Which Sampling Distribution to Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Is sigma known?"} Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q2{"Is n greater than or equal 30?"} Q1 -->|"No"| Q3{"Is n greater than or equal 30?"} Q2 -->|"Yes"| R1["Use Z distribution
N 0 1"] Q2 -->|"No"| R2["Use Z distribution
approx valid by CLT"] Q3 -->|"Yes"| R3["Use Z with S
approx valid"] Q3 -->|"No"| R4["Use t distribution
nu equals n minus 1"] R1 --> Q4{"Population finite and n/N greater than 0.05?"} R2 --> Q4 R3 --> Q4 R4 --> Q4 Q4 -->|"Yes"| R5["Apply FPC
sqrt N minus n over N minus 1"] Q4 -->|"No"| R6["Ignore FPC
Use standard formula"] ``` ## 4.4 Sampling Techniques Comparison Map ```mermaid flowchart TD SP["SAMPLING TECHNIQUES"] --> PS["PROBABILITY SAMPLING"] SP --> NPS["NON PROBABILITY SAMPLING"] PS --> SRS["Simple Random Sampling
Homogeneous Population"] PS --> ST["Stratified Sampling
Heterogeneous Population with Subgroups"] PS --> SY["Systematic Sampling
Ordered Population"] PS --> CL["Cluster Sampling
Geographically Dispersed"] NPS --> CO["Convenience Sampling"] NPS --> JU["Judgmental Sampling"] NPS --> SN["Snowball Sampling"] SRS --> USE["Use When Randomization Possible"] ST --> USE SY --> USE CL --> USE ``` ## 4.5 Analytical Architecture: How the Sampling Distribution Links to DOE ```mermaid flowchart TD DOE["DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS"] --> FM["Factor Matrix"] DOE --> RA["Randomization"] DOE --> RE["Replication"] FM --> SD1["Sampling Distribution of Treatment Means"] RA --> SD2["Sampling Distribution of Error"] RE --> SD3["Sampling Distribution of Effect Estimates"] SD1 --> ANOVA["ANOVA Table"] SD2 --> ANOVA SD3 --> ANOVA ANOVA --> INF["Statistical Inference on Effects"] ``` # SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]` > **Define the term "Sampling Distribution." Why is it important in statistical inference?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **sampling distribution** is the probability distribution of a sample statistic (such as $\bar{X}$, $S^2$, or $\hat{p}$) computed from all possible samples of a given size $n$ drawn from the same population. It is important because: 1. It quantifies the **variability** of the statistic across repeated samples, captured by the **standard error**. 2. It enables the construction of **confidence intervals** and the conduct of **hypothesis tests** without needing access to the entire population. 3. It forms the **theoretical foundation** of all inferential statistics and experimental design, including ANOVA and regression. --- > **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]` > **State the Central Limit Theorem. What is its significance for the sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement:** If $\bar{X}$ is the sample mean of $n$ independent observations drawn from any population with finite mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$, then as $n \to \infty$: $$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} N(0, 1)$$ **Significance:** - The sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$ approaches **normality regardless of the parent population's shape**. - For $n \geq 30$, we can use the standard normal distribution to compute probabilities and critical values, even when the underlying distribution is skewed or unknown. - It enables the use of Z-tests and confidence intervals in practical DOE applications. --- ## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **PART B carries 14 marks with internal choice. Solve either Question A or Question B in full.** --- ### ŌØō Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the different **probability sampling techniques** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A random sample of size $n = 64$ is drawn from a population with mean $\mu = 80$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 16$. Find the probability that the sample mean $\bar{X}$ lies between 78 and 84. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution (a) The four main probability sampling techniques are: **1. Simple Random Sampling (SRS):** Every possible sample of size $n$ from the population of size $N$ has an equal probability of being selected. *Example:* Drawing 50 bolts randomly from a batch of 5,000 to test tensile strength. **2. Stratified Sampling:** The population is divided into mutually exclusive and exhaustive *strata* based on a known characteristic; a random sample is drawn from each stratum (proportionate or disproportionate). *Example:* Dividing a workforce into departments (R&D, Production, Sales) and sampling employees from each. **3. Systematic Sampling:** The population is ordered; a random starting point is chosen, and then every $k^{th}$ unit is selected, where $k = N/n$. *Example:* Selecting every 10th item from a conveyor belt for quality inspection. **4. Cluster Sampling:** The population is divided into *clusters* (often geographically), and entire clusters are randomly selected for study. *Example:* Randomly selecting 5 districts out of 14 in Kerala and surveying all households within them. #### Model Solution (b) **Given:** $n = 64$, $\mu = 80$, $\sigma = 16$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Standard Error:** $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{\bar{X}} &= \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \\ &= \frac{16}{\sqrt{64}} \\ &= \frac{16}{8} = 2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Standardize the Bounds:** $$\begin{aligned} Z_1 &= \frac{78 - 80}{2} = \frac{-2}{2} = -1.00 \\ Z_2 &= \frac{84 - 80}{2} = \frac{4}{2} = 2.00 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Probability:** $$\begin{aligned} P(78 < \bar{X} < 84) &= P(-1.00 < Z < 2.00) \\ &= \Phi(2.00) - \Phi(-1.00) \\ &= 0.9772 - 0.1587 \\ &= 0.8185 \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** The probability is approximately **0.8185** (81.85%). --- ### ŌØō Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]` **(a)** Derive the **mean and standard error of the sample proportion** $\hat{p}$. State the conditions under which the normal approximation is valid. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A manufacturer claims that only 5% of his products are defective. A random sample of 200 items is tested and 15 are found defective. Test at the 5% level of significance whether the manufacturer's claim is valid. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution (a) **Setup:** Let $X_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$ for $i = 1, \ldots, n$, where $X_i = 1$ if the unit is a success, else $0$. Then: $$\hat{p} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i = \frac{X}{n}$$ **Mean of $\hat{p}$:** Applying the linearity of expectation: $$\begin{aligned} E(\hat{p}) &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i) = \frac{1}{n} \cdot n \cdot p = p \end{aligned}$$ **Variance of $\hat{p}$:** Since the $X_i$ are independent, each with variance $p(1-p)$: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Var}(\hat{p}) &= \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i) = \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n \cdot p(1-p) = \frac{p(1-p)}{n} \end{aligned}$$ $$\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$ **Normal Approximation Validity Conditions:** - $np \geq 5$ (rule of thumb) - $n(1-p) \geq 5$ When satisfied, $Z = (\hat{p} - p)/\sigma_{\hat{p}} \sim N(0, 1)$. #### Model Solution (b) **Given:** $p_0 = 0.05$, $n = 200$, $x = 15$, $\alpha = 0.05$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Hypotheses:** $$H_0: p = 0.05 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: p \neq 0.05$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Sample Proportion:** $$\hat{p} = \frac{15}{200} = 0.075$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Standard Error Under $H_0$:** $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{\hat{p}} &= \sqrt{\frac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n}} \\ &= \sqrt{\frac{0.05 \times 0.95}{200}} \\ &= \sqrt{\frac{0.0475}{200}} \\ &= \sqrt{0.0002375} \\ &\approx 0.01541 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Test Statistic:** $$\begin{aligned} Z &= \frac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sigma_{\hat{p}}} \\ &= \frac{0.075 - 0.05}{0.01541} \\ &= \frac{0.025}{0.01541} \\ &\approx 1.622 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Critical Value & Decision:** For a two-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$: $Z_{\text{critical}} = \pm 1.96$. Since $\vert Z_{\text{calc}} \vert = 1.622 < 1.96$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**. **Conclusion:** At the 5% significance level, there is **insufficient evidence** to reject the manufacturer's claim that the defect rate is 5%. --- ## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Sampling Distribution Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting the FPC factor** when $n/N > 0.05$ ŌĆö examiners specifically test this. Marks lost: ~2. > 2. **Using $n$ instead of $n-1$** in the denominator of sample variance $S^2$ ŌĆö this is the #1 mistake in t-distribution problems. Marks lost: 1ŌĆō2. > 3. **Not stating CLT assumptions** before applying $Z$ ŌĆö always mention "by CLT, $\bar{X}$ is approximately normal for large $n$." Marks lost: 1. > 4. **Confusing $\sigma$ (population) with $S$ (sample)** ŌĆö explicitly state which is given/used. Marks lost: 1. > 5. **Two-tailed vs. one-tailed mismatch** in hypothesis testing ŌĆö read $H_1$ carefully before choosing critical value. Marks lost: 2. > 6. **Forgetting to check $np \geq 5$ and $n(1-p) \geq 5$** in proportion problems ŌĆö failure to validate leads to wrong normal approximation. Marks lost: 1. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **­¤ÜĆ Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 1: Sampling and Sampling Distribution** - **Population vs. Sample:** $N$ vs. $n$; **Parameter vs. Statistic**: fixed vs. random. - **Probability Sampling Methods:** SRS, Stratified, Systematic, Cluster ŌĆö each suited to different population structures. - **Non-Probability Sampling:** Convenience, Judgmental, Snowball ŌĆö *cannot* support formal inference. - **Mean of Sample Mean:** $E(\bar{X}) = \mu$ (unbiased). - **Standard Error of Mean:** $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$ (infinite population). - **Finite Population Correction:** Multiply SE by $\sqrt{(N-n)/(N-1)}$ when $n/N > 0.05$. - **Sample Variance:** Use divisor $n-1$ for unbiasedness; $E(S^2) = \sigma^2$. - **Sampling Distribution of Proportion:** $\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$; CLT valid when $np, n(1-p) \geq 5$. - **Central Limit Theorem:** $\bar{X} \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$ approximately for $n \geq 30$. - **Z-statistic:** $Z = (\bar{X} - \mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt{n})$ when $\sigma$ is known and $n \geq 30$. - **t-statistic:** $t = (\bar{X} - \mu)/(S/\sqrt{n})$ when $\sigma$ is unknown; degrees of freedom $\nu = n-1$. - **Difference of Two Means:** $\sigma_{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2/n_1 + \sigma_2^2/n_2}$. - **Sample Size Formula (mean):** $n = (Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma / E)^2$. - **Sample Size Formula (proportion):** $n = Z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot p(1-p) / E^2$. - **Standard Error Reduction:** To halve the SE, **quadruple** the sample size (inverse-square-root relationship).

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Statistical Distributions: Normal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 ŌĆö Statistical Distributions: Normal Distribution

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normal Distribution (Gaussian Distribution):** A continuous probability distribution characterized by its symmetric, bell-shaped probability density function. It is formally defined as a random variable $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ whose probability density function (PDF) is given by the **de MoivreŌĆōLaplaceŌĆōGauss** equation. In Design of Experiments (DOE), the Normal Distribution is the foundational assumption for the error term $\varepsilon \sim N(0,\sigma^2)$ in linear statistical models (ANOVA, regression, factorial designs).

**Mathematical Statement:**
A continuous random variable $X$ follows a Normal Distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$ if its PDF is:

$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right], \quad -\infty < x < \infty
$$

where $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ is the location (mean) parameter and $\sigma^2 > 0$ is the scale (variance) parameter.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö Plain English

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The "Bell Curve of Human Heights":**
> Imagine you measure the heights of every adult male in Kerala. If you plot the number of people against height, you get a bell-shaped curve: most men cluster around the average height (~170 cm), with very few being extremely short or extremely tall. This is the Normal Distribution in action. The **center of the bell** is the mean ($\mu$), and the **width of the bell** is controlled by the standard deviation ($\sigma$).

**Engineering Analogy for DOE:**
Think of every measurement in an experiment as a "true value" + "random noise". The noise itself behaves like a Normal Distribution centered at zero. This is why the bell curve is the heartbeat of experimental design ŌĆö we model *errors* as Normal because the Central Limit Theorem tells us that sums of small independent errors always look bell-shaped.

---

## 1.3 Why Normal Distribution is Central to DOE

- **Error assumption:** All classical DOE models (CRD, RBD, LSD, factorial) assume experimental errors $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.
- **Test statistics foundation:** The F-test (ANOVA), t-test, and $\chi^2$-test all derive their sampling distributions from the Normal Distribution.
- **Prediction & Confidence Intervals:** Construction of CIs for means and prediction intervals for new observations relies on Normality.
- **Residual Analysis:** Validity of any DOE model is verified by checking whether residuals are approximately Normal (QQ-plot, ShapiroŌĆōWilk test).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 1 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Properties of the Normal Distribution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Cardinal Properties (Must be memorized for KTU exams):**

1. **Symmetry:** The PDF is perfectly symmetric about the vertical line $x = \mu$. Therefore, $f(\mu + d) = f(\mu - d)$ for any $d$.
2. **Central Tendency Equality:** Mean = Median = Mode = $\mu$.
3. **Bell Shape:** Unimodal with a single peak at $x = \mu$; the curve has inflection points exactly at $x = \mu \pm \sigma$.
4. **Asymptotic Tails:** The curve approaches the horizontal axis asymptotically ŌĆö it never touches zero.
5. **Total Area = 1:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$ (it is a valid probability density).

---

## 2.2 The Standard Normal Distribution

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardization:** Any Normal random variable $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ can be transformed into a **Standard Normal** variable $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ using the linear transformation:
> $$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$
> This transformation is the **single most important operation** in applied statistics because it lets us use a single universal table (the Z-table) for *every* Normal curve.

The Standard Normal PDF simplifies to:

$$
\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{z^2}{2}\right), \quad z \in \mathbb{R}
$$

The corresponding CDF is denoted:

$$
\Phi(z) = P(Z \leq z) = \int_{-\infty}^{z} \phi(t)\,dt
$$

---

## 2.3 Empirical Rule (68ŌĆō95ŌĆō99.7 Rule)

This is the **highest-frequency KTU question** topic. Memorize it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical Rule for $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$:**
> - $P(\mu - \sigma \leq X \leq \mu + \sigma) \approx 0.6827$ (Ōēł **68.27%**)
> - $P(\mu - 2\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 2\sigma) \approx 0.9545$ (Ōēł **95.45%**)
> - $P(\mu - 3\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 0.9973$ (Ōēł **99.73%**)

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference ŌĆö Always carry these to the exam hall.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description / Meaning** | **Where Used in DOE** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | General Normal PDF | Random error modeling |
| 2 | $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-z^2/2}$ | Standard Normal PDF | Tabulated lookup |
| 3 | $Z = \dfrac{X-\mu}{\sigma}$ | Standardization transformation | Converting $X$ to $Z$ |
| 4 | $\Phi(z) = P(Z \leq z)$ | Standard Normal CDF | Area from $-\infty$ to $z$ |
| 5 | $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{b-\mu}{\sigma}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{a-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | Probability in interval $[a,b]$ | Tolerance limits |
| 6 | $\mu \pm 1\sigma \Rightarrow 68.27\%$ | Empirical Rule 1-$\sigma$ | Quality control |
| 7 | $\mu \pm 2\sigma \Rightarrow 95.45\%$ | Empirical Rule 2-$\sigma$ | Control charts |
| 8 | $\mu \pm 3\sigma \Rightarrow 99.73\%$ | Empirical Rule 3-$\sigma$ | Six-Sigma |
| 9 | $E[X] = \mu, \quad \mathrm{Var}(X) = \sigma^2$ | Moments of Normal | Model assumptions |
| 10 | If $X_i \sim N(\mu_i,\sigma_i^2)$ independent, then $\sum a_i X_i \sim N\!\left(\sum a_i\mu_i,\ \sum a_i^2\sigma_i^2\right)$ | Reproductive property | Linear combinations in DOE |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Six-Sigma quality programs rely on the $\mu \pm 6\sigma$ specification window to achieve 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO).
- **Process Control:** Control charts (X-bar, R-charts) use $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ as control limits under the assumption of Normality.
- **Tolerance Design:** Probability of a part falling within engineering tolerances is computed via the Normal CDF.
- **Measurement Systems (Gage R&R):** Repeatability and reproducibility variances are estimated assuming Normal measurement errors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 1 ŌĆö Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Standardization Transformation

**Claim:** If $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ follows $N(0, 1)$.

**Proof using Moment Generating Functions (MGF):**

The MGF of $X$ is well known:

$$
M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}] = \exp\!\left(\mu t + \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2 t^2\right)
$$

Now derive the MGF of $Z$:

$$
M_Z(t) = E\!\left[e^{tZ}\right] = E\!\left[e^{t(X-\mu)/\sigma}\right] = e^{-\mu t/\sigma}\, E\!\left[e^{(t/\sigma)X}\right]
$$

Recognize $E[e^{(t/\sigma)X}] = M_X(t/\sigma)$. Substitute:

$$
M_Z(t) = e^{-\mu t/\sigma}\,\exp\!\left(\mu \cdot \frac{t}{\sigma} + \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2\cdot\frac{t^2}{\sigma^2}\right)
$$

$$
= \exp\!\left(-\frac{\mu t}{\sigma} + \frac{\mu t}{\sigma} + \frac{1}{2}t^2\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{2}t^2\right)
$$

This is exactly the MGF of a Standard Normal $N(0, 1)$. Since MGFs uniquely identify distributions:

$$
\therefore \quad Z = \frac{X-\mu}{\sigma} \sim N(0,1) \qquad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Empirical Rule Application

**Problem:** The tensile strength of a steel wire is $X \sim N(500, 25^2)$ N. Find:
- (i) $P(X \leq 475)$
- (ii) $P(450 \leq X \leq 525)$

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**(i)** Standardize the value $x = 475$:

$$
z = \frac{475 - 500}{25} = \frac{-25}{25} = -1
$$

So:

$$
P(X \leq 475) = P(Z \leq -1) = \Phi(-1)
$$

By symmetry, $\Phi(-1) = 1 - \Phi(1) = 1 - 0.8413 = 0.1587$.

$$
\boxed{P(X \leq 475) \approx 0.1587 \;(\text{i.e., } 15.87\%)} \quad \text{(4 marks for full derivation)}
$$

**(ii)** Standardize both bounds:

$$
z_1 = \frac{450-500}{25} = -2, \qquad z_2 = \frac{525-500}{25} = +1
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(450 \leq X \leq 525) = \Phi(1) - \Phi(-2) = 0.8413 - 0.0228 = 0.8185
$$

$$
\boxed{P(450 \leq X \leq 525) \approx 0.8185 \;(\text{i.e., } 81.85\%)} \quad \text{(3 marks)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** Notice how the Empirical Rule gives a quick estimate: $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ contains 95.45% ŌĆö here we asked for $\mu - 2\sigma$ to $\mu + \sigma$, which must be slightly *less* than 95.45%, matching our answer of 81.85%.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Inverse Problem (Finding the Cut-off)

**Problem:** A battery has lifetime $X \sim N(40, 4^2)$ months. The manufacturer wants to set a warranty such that at most **5%** of batteries fail before the warranty expires. Find the warranty period $W$.

**Solution:**

We need $P(X \leq W) = 0.05$. From Z-tables, $\Phi(z) = 0.05 \Rightarrow z = -1.645$.

Apply standardization backwards:

$$
W = \mu + z\sigma = 40 + (-1.645)(4) = 40 - 6.58
$$

$$
\boxed{W \approx 33.42 \text{ months}} \quad \text{(7 marks)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Normal reasoning** is heavily tested in DOE because engineers frequently need to find the percentile/critical value, not the probability.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Table ŌĆö Operation Map

| **Operation Type** | **Given** | **Find** | **Tool / Formula** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Forward | $x$ value | $P(X \leq x)$ or $P(a \leq X \leq b)$ | Standardize then use Z-table |
| Inverse | Probability $p$ | Critical value $x_p$ | Z-table lookup then $x_p = \mu + z_p\sigma$ |
| Empirical | Range $(\mu \pm k\sigma)$ | Probability | Use 68ŌĆō95ŌĆō99.7 Rule |
| Symmetry | $z < 0$ | Convert to $z > 0$ | $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ |

# Module 1 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### Q1. Define the Normal Distribution. State any four important properties of the Normal Distribution.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow a Normal Distribution with parameters $\mu$ (mean) and $\sigma^2$ (variance) if its PDF is:

$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right], \quad -\infty < x < \infty
$$

**Four Properties (1/2 mark each):**
1. The curve is **bell-shaped** and **symmetric** about the line $x = \mu$.
2. **Mean = Median = Mode** = $\mu$.
3. The total area under the curve equals **1**.
4. The **inflection points** occur at $x = \mu \pm \sigma$.
5. (Bonus) The curve approaches but never touches the x-axis.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Model Paper]**

### Q2. State and explain the Empirical Rule (68ŌĆō95ŌĆō99.7 Rule) for a Normal Distribution.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

If $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then:

- Approximately **68.27%** of the observations lie within $\mu \pm 1\sigma$.
- Approximately **95.45%** of the observations lie within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$.
- Approximately **99.73%** of the observations lie within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.

**Engineering Significance:** This rule is used in statistical quality control (SQC) to set **control limits** in Shewhart control charts (X-bar, R-charts). Most manufacturing processes operate within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ ŌĆö any point outside this range is flagged as an "out-of-control signal", indicating an assignable cause in the Six-Sigma framework.

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module 1 ŌĆö Question Paper Pattern**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** *(Attempt any ONE from A or B)*

#### (a) Explain the properties of the Normal Distribution with a neat sketch. State its PDF. Why is it called a "Gaussian" distribution? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**PDF (1 Mark):**

$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]
$$

**Sketch (2 Marks):** Bell-shaped, symmetric about $\mu$, with tails approaching the x-axis asymptotically.

```
         f(x)
          Ō¢▓
          Ōöé        ___
          Ōöé      _/   \_
          Ōöé    _/       \_
          Ōöé   /           \
          Ōöé  /             \
          Ōöé /               \
          Ōöé/                 \
          Ōö╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ x
              ╬╝-2Žā  ╬╝-Žā  ╬╝  ╬╝+Žā  ╬╝+2Žā
```

**Properties (3 Marks):**
1. Symmetric about the mean.
2. Mean = Median = Mode.
3. Total area = 1.
4. Inflection points at $\mu \pm \sigma$.
5. Reproducibility under linear combinations.
6. Range: $-\infty$ to $+\infty$.

**"Gaussian" Naming (1 Mark):** Named after the German mathematician **Carl Friedrich Gauss** (1777ŌĆō1855), who used it to model errors in astronomical observations. Also independently derived by **Abraham de Moivre** (1733) and **Pierre-Simon Laplace**.

---

#### (b) The lifetimes of electric bulbs produced by a company are normally distributed with mean $\mu = 1200$ hours and standard deviation $\sigma = 100$ hours. Find the probability that a randomly selected bulb will last: **(i)** between 1100 and 1300 hours, **(ii)** more than 1400 hours. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Standardize the bounds.**

For $x_1 = 1100$:

$$
z_1 = \frac{1100 - 1200}{100} = -1.0
$$

For $x_2 = 1300$:

$$
z_2 = \frac{1300 - 1200}{100} = +1.0
$$

For $x_3 = 1400$:

$$
z_3 = \frac{1400 - 1200}{100} = +2.0
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute (i):**

$$
P(1100 \leq X \leq 1300) = P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) = \Phi(1) - \Phi(-1)
$$

Using symmetry $\Phi(-1) = 1 - \Phi(1)$:

$$
= \Phi(1) - [1 - \Phi(1)] = 2\Phi(1) - 1 = 2(0.8413) - 1 = 0.6826
$$

$$
\boxed{P(1100 \leq X \leq 1300) \approx 0.6826 \text{ or } 68.26\%} \quad \text{(3 Marks)}
$$

This matches the Empirical Rule (68.27% within $\mu \pm \sigma$).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute (ii):**

$$
P(X > 1400) = P(Z > 2) = 1 - \Phi(2) = 1 - 0.9772 = 0.0228
$$

$$
\boxed{P(X > 1400) \approx 0.0228 \text{ or } 2.28\%} \quad \text{(3 Marks)}
$$

**Verification:** By Empirical Rule, $P(X > \mu + 2\sigma) \approx 2.275\%$. Ō£ō Matches.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

#### (a) Derive the standardization formula for a Normal Distribution. Explain its significance in solving DOE problems. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

Let $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ and define $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$. We use the **MGF method** (already shown in Section 3.1) or the **Jacobian method**:

$$
f_X(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]
$$

Let $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, so $x = \sigma z + \mu$ and $\dfrac{dx}{dz} = \sigma$, hence $\left|\dfrac{dx}{dz}\right| = \sigma$.

By change of variables:

$$
f_Z(z) = f_X(x)\cdot\left|\frac{dx}{dz}\right| = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(\sigma z + \mu - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right] \cdot \sigma
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{z^2}{2}\right] = \phi(z)
$$

$$
\therefore Z \sim N(0, 1)
$$

**Significance in DOE (2 Marks):**
1. Enables use of a **single universal Z-table** for all Normal distributions.
2. Foundation for deriving the **t-distribution** (used in small-sample DOE).
3. Forms the basis for **ANOVA F-distribution** construction.
4. Critical for computing **Type-I & Type-II errors** in hypothesis testing.

---

#### (b) In a CNC machining process, the diameter of a shaft is normally distributed with mean 25.00 mm and standard deviation 0.05 mm. The design specification is **24.90 mm to 25.10 mm**. Calculate: **(i)** the percentage of shafts meeting the specification, **(ii)** the value below which 90% of shafts fall. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Percentage within specification (4 Marks):**

Lower bound:

$$
z_L = \frac{24.90 - 25.00}{0.05} = \frac{-0.10}{0.05} = -2.0
$$

Upper bound:

$$
z_U = \frac{25.10 - 25.00}{0.05} = \frac{+0.10}{0.05} = +2.0
$$

$$
P(24.90 \leq X \leq 25.10) = \Phi(2) - \Phi(-2) = 0.9772 - 0.0228 = 0.9544
$$

$$
\boxed{95.44\% \text{ of shafts meet the specification}}
$$

This is consistent with the Empirical Rule: $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ contains 95.45%.

**(ii) Value below which 90% of shafts fall (3 Marks):**

We need $P(X \leq x_{0.90}) = 0.90$. From Z-table, $\Phi(z) = 0.90 \Rightarrow z = 1.282$.

Back-transform:

$$
x_{0.90} = \mu + z\sigma = 25.00 + (1.282)(0.05) = 25.00 + 0.0641
$$

$$
\boxed{x_{0.90} \approx 25.064 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to standardize:** Students directly plug $x$ into $\Phi$ without applying $Z = (X-\mu)/\sigma$. This loses **2 to 3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Sign errors in Z:** $z = (475-500)/25 = -1$, not $+1$. Always compute (lower ŌłÆ higher) carefully.
> 3. **Symmetry confusion:** $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$, **not** $\Phi(-z) = \Phi(z)$. Tabulated values are always for positive $z$.
> 4. **Empirical Rule vs exact Z-table:** If the problem specifies "approximately", use 68/95/99.7. If it specifies exact values, you MUST use the Z-table.
> 5. **Units inconsistency:** Always verify $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are in the same unit before applying the formula.
> 6. **Inverse problem mis-application:** When finding a cut-off, ensure you back-transform correctly: $x = \mu + z\sigma$, NOT $x = z\sigma - \mu$.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember ŌĆö At a Glance:**

- Ō£ģ Normal Distribution PDF: $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\,\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$
- Ō£ģ Mean = Median = Mode = $\mu$; Variance = $\sigma^2$
- Ō£ģ Standardization formula: $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$, $Z \sim N(0,1)$
- Ō£ģ Empirical Rule: 68.27% within $1\sigma$, 95.45% within $2\sigma$, 99.73% within $3\sigma$
- Ō£ģ Symmetry property: $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$
- Ō£ģ Forward Problem: $x \to z \to \Phi(z) \to$ Probability
- Ō£ģ Inverse Problem: Probability $\to z \to x = \mu + z\sigma$
- Ō£ģ Reproductive property: Linear combination of independent Normals is Normal
- Ō£ģ Inflection points of the bell curve are at $\mu \pm \sigma$
- Ō£ģ DOE relevance: Foundation of ANOVA, t-test, F-test, control charts, residual analysis
- Ō£ģ Examiner loves: Standardization, Empirical Rule, and inverse Normal problems ŌĆö practice at least 5 of each!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Log Normal and Weibull Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Log-Normal Distribution

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A continuous random variable **X** is said to follow a **Log-Normal Distribution** if its natural logarithm **Y = ln(X)** follows a Normal Distribution **N(╬╝, Žā┬▓)**. Equivalently, if X > 0 and ln(X) Ōł╝ N(╬╝, Žā┬▓), then X is log-normally distributed with parameters ╬╝ and Žā┬▓, denoted as **X Ōł╝ LN(╬╝, Žā┬▓)**.

The two parameters are:
- **╬╝** = mean of ln(X)
- **Žā┬▓** = variance of ln(X)
- Both are defined such that **Žā > 0**.

### Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Chain of Percentages" Story

Imagine the price of a stock today. It is the result of yesterday's price multiplied by a small percentage change. Yesterday's price was itself the result of the day before multiplied by *another* small percentage change ŌĆö and so on, stretching back thousands of trading days.

Mathematically:

$$X_t = X_{t-1} \cdot (1 + r_t)$$

Taking logarithms:

$$\ln(X_t) = \ln(X_{t-1}) + \ln(1 + r_t)$$

By the **Central Limit Theorem**, when we sum up thousands of these tiny multiplicative factors, the resulting distribution of ln(X) becomes **Normal**, and therefore X itself becomes **Log-Normal**. This is why log-normal distributions are used to model:
- **Stock prices** and asset returns
- **Particle sizes** in comminution and crushing
- **Failure times** when failure is due to accumulated random shocks
- **Income distributions** in econometrics
- **Rainfall and flood volumes** in hydrology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The log-normal distribution is **positively skewed** (right-skewed). Unlike the normal distribution, it is bounded below by **0** but unbounded above, and its mean, median, and mode are **all different**.

---

## 1.2 The Weibull Distribution

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A continuous random variable **X** is said to follow a **Weibull Distribution** if its probability density function (PDF) is given by:

$$f(x) = \frac{k}{\lambda}\left(\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(x/\lambda)^k}, \quad x \geq 0$$

where:
- **k > 0** is the **shape parameter** (also called the Weibull slope)
- **╬╗ > 0** is the **scale parameter**
- **X Ōēź 0** is the random variable

The corresponding Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) is:

$$F(x) = 1 - e^{-(x/\lambda)^k}, \quad x \geq 0$$

### Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Weakest Link in a Chain"

The Weibull distribution was originally developed by **Waloddi Weibull (1939)** to model the **breaking strength of materials**. The intuitive picture is a chain with **n** links. The chain fails when the **weakest link breaks** ŌĆö and the distribution of the minimum of n random strengths turns out to have a Weibull shape in the limit.

**Real-world engineering applications:**
- **Reliability engineering** ŌĆö Time-to-failure of mechanical components, bearings, capacitors
- **Quality control** ŌĆö Lifetime of vacuum tubes, ball bearings, electrical insulation
- **Wind speed analysis** ŌĆö Wind energy potential studies
- **Hydrology** ŌĆö Flood and rainfall frequency analysis
- **Aerospace** ŌĆö Fatigue life of aircraft components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The shape parameter **k** is the soul of the Weibull distribution. It dictates the **shape** of the hazard (failure) rate:
> - **k < 1**: Decreasing failure rate (infant mortality)
> - **k = 1**: Constant failure rate (random failures ŌĆö becomes exponential)
> - **k > 1**: Increasing failure rate (wear-out failures)
> - **k Ōēł 3.4**: Closely approximates the Normal distribution
>
> **This flexibility is why Weibull is the workhorse distribution of reliability engineering.**

# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of the Log-Normal Distribution

Let **X Ōł╝ LN(╬╝, Žā┬▓)**. Then:

1. **Support:** x Ōłł (0, Ōł×)
2. **Mode** (peak of the PDF): 
$$\text{Mode}(X) = e^{\mu - \sigma^2}$$
3. **Median** (50th percentile):
$$\text{Median}(X) = e^{\mu}$$
4. **Mean** (1st moment):
$$E(X) = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}$$
5. **Variance** (2nd central moment):
$$\text{Var}(X) = \left(e^{\sigma^2} - 1\right) e^{2\mu + \sigma^2}$$
6. **Skewness coefficient:**
$$\gamma_1 = \sqrt{e^{\sigma^2} - 1}\left(e^{\sigma^2} + 2\right)$$
7. **Coefficient of variation (CV):**
$$CV = \sqrt{e^{\sigma^2} - 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of central tendency for Log-Normal:**
> $$\text{Mode} < \text{Median} < \text{Mean}$$
> This ordering holds because the distribution is right-skewed.

## 2.2 Properties of the Weibull Distribution

Let **X Ōł╝ Weibull(k, ╬╗)**. Then:

1. **Support:** x Ōłł [0, Ōł×)
2. **Mode** (for k > 1):
$$\text{Mode}(X) = \lambda \left(\frac{k-1}{k}\right)^{1/k}$$
3. **Median** (50th percentile):
$$\text{Median}(X) = \lambda (\ln 2)^{1/k}$$
4. **Mean** (1st moment):
$$E(X) = \lambda \,\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{k}\right)$$
5. **Variance** (2nd central moment):
$$\text{Var}(X) = \lambda^2 \left[\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{2}{k}\right) - \left(\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{k}\right)\right)^2\right]$$
6. **Reliability / Survivor function:**
$$R(x) = 1 - F(x) = e^{-(x/\lambda)^k}$$
7. **Hazard (failure) rate function:**
$$h(x) = \frac{f(x)}{R(x)} = \frac{k}{\lambda}\left(\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula you must memorize for the KTU ESE.**

| Distribution | Parameter | PDF $f(x)$ | CDF $F(x)$ | Mean | Variance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Log-Normal** $LN(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | $\mu \in \mathbb{R},\; \sigma>0$ | $\dfrac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\Phi\!\left(\dfrac{\ln x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}$ | $(e^{\sigma^2}-1)\,e^{2\mu+\sigma^2}$ |
| **Weibull** $W(k,\lambda)$ | $k>0,\;\lambda>0$ | $\dfrac{k}{\lambda}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1}\!e^{-(x/\lambda)^k}$ | $1-e^{-(x/\lambda)^k}$ | $\lambda\,\Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{k}\right)$ | $\lambda^2\!\left[\Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{2}{k}\right)-\Gamma^2\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{k}\right)\right]$ |

| Property | Log-Normal | Weibull |
|---|---|---|
| Median | $e^{\mu}$ | $\lambda(\ln 2)^{1/k}$ |
| Mode | $e^{\mu - \sigma^2}$ | $\lambda\!\left(\dfrac{k-1}{k}\right)^{1/k}$ for $k>1$ |
| Reliability $R(x)$ | $1-\Phi\!\left(\dfrac{\ln x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $e^{-(x/\lambda)^k}$ |
| Hazard $h(x)$ | $\dfrac{f(x)}{1-F(x)}$ (no closed form) | $\dfrac{k}{\lambda}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1}$ |
| Skewness | $\sqrt{e^{\sigma^2}-1}\,(e^{\sigma^2}+2)$ | $\dfrac{\Gamma(1+3/k)-3\Gamma(1+1/k)\Gamma(1+2/k)+2\Gamma^3(1+1/k)}{[\Gamma(1+2/k)-\Gamma^2(1+1/k)]^{3/2}}$ |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility ŌĆö Where Each Distribution is Indispensable

- **Log-Normal in Mining & Comminution:** Particle size distributions produced by crushers and ball mills are notoriously log-normal because breakage is a multiplicative cascade. The Rosin-Rammler equation (closely related to Weibull) is its sibling in the same domain.

- **Weibull in Reliability:** When an engineer cannot decide between exponential (constant failure rate) and log-normal (increasing failure rate), Weibull is the **default choice** because its shape parameter k subsumes both cases. By simply inspecting the value of k, the engineer can diagnose whether the system is in infant-mortality, useful-life, or wear-out phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Reference to KTU 2024 Module Outcomes:** This topic maps to **CO1** of OECHT727 ŌĆö "Understand the principles of statistical distributions and their applications in experimental data analysis." Mastery of the two distributions directly enables the unit's problem-solving questions.

# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean of the Log-Normal Distribution

**Given:** $X \sim LN(\mu, \sigma^2)$ means $\ln X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

PDF of X:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad x > 0$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the expectation integral:**

$$E(X) = \int_0^\infty x \cdot \frac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

The $x$ in the numerator cancels with the $x$ in the denominator:

$$E(X) = \int_0^\infty \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitution.** Let $t = \dfrac{\ln x - \mu}{\sigma}$, so $\ln x = \sigma t + \mu$.

Differentiating: $dx = \sigma e^{\sigma t + \mu}\, dt = \sigma x \, dt$.

Therefore: $\dfrac{dx}{x} = \sigma\, dt$.

When $x \to 0^+$, $t \to -\infty$. When $x \to \infty$, $t \to \infty$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute back:**

$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{t^2}{2}\right) \cdot \sigma \, e^{\sigma t + \mu}\, dt$$

$$\boxed{E(X) = e^\mu \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{t^2}{2} + \sigma t\right) dt}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Complete the square.** Inside the exponent:

$$-\frac{t^2}{2} + \sigma t = -\frac{1}{2}(t^2 - 2\sigma t) = -\frac{1}{2}\left[(t-\sigma)^2 - \sigma^2\right] = -\frac{(t-\sigma)^2}{2} + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Recognize the standard normal integral:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(t-\sigma)^2}{2}\right) dt = 1$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Conclude:**

$$\boxed{E(X) = e^\mu \cdot e^{\sigma^2/2} = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Variance of the Log-Normal Distribution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $E(X^2)$** using the same substitution with $x^2 = e^{2(\sigma t + \mu)}$:

$$E(X^2) = \int_0^\infty \frac{x}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

With the same substitution:

$$E(X^2) = e^{2\mu} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(t-\sigma)^2}{2} + \frac{\sigma^2}{2} + \sigma t\right) dt$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Combine the exponent:**

$$-\frac{(t-\sigma)^2}{2} + \sigma t + \frac{\sigma^2}{2} = -\frac{t^2 - 2\sigma t + \sigma^2}{2} + \sigma t + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}$$

$$= -\frac{t^2}{2} + \sigma t - \frac{\sigma^2}{2} + \sigma t + \frac{\sigma^2}{2} = -\frac{t^2}{2} + 2\sigma t$$

Now complete the square:

$$-\frac{t^2}{2} + 2\sigma t = -\frac{(t-2\sigma)^2}{2} + 2\sigma^2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply standard normal integral:**

$$E(X^2) = e^{2\mu + 2\sigma^2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply variance identity:**

$$\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = e^{2\mu + 2\sigma^2} - e^{2\mu + \sigma^2}$$

Factor out $e^{2\mu + \sigma^2}$:

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = e^{2\mu + \sigma^2}\left(e^{\sigma^2} - 1\right)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Mean of the Weibull Distribution

**Given PDF:**

$$f(x) = \frac{k}{\lambda}\left(\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(x/\lambda)^k}, \quad x \geq 0$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the expectation integral:**

$$E(X) = \int_0^\infty x \cdot \frac{k}{\lambda}\left(\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(x/\lambda)^k} dx$$

$$E(X) = \frac{k}{\lambda^k} \int_0^\infty x^k \, e^{-(x/\lambda)^k} dx$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitution.** Let $u = (x/\lambda)^k$, so $x = \lambda u^{1/k}$.

Then $du = \dfrac{k}{\lambda}\left(\dfrac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1} dx$, hence $dx = \dfrac{\lambda}{k} u^{1/k - 1} du$.

Also: $x^k = \lambda^k u$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute:**

$$E(X) = \frac{k}{\lambda^k} \int_0^\infty \lambda^k u \cdot e^{-u} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{k} u^{1/k - 1} du$$

The constants cancel:

$$E(X) = \lambda \int_0^\infty u^{1/k} \cdot e^{-u} du$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Recognize the Gamma integral.** By definition:

$$\Gamma(\alpha) = \int_0^\infty u^{\alpha - 1} e^{-u} du$$

Here $\alpha - 1 = 1/k$, so $\alpha = 1 + 1/k$:

$$\boxed{E(X) = \lambda \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{k}\right)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Variance of the Weibull Distribution

By analogous substitution $u = (x/\lambda)^k$:

$$E(X^2) = \frac{k}{\lambda^k} \int_0^\infty x^{k+1} e^{-(x/\lambda)^k} dx = \lambda^2 \int_0^\infty u^{2/k} e^{-u} du = \lambda^2 \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{2}{k}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = \lambda^2 \left[\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{2}{k}\right) - \left(\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{k}\right)\right)^2\right]}$$

---

## 3.5 Useful Gamma Function Values (Quick Lookup)

| $k$ | $\Gamma(1+1/k)$ | $\Gamma(1+2/k)$ |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | $\Gamma(2) = 1$ | $\Gamma(3) = 2$ |
| **2** | $\Gamma(1.5) = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$ | $\Gamma(2) = 1$ |
| **3** | $\Gamma(4/3) \approx 0.8930$ | $\Gamma(5/3) \approx 0.9027$ |
| **4** | $\Gamma(1.25) \approx 0.9064$ | $\Gamma(1.5) \approx 0.8862$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exponential Special Case:** When $k = 1$, Weibull reduces to **Exponential(╬╗)** with mean $\lambda$ and variance $\lambda^2$. This is because $\Gamma(2) = 1$ and $\Gamma(3) = 2$.

# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Distribution Relationship & Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous Distributions] --> B[Normal Family]
    A --> C[Skewed Family]

    B --> B1[Normal N mu sigma squared]
    B1 --> B2[Log Normal via Y equals ln X]

    C --> C1[Exponential family]
    C --> C2[Gamma family]
    C --> C3[Weibull family]

    C1 --> D1[Exponential lambda is Weibull k equals 1]
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2[Weibull k approx 3.4 approximates Normal]

    B2 --> E1[Stock prices, particle sizes, lifetimes]
    D1 --> E2[Constant hazard random failures]
    D2 --> E3[Wear-out fatigue life]
```

## 4.2 Log-Normal PDF Behaviour (Effect of Žā)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[mu equals 0 sigma small] --> P1a[Tight, near symmetric bell]
    P2[mu equals 0 sigma equals 1] --> P2a[Moderate right skew]
    P3[mu equals 0 sigma large] --> P3a[Strong right skew, long tail]

    P1a --> Q1[Mode near Median near Mean]
    P2a --> Q2[Mode less than Median less than Mean]
    P3a --> Q3[Mean pulled far right by tail]
```

## 4.3 Weibull Hazard Rate Behaviour (Effect of k)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Weibull shape parameter k] --> W1[k less than 1]
    W --> W2[k equals 1]
    W --> W3[k greater than 1]

    W1 --> H1[Decreasing hazard: infant mortality]
    W2 --> H2[Constant hazard: random failure equals exponential]
    W3 --> H3[Increasing hazard: wear out failure]

    H1 --> A1[Burn-in period, defective units fail early]
    H2 --> A2[Useful life phase]
    H3 --> A3[Aging components, fatigue cracks]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: When to Use Which Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Step 1: Is data bounded below by 0] --> S1{Yes}
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Is hazard rate monotonic]
    S2 --> S3{Monotonically decreasing}
    S2 --> S4{Constant}
    S2 --> S5{Monotonically increasing}

    S3 --> R1[Use Weibull with k less than 1]
    S4 --> R2[Use Weibull with k equals 1, or Exponential]
    S5 --> R3[Step 3: Is data multiplicative cascade]

    R3 --> R4{Yes}
    R3 --> R5{No}
    R4 --> O1[Use Log Normal]
    R5 --> O2[Use Weibull with k greater than 1]
```

# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]`
**Define the Log-Normal distribution. State its probability density function, mean and variance.**

**Model Answer:**

A random variable $X > 0$ follows a **log-normal distribution** with parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ (denoted $X \sim LN(\mu, \sigma^2)$) if $Y = \ln(X)$ follows a normal distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

PDF:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad x > 0$$

Mean and Variance:

$$E(X) = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}, \quad \text{Var}(X) = (e^{\sigma^2} - 1)\,e^{2\mu + \sigma^2}$$

It is used to model stock prices, particle sizes, and lifetimes arising from multiplicative random shocks. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]`
**What is the Weibull distribution? Mention the role of the shape parameter k in determining the failure rate.**

**Model Answer:**

A continuous random variable $X \geq 0$ follows a **Weibull distribution** with shape parameter $k > 0$ and scale parameter $\lambda > 0$ if its PDF is:

$$f(x) = \frac{k}{\lambda}\left(\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(x/\lambda)^k}$$

The shape parameter **k** controls the **failure (hazard) rate** $h(x) = \dfrac{k}{\lambda}\left(\dfrac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1}$:

- $k < 1$ ŌåÆ **decreasing** failure rate (infant mortality)
- $k = 1$ ŌåÆ **constant** failure rate (random failures; reduces to exponential)
- $k > 1$ ŌåÆ **increasing** failure rate (wear-out)

This adaptability makes Weibull the most widely used distribution in **reliability engineering**. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]`

**Question A(a) [7 marks]:** Derive the mean and variance of a random variable $X$ following the log-normal distribution $LN(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

**Model Solution:**

Let $Y = \ln(X) \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. The PDF of X is:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad x > 0$$

**Mean:** Apply the substitution $t = (\ln x - \mu)/\sigma$, so $\ln x = \sigma t + \mu$ and $dx/x = \sigma \, dt$:

$$E(X) = \int_0^\infty \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx = \int_{-\infty}^\infty \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{\sigma t + \mu} e^{-t^2/2} dt$$

$$= e^\mu \int_{-\infty}^\infty \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{t^2}{2} + \sigma t\right) dt$$

Complete the square: $-t^2/2 + \sigma t = -(t-\sigma)^2/2 + \sigma^2/2$. Hence:

$$E(X) = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}$$

**Variance:** Similarly, $E(X^2) = e^{2\mu + 2\sigma^2}$. Then:

$$\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = e^{2\mu + 2\sigma^2} - e^{2\mu + \sigma^2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = e^{2\mu + \sigma^2}(e^{\sigma^2} - 1)}$$

**Question A(b) [7 marks]:** The lifetimes (in hours) of 6 electronic components are observed to be:
`1200, 1450, 980, 1500, 1100, 1350`. The data is believed to follow a log-normal distribution. Estimate the parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ using the method of moments.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute sample mean and variance:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1200 + 1450 + 980 + 1500 + 1100 + 1350}{6} = \frac{7580}{6} \approx 1263.33$$

$$s^2 = \frac{1}{5}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 = \frac{1}{5}\big[(-63.33)^2 + (186.67)^2 + (-283.33)^2 + (236.67)^2 + (-163.33)^2 + (86.67)^2\big]$$

Computing each term:

- $(1200 - 1263.33)^2 = (-63.33)^2 \approx 4010.69$
- $(1450 - 1263.33)^2 = (186.67)^2 \approx 34845.69$
- $(980 - 1263.33)^2 = (-283.33)^2 \approx 80275.89$
- $(1500 - 1263.33)^2 = (236.67)^2 \approx 56012.69$
- $(1100 - 1263.33)^2 = (-163.33)^2 \approx 26676.69$
- $(1350 - 1263.33)^2 = (86.67)^2 \approx 7511.69$

Sum: $\approx 209333.34$

$$s^2 \approx \frac{209333.34}{5} = 41866.67$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Method of moments.** For log-normal:

$$\bar{x} = e^{\hat{\mu} + \hat{\sigma}^2/2}, \quad s^2 = e^{2\hat{\mu} + \hat{\sigma}^2}(e^{\hat{\sigma}^2} - 1)$$

Taking logs of the first equation:

$$\ln(\bar{x}) = \hat{\mu} + \frac{\hat{\sigma}^2}{2} \Rightarrow \hat{\mu} = \ln(1263.33) - \frac{\hat{\sigma}^2}{2}$$

Compute $\ln(1263.33) \approx 7.1422$.

The coefficient of variation squared gives:

$$CV^2 = \frac{s^2}{\bar{x}^2} = \frac{41866.67}{(1263.33)^2} = \frac{41866.67}{1596122.59} \approx 0.02623$$

For log-normal: $CV^2 = e^{\sigma^2} - 1$, so:

$$e^{\hat{\sigma}^2} = 1 + 0.02623 = 1.02623 \Rightarrow \hat{\sigma}^2 = \ln(1.02623) \approx 0.02589$$

Therefore:

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 \approx 0.0259, \quad \hat{\mu} = 7.1422 - 0.02589/2 = 7.1422 - 0.01295 \approx 7.1293$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\mu} \approx 7.1293, \quad \hat{\sigma}^2 \approx 0.0259}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]` *(Alternative to Question A)*

**Question B(a) [7 marks]:** Derive the mean of a random variable $X$ following the Weibull distribution with parameters $k$ and $\lambda$.

**Model Solution:**

PDF:

$$f(x) = \frac{k}{\lambda}\left(\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(x/\lambda)^k}, \quad x \geq 0$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the expectation:**

$$E(X) = \int_0^\infty x \cdot \frac{k}{\lambda}\left(\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(x/\lambda)^k} dx = \frac{k}{\lambda^k} \int_0^\infty x^k e^{-(x/\lambda)^k} dx$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitution.** Let $u = (x/\lambda)^k \Rightarrow x = \lambda u^{1/k}$, and $dx = \dfrac{\lambda}{k} u^{1/k - 1} du$.

Substituting:

$$E(X) = \frac{k}{\lambda^k} \int_0^\infty \lambda^k u \cdot e^{-u} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{k} u^{1/k - 1} du = \lambda \int_0^\infty u^{1/k} e^{-u} du$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recognize the Gamma function:**

$$\boxed{E(X) = \lambda \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{k}\right)}$$

**Question B(b) [7 marks]:** The time-to-failure (in hours) of a certain brand of bearing is modeled by a Weibull distribution with $k = 2$ and $\lambda = 5000$. Find (i) the mean lifetime, (ii) the median lifetime, (iii) the probability that a bearing lasts more than 6000 hours.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verify Gamma function values.** For $k = 2$:

$$\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) = \Gamma(1.5) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862$$

**(i) Mean lifetime:**

$$E(X) = \lambda \, \Gamma(1.5) = 5000 \times 0.8862 = 4431.0 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Median lifetime:**

$$\text{Median} = \lambda (\ln 2)^{1/k} = 5000 \times (\ln 2)^{1/2} = 5000 \times \sqrt{0.6931}$$

$$\text{Median} = 5000 \times 0.8326 = 4162.9 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Probability $X > 6000$:**

$$P(X > 6000) = R(6000) = e^{-(6000/5000)^2} = e^{-(1.2)^2} = e^{-1.44}$$

Using $e^{-1.44} \approx 0.2369$:

$$\boxed{P(X > 6000) \approx 0.2369 \text{ or } 23.69\%}$$
