<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define a 2^k factorial design. Why is it called a "screening" design in industrial experimentation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A 2^k factorial design is a full factorial design in which *k* independent factors are each studied at exactly two levels (low = ŌłÆ1 and high = +1), giving a total of 2^k experimental runs covering all possible factor combinations. It is called a "screening design" because it efficiently identifies the **most influential factors and their key interactions** from a large pool of candidate variables using the **minimum number of runs**. Since the design is orthogonal, the effect of each factor can be estimated independently of all others. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for screening rationale, 1 for orthogonal property)

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is the Yates algorithm? State any two of its advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Yates algorithm is a **recursive forward-difference computational procedure** used to estimate the effects and sums of squares for all $2^k - 1$ effects of an unreplicated 2^k factorial design from a single response column. It requires exactly *k* iterations of pairwise summation and subtraction.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Computational efficiency:** All effects and SS are obtained in a single pass with only additions and subtractions, no matrix inversion needed.
2. **Numerical stability:** Avoids large intermediate numbers compared to direct contrast summation.
3. **Manual calculation:** Permits hand computation for 2^k designs up to k = 5, which is useful in academic settings.
4. **Direct SS output:** Produces sum of squares directly from the squared contrast divided by N. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for first advantage, 1 for second advantage)

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A ŌĆö Option 1 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 2┬▓ factorial design is used to study the effect of two factors A (Temperature) and B (Pressure) on the yield of a chemical reaction. The data obtained is:

| Run | A | B | Treatment | Yield y |
|:---:|:-:|:-:|:---------:|:-------:|
| 1 | ŌłÆ1 | ŌłÆ1 | (1) | 20 |
| 2 | +1 | ŌłÆ1 | a | 30 |
| 3 | ŌłÆ1 | +1 | b | 25 |
| 4 | +1 | +1 | ab | 40 |

**Compute the main effects of A and B, the AB interaction effect, and the sum of squares for each. State the fitted first-order model with interactions.**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of **sparsity of effects**. How is it used in the analysis of unreplicated 2^k factorial designs to identify active effects? Briefly describe the **normal probability plot** method for effect identification.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the column signs:**

- A: +1 in runs 2, 4; ŌłÆ1 in runs 1, 3
- B: +1 in runs 3, 4; ŌłÆ1 in runs 1, 2
- AB: +1 in runs 1, 4; ŌłÆ1 in runs 2, 3 (since AB = A ├Ś B)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Contrasts:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Contrast}_A &= (30 + 40) - (20 + 25) = 70 - 45 = 25 \\
\text{Contrast}_B &= (25 + 40) - (20 + 30) = 65 - 50 = 15 \\
\text{Contrast}_{AB} &= (20 + 40) - (30 + 25) = 60 - 55 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Effects (using N = 4):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effect}_A &= \dfrac{2 \times 25}{4} = 12.5 \\
\text{Effect}_B &= \dfrac{2 \times 15}{4} = 7.5 \\
\text{Effect}_{AB} &= \dfrac{2 \times 5}{4} = 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sum of Squares:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_A &= \dfrac{(25)^2}{4} = \dfrac{625}{4} = 156.25 \\
SS_B &= \dfrac{(15)^2}{4} = \dfrac{225}{4} = 56.25 \\
SS_{AB} &= \dfrac{(5)^2}{4} = \dfrac{25}{4} = 6.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Fitted Regression Model:**

The grand mean is:

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{20 + 30 + 25 + 40}{4} = \dfrac{115}{4} = 28.75
$$

The regression coefficients are $\beta = \text{Effect}/2$:

$$
\beta_0 = 28.75, \quad \beta_A = 6.25, \quad \beta_B = 3.75, \quad \beta_{AB} = 1.25
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{y} = 28.75 + 6.25 x_A + 3.75 x_B + 1.25 x_A x_B}
$$

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Principle of Sparsity of Effects:**

The **principle of sparsity of effects**, introduced by Box and Wilson (1951), states that in most physical, chemical, and engineering systems, the response is dominated by a **small subset** of the total possible effects. Specifically:

- The **main effects** of a few factors tend to be large and significant.
- **Low-order interactions** (especially two-factor interactions) are often important.
- **High-order interactions** (three-factor and above) are typically **negligible** in magnitude and can be considered as random noise.

**Use in Unreplicated 2^k Designs:**

In an unreplicated 2^k design, there are **no degrees of freedom for pure error** (each effect uses 1 df and total df = 2^k ŌłÆ 1, leaving zero for error). To overcome this, the principle of sparsity is applied:

1. The *k* main effects and the $\binom{k}{2}$ two-factor interactions are identified as *potentially active*.
2. The remaining higher-order interactions (3FI, 4FI, etc.) are **pooled** to form an estimate of error.
3. The pooled error mean square is then used in an F-test or t-test to determine which of the *active* effects are statistically significant.

**Normal Probability Plot Method:**

The **normal probability plot** is a graphical technique for separating active effects from inactive (noise) effects:

1. Compute all $2^k - 1$ effect estimates.
2. Take the **absolute values** and arrange them in ascending order.
3. Plot the ordered effects on the **y-axis** against the corresponding **normal scores** (probabilities $i - 0.5 / (2^k - 1)$ converted to standard normal deviates) on the x-axis.
4. **Interpretation:**
   - Inactive (noise) effects form a **straight line** through the origin (they are approximately normally distributed around zero).
   - Active (significant) effects appear as **outliers** in the upper-right or lower-left tails, deviating sharply from the straight line.
5. The effects that lie far from the line are declared **significant active effects** and are retained in the model.

> **Marking note (b):** 2 marks for principle of sparsity statement, 2 marks for its use in pooling, 1 mark for normal probability plot procedure, 2 marks for interpretation of active vs inactive.

---

### **Question B ŌĆö Option 2 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **2^k factorial design** as a screening tool. With the help of a suitable block diagram, explain the **methodology of conducting a 2^k factorial experiment** in an industrial setting, from problem definition to confirmation runs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2┬│ factorial experiment is conducted to study the effect of three factors A, B, C on a product quality characteristic. The response data is given below:

| Run | A | B | C | y |
|:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:--:|
| 1 | ŌłÆ1 | ŌłÆ1 | ŌłÆ1 | 5 |
| 2 | +1 | ŌłÆ1 | ŌłÆ1 | 9 |
| 3 | ŌłÆ1 | +1 | ŌłÆ1 | 7 |
| 4 | +1 | +1 | ŌłÆ1 | 11 |
| 5 | ŌłÆ1 | ŌłÆ1 | +1 | 8 |
| 6 | +1 | ŌłÆ1 | +1 | 12 |
| 7 | ŌłÆ1 | +1 | +1 | 10 |
| 8 | +1 | +1 | +1 | 14 |

**Compute the effects of A, B, C, AB, AC, BC, and ABC. Identify the two most important effects and calculate their percentage contribution to the total sum of squares.**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**The 2^k Factorial Design as a Screening Tool:**

A 2^k factorial design is the **first-line design of choice** in industrial screening because it allows the experimenter to identify the most influential factors from a large set of candidate variables with the **minimum number of experimental trials**. The use of two levels per factor ensures that the design is **economical**, **orthogonal**, and **rotatable** (when augmented with center points). It is particularly effective in the **early stages** of process optimization, where the goal is *factor screening* rather than precise optimization.

**Methodology of Conducting a 2^k Experiment:**

The systematic procedure involves the following stages (refer to the flow diagram in Section 4.2):

**Stage 1 ŌĆö Problem Definition:** Clearly state the objective of the experiment, identify the response variable (which must be measurable on a continuous scale), and define the *k* candidate factors suspected of influencing the response.

**Stage 2 ŌĆö Factor and Level Selection:** Choose a low (ŌłÆ1) and high (+1) level for each factor. The levels must be within the safe operating region of the process. For qualitative factors (e.g., catalyst type), the two levels represent the two categories.

**Stage 3 ŌĆö Design Matrix Construction:** Generate the 2^k standard-order treatment combinations. This is done systematically: the first column cycles every 2^(kŌłÆ1) runs, the second column every 2^(kŌłÆ2) runs, ..., and the last column alternates every run.

**Stage 4 ŌĆö Randomization and Execution:** Conduct the 2^k runs in **random order** to protect against time-related trends, equipment drift, and environmental variations. Record the response for each run.

**Stage 5 ŌĆö Statistical Analysis:** Apply the Yates algorithm or direct contrast formulas to compute effect estimates and sums of squares. Use a normal probability plot (for unreplicated designs) or F-test (for replicated designs) to identify active effects.

**Stage 6 ŌĆö Model Fitting and Refinement:** Fit a regression equation containing only the active effects. Validate the model using residual analysis (normality, independence, constant variance).

**Stage 7 ŌĆö Confirmation Runs:** Conduct additional runs at the optimal factor settings (typically chosen from the sign of the main effects) to **verify** the predicted optimum and ensure reproducibility.

> **Marking note (a):** 2 marks for screening justification, 3 marks for the 7 stages of methodology, 2 marks for industry relevance/confirmation runs.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Grand Mean and Total SS:**

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{5 + 9 + 7 + 11 + 8 + 12 + 10 + 14}{8} = \dfrac{76}{8} = 9.5
$$

Computing $SS_T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_T &= (5-9.5)^2 + (9-9.5)^2 + (7-9.5)^2 + (11-9.5)^2 \\
     &+ (8-9.5)^2 + (12-9.5)^2 + (10-9.5)^2 + (14-9.5)^2 \\
     &= 20.25 + 0.25 + 6.25 + 2.25 + 2.25 + 6.25 + 0.25 + 20.25 \\
     &= 58.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Contrasts for Each Effect:**

- **A** (high in runs 2, 4, 6, 8; low in 1, 3, 5, 7):
$$
\text{Contrast}_A = (9+11+12+14) - (5+7+8+10) = 46 - 30 = 16
$$

- **B** (high in runs 3, 4, 7, 8; low in 1, 2, 5, 6):
$$
\text{Contrast}_B = (7+11+10+14) - (5+9+8+12) = 42 - 34 = 8
$$

- **C** (high in runs 5, 6, 7, 8; low in 1, 2, 3, 4):
$$
\text{Contrast}_C = (8+12+10+14) - (5+9+7+11) = 44 - 32 = 12
$$

- **AB** = A ├Ś B (high in runs 1, 4, 5, 8; low in 2, 3, 6, 7):
$$
\text{Contrast}_{AB} = (5+11+8+14) - (9+7+12+10) = 38 - 38 = 0
$$

- **AC** = A ├Ś C (high in runs 1, 3, 6, 8; low in 2, 4, 5, 7):
$$
\text{Contrast}_{AC} = (5+7+12+14) - (9+11+8+10) = 38 - 38 = 0
$$

- **BC** = B ├Ś C (high in runs 1, 2, 7, 8; low in 3, 4, 5, 6):
$$
\text{Contrast}_{BC} = (5+9+10+14) - (7+11+8+12) = 38 - 38 = 0
$$

- **ABC** = A ├Ś B ├Ś C (high in runs 2, 3, 5, 8; low in 1, 4, 6, 7):
$$
\text{Contrast}_{ABC} = (9+7+8+14) - (5+11+12+10) = 38 - 38 = 0
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Effect Estimates and Sum of Squares:**

| Effect | Contrast | Effect = 2┬ĘContrast/N | SS = Contrast┬▓/N | % Contribution |
|:------:|:--------:|:---------------------:|:----------------:|:--------------:|
| A | 16 | 4.00 | 32.00 | 55.17% |
| B | 8 | 2.00 | 8.00 | 13.79% |
| C | 12 | 3.00 | 18.00 | 31.03% |
| AB | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00% |
| AC | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00% |
| BC | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00% |
| ABC | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00% |
| **Total** | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **58.00** | **100.00%** |

**Step 4 ŌĆö Two Most Important Effects:**

The two most important effects are:
1. **Factor A** with $SS_A = 32.00$ contributing **55.17%** of the total variation.
2. **Factor C** with $SS_C = 18.00$ contributing **31.03%** of the total variation.

These two effects together account for **86.20%** of the total variability, confirming that A and C are the dominant factors in this experiment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Critical Loss-of-Marks Points):**
> 1. **Formula for Effect:** Students frequently write *Effect = Contrast/N* instead of *Effect = 2┬ĘContrast/N*. The factor of 2 arises because we need to average over the half-range (the change from ŌłÆ1 to +1 is a *double* change relative to the sign value).
> 2. **Misidentifying AB column:** The interaction column AB is the *element-wise product* of columns A and B, NOT the product of their effect values. Missing this leads to a wrong sign on the entire interaction.
> 3. **Percent contribution rounding:** Use at least 2 decimal places. The sum of all % contributions may not exactly equal 100% due to rounding ŌĆö this is acceptable, but document it.
> 4. **Standard order vs. Random order:** The 2^k design must be executed in *random order* to avoid bias, but *analysis* is done in *standard (Yates) order*. Forgetting to reorder the data back to standard order before applying the Yates algorithm is a common error.
> 5. **F-test vs. Normal plot choice:** Use the *normal probability plot* method for **unreplicated** designs (no error df). Use the *F-test* only for **replicated** designs. Mixing them up results in zero marks for the analysis part.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary ŌĆö Memorize Before Exam**

### **Core Concepts**
- Ō£ģ A **2^k factorial design** studies *k* factors at two levels (ŌłÆ1 and +1) using $N = 2^k$ runs.
- Ō£ģ Notation `(1)`, `a`, `b`, `ab`, `abc` denotes treatment combinations in **Yates standard order**.
- Ō£ģ Design is **orthogonal**, meaning all $2^k - 1$ effects are estimated independently.

### **Key Formulas (Must Memorize)**
- Ō£ģ $\text{Effect} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{Contrast}}{N}$
- Ō£ģ $SS = \dfrac{(\text{Contrast})^2}{N}$
- Ō£ģ $\text{Contrast} = \sum (x_i \cdot y_i)$ where $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$
- Ō£ģ $\% \text{Contribution} = \dfrac{SS_{\text{effect}}}{SS_T} \times 100$
- Ō£ģ Regression coefficient: $\beta = \dfrac{\text{Effect}}{2}$, intercept $\beta_0 = \bar{y}$

### **Effect Count**
- Ō£ģ Main effects: $k$
- Ō£ģ Two-factor interactions: $\binom{k}{2} = \dfrac{k(k-1)}{2}$
- Ō£ģ Three-factor interactions: $\binom{k}{3} = \dfrac{k(k-1)(k-2)}{6}$
- Ō£ģ Total effects: $2^k - 1$

### **Analysis Methods**
- Ō£ģ **Replicated design:** Use *F-test* on each effect (MS_E computed from replicates).
- Ō£ģ **Unreplicated design:** Use *Normal probability plot* of effects OR *Lenth's method*.
- Ō£ģ **Principle of Sparsity:** Pool higher-order (3FI, 4FI, ...) into error term.

### **Procedural Steps**
- Ō£ģ Define objective and response.
- Ō£ģ Choose factors and levels.
- Ō£ģ Construct design matrix in standard order.
- Ō£ģ Randomize and execute the 2^k runs.
- Ō£ģ Analyze with Yates algorithm or contrast formulas.
- Ō£ģ Identify active effects via F-test or normal plot.
- Ō£ģ Fit reduced regression model with active effects only.
- Ō£ģ Confirm with residual analysis and confirmation runs.

### **Common Numerical Defaults (Avoid These Traps)**
- ŌØī Forgetting the factor of **2** in the Effect formula.
- ŌØī Treating *standard order* and *random order* as interchangeable.
- ŌØī Computing interaction columns by *adding* instead of *multiplying* parent columns.
- ŌØī Failing to identify the *interaction columns* (AB, AC, BC, ABC) correctly using element-wise product.
- ŌØī Reporting Effect values without stating the units (response units).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blocking and Confounding in the 2k Factorial Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Blocking and Confounding in the 2k Factorial Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

In the **2$^k$ Factorial Design**, when the total number of treatment combinations (runs) becomes large (e.g., $2^5 = 32$, $2^6 = 64$), it is often impractical to run all replicates of the experiment under homogeneous conditions. **Blocking** is the strategic technique of partitioning the complete set of $2^k$ runs into smaller homogeneous sub-groups (called **blocks**) so that each block accounts for one level of a *nuisance factor* (an uncontrollable but measurable source of variability such as batches of raw material, days of testing, machines, or operators).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blocking Principle (Fisher's Principle of Local Control):** "Block what you can control; randomize what you cannot." In 2$^k$ designs, blocking is achieved by deliberately **confounding** (aliasing) one or more higher-order interaction effects with the block effects.

**Confounding** is the statistical design technique where selected treatment effects are deliberately made indistinguishable (aliased) from block differences. The effect chosen to be confounded with blocks is called the **Defining Contrast**, denoted by a generalized identity equation of the form:

$$I = \text{(Treatment Interaction)}$$

When we write $I = ABC$, it means that the three-factor interaction $ABC$ is identically equal to the mean ($I$), so the block contrasts become indistinguishable from the $ABC$ interaction effect.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a quality engineer at a chocolate factory testing 8 different recipes (a 2$^3$ factorial). You cannot bake all 8 recipes on the same day because of oven capacity, dough freshness, and shifts. 

- **Analogy of Blocking:** You decide to bake 4 recipes on **Monday** and the remaining 4 on **Tuesday**. The day (Monday/Tuesday) is the **block** because the flour quality, ambient humidity, and operator fatigue might differ between days. By grouping recipes into days, you ensure that comparisons *within* a day are highly precise (local control).

- **Analogy of Confounding:** But wait ŌĆö what if the recipes placed on Monday happen to share a special feature (say, all four contain cocoa powder)? Then you cannot tell whether the difference you observe is due to the recipe *or* due to Monday's oven being slightly hotter. That shared feature is **confounded** with the block. You have "spent" one degree of freedom to remove the day-to-day variation, but in return, you have permanently lost the ability to measure one specific effect.

In short: **Blocking removes unwanted noise, but Confounding tells you exactly which experimental signal you are sacrificing in exchange.**

### 1.3 Why Blocking is Essential in 2$^k$ Designs

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> The Course Outcome mapped to this module emphasizes the student's ability to *apply* blocking and confounding techniques to industrial experiments. Board questions frequently test the construction of the design layout and identification of confounded effects.

**Three practical reasons:**
1. **Resource constraints:** Limited capacity of experimental setup (e.g., only 4 reactors available, but a $2^4 = 16$ run design is needed).
2. **Heterogeneity over time:** Long experiments span multiple days/shifts where environmental drift contaminates results.
3. **Cost optimization:** A single homogeneous batch of raw material may suffice for only a few runs, making it economically sensible to process in batches (blocks).

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | KTU Notation |
|------|---------|--------------|
| **Block** | A homogeneous subgroup of runs | $B$ |
| **Confounding** | Aliasing of treatment effect with block effect | $E_A \equiv B$ |
| **Defining Contrast** | The interaction chosen to be confounded | $I = ABC$ |
| **Generalized Interaction** | Product of two or more defining contrasts | $I = (ABC)(ABD)$ |
| **Resolution** | Word length of the shortest defining relation | III, IV, V |
| **Block Size** | Runs per block | $2^{k-p}$ |
| **Number of Blocks** | Required sub-divisions | $2^p$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ŌĆö The Logic Behind Blocking

When a 2$^k$ factorial design is to be blocked, the experimenter faces a design choice problem. The total number of treatment combinations is fixed at $2^k$. If the experimental conditions are heterogeneous, the engineer partitions these $2^k$ runs into $2^p$ blocks (where $p \geq 1$, and $p < k$), each containing $b = 2^{k-p}$ runs.

**The Logic Sequence:**

1. **Identify the nuisance factor** (e.g., day, batch, machine) that introduces systematic variability.
2. **Decide the number of blocks** $2^p$ required to absorb this nuisance variation.
3. **Choose $p$ effects** (usually the highest-order interactions available) to be confounded with the block contrasts. These are the **defining contrasts**.
4. **Construct the design** by assigning each of the $2^k$ treatment combinations to one block based on the sign of the confounding interaction.
5. **Randomize run order** *within* each block to eliminate bias.
6. **Analyze the data** using Yates' algorithm with the block effect removed as a covariate in the ANOVA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why confound the *highest-order* interaction?**
> In the **Effect Hierarchy Principle** (Box & Hunter), lower-order effects (main effects and two-factor interactions) are nearly always more important and likely to be physically real, whereas three-factor and higher interactions are often negligible. Sacrificing an effect that we don't care about (e.g., $ABCD$) to gain precision on the effects we do care about is a classic *trade-off strategy*.

### 2.2 The Principle of Confounding ŌĆö Mathematical Setup

Let the treatment vector be represented in **Yates' standard order** as columns of $+1$ and $-1$. Consider the $i^{th}$ treatment combination having a column for factor $A$, factor $B$, ..., factor $K$. The **block contrast** is constructed by multiplying the columns of the chosen interaction. For example, if we confound $ABC$, then:

- Treatment combinations where $A \times B \times C = +1$ go to **Block 1**.
- Treatment combinations where $A \times B \times C = -1$ go to **Block 2**.

Mathematically, this is equivalent to writing:

$$I = ABC$$

The generalized identity $I = ABC$ implies that the contrast for $ABC$ is mathematically identical to the grand mean ($I$), so any measured variation along the $ABC$ column cannot be separated from the block effect.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The following table is the *minimum* formula set required. Memorize the resolution formulas ŌĆö they appear in nearly every 14-mark question on this topic.

\begin{aligned}
\text{Block Size } (b) &= 2^{k-p} \\
\text{Number of Blocks} &= 2^p \\
\text{Total Runs} &= 2^k \\
\text{Effects Confounded with Blocks} &= 2^p - 1 \\
\text{Resolution (R)} &= \text{Length of shortest word in defining relation} \\
\end{aligned}

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Utility |
|-------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|
| Defining Relation (single) | $I = ABC \dots$ (length $r$) | Identifies the sacrificed effect |
| Generalized Identity | Product of any two defining contrasts | Identifies ALL confounded alias sets |
| Resolution III Design | Main effects $\equiv$ 2-factor interactions | Used when higher-order interactions are negligible |
| Resolution IV Design | Main effects $\equiv$ 3-factor interactions (no 2-factor $\equiv$ 2-factor) | Safer choice for industrial R\&D |
| Resolution V Design | Main effects $\equiv$ 4-factor interactions (no 2 $\equiv$ 3) | Gold standard; needs many runs |
| Block $\to$ Treatment Assignment | Sign of $\prod (\text{Defining Contrast columns})$ | Used to construct the design matrix |
| Variance of Block Effect | $\sigma^2_{\text{block}} = \sigma^2 / b$ | Quantifies reduction in error variance |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Integrity Protocol:** Notice that vertical bars in column formulas above are rendered using LaTeX `\vert` (not raw $\vert$) to prevent breaking the markdown table parser.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial applications, the **Resolution** of the design is the critical quality indicator:

- **Resolution III ($R_{\text{III}}$):** Used in **screening experiments** where the goal is to identify the *most influential* factors among many. Common in pharmaceutical pre-formulation and semiconductor yield analysis. The risk: if a two-factor interaction $AB$ is real, it will be falsely attributed to factor $A$ alone.

- **Resolution IV ($R_{\text{IV}}$):** The **workhorse** of industrial DOE. Main effects are clean of two-factor confusions. Most response surface methodology (RSM) follow-up designs begin from $R_{\text{IV}}$ designs. Examples: $2^{4-1}$ with $I = ABCD$, $2^{5-1}$ with $I = ABCDE$.

- **Resolution V ($R_{\text{V}}$):** Premium precision. Two-factor interactions can be cleanly estimated independent of each other. Required for **chemical reaction optimization** where multiple interactions are suspected to be active simultaneously.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Confounding a $2^3$ Design in $2^1 = 2$ Blocks

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A chemical engineer is studying the effect of three factors on yield: A = Temperature, B = Pressure, C = Catalyst Concentration. A $2^3 = 8$ run factorial is to be conducted. Due to the limited capacity of the reactor, only 4 runs can be performed per day. **Confound the $ABC$ interaction with blocks and construct the design layout.**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Parameters**

\begin{aligned}
k &= 3 \\
p &= 1 \quad (\text{so } 2^p = 2 \text{ blocks}) \\
b &= 2^{k-p} = 2^{3-1} = 4 \text{ runs per block} \\
\text{Defining Contrast:} \quad I &= ABC
\end{aligned}

**Step 2: Construct Yates' Standard Order Table**

The standard order for a $2^3$ design is generated by writing the sign columns:

| Run | A | B | C | $ABC$ |
|-----|---|---|---|-------|
| 1   | - | - | - | -     |
| 2   | + | - | - | +     |
| 3   | - | + | - | +     |
| 4   | + | + | - | -     |
| 5   | - | - | + | +     |
| 6   | + | - | + | -     |
| 7   | - | + | + | -     |
| 8   | + | + | + | +     |

**Step 3: Assign Runs to Blocks Based on Sign of $ABC$**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Block 1: } ABC = -1 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{Runs } \{1, 4, 6, 7\} \\
\text{Block 2: } ABC = +1 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{Runs } \{2, 3, 5, 8\}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4: Identify the Confounded (Aliased) Effects**

When $I = ABC$ is the defining contrast, multiplying both sides by any effect gives its alias. The complete alias set is:

\begin{aligned}
I &= ABC \\
A &= BC \\
B &= AC \\
C &= AB
\end{aligned}

**Interpretation:**
- The $ABC$ effect is **sacrificed** (confounded with blocks).
- The two-factor interaction $AB$ is now **indistinguishable** from the main effect of $C$.
- Similarly, $AC \equiv B$ and $BC \equiv A$.

**Step 5: Compute the Resolution**

The shortest word in the defining relation is $ABC$ (length = 3). Therefore, the design has **Resolution III** ($R_{\text{III}}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Trade-off:** We lose the ability to estimate $ABC$, but we gain the precision of having all 4 runs in a block done under nearly identical reactor conditions. This is a sound trade-off because $ABC$ is a three-factor interaction, which is rarely physically meaningful.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Confounding a $2^4$ Design in $2^2 = 4$ Blocks

**Problem Statement:** A $2^4$ design (16 runs) is to be blocked into 4 blocks of size 4 each. Confound the interactions $ABCD$ and $ABC$ with blocks. Identify the resolution and all confounded effects.

**Step 1: Parameters**

\begin{aligned}
k &= 4 \\
p &= 2 \quad (\text{so } 2^p = 4 \text{ blocks}) \\
b &= 2^{k-p} = 2^{4-2} = 4 \text{ runs per block} \\
\text{Defining Contrasts:} \quad I_1 &= ABCD, \quad I_2 = ABC
\end{aligned}

**Step 2: Construct the Generalized Interaction**

The generalized interaction (GI) is the product of the individual defining contrasts:

\begin{aligned}
I_3 &= I_1 \times I_2 = (ABCD)(ABC) = A^2 B^2 C^2 D = D
\end{aligned}

(Note: since $A \cdot A = 1$ in $+1/-1$ arithmetic, all squared letters vanish.)

**Step 3: The Complete Defining Relation**

\begin{aligned}
I &= ABCD \\
I &= ABC \\
I &= D
\end{aligned}

**Step 4: Resolution Determination**

The shortest word in the defining relation is $D$ (length 1). 

Wait ŌĆö this would imply $D$ is confounded with the mean, which means we have completely **lost the ability to estimate the main effect of $D$**!

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** A defining relation containing a single letter (e.g., $I = D$) means that main effect is **completely confounded with the mean (blocks)**. This makes the design **Resolution I**, which is practically useless. Always verify that no individual main effect appears as a defining contrast.

**Corrected Design Choice:** To obtain a **Resolution IV** design, choose two defining contrasts such that no single letter or pair of letters emerges as a GI. The classical choice is:

\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= ABCD \quad \text{(length 4)} \\
I_2 &= ACE \quad \text{(... wait, $k=4$ so $E$ does not exist)}
\end{aligned}

**Re-correction for $2^4$:** Since $k = 4$, only letters $A, B, C, D$ exist. Valid choices for two length-4 defining contrasts are:

\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= ABCD \\
I_2 &= ABCE \quad \text{(Not valid ŌĆö no } E \text{)}
\end{aligned}

The **standard solution** for blocking a $2^4$ design in 4 blocks of 4 is to use the **half-fraction** $2^{4-1}$ with $I = ABCD$, and then within each block of 8, sub-divide further. However, for blocking $2^4$ into exactly **4 blocks**, we use the **quarter-fraction** $2^{4-2}$:

\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= ABCD \\
I_2 &= ABE \quad \text{... not applicable}
\end{aligned}

**Correct Approach ŌĆö Use length-3 Defining Contrasts:**

A valid choice is:

\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= ABD \\
I_2 &= BCD
\end{aligned}

Then:

\begin{aligned}
I_3 &= (ABD)(BCD) = A B^2 C D^2 = AC \quad \text{(length 2)}
\end{aligned}

The defining relation $\{ABD, BCD, AC\}$ has shortest word length **2**, giving **Resolution II**. This is also poor.

**The Industry-Standard Resolution IV Design for $2^4$ in 4 Blocks:**

Choose the defining contrasts as **length-3** interactions where the GI is also length 3 or higher:

\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= ABC \\
I_2 &= ABD
\end{aligned}

Then:

\begin{aligned}
I_3 &= (ABC)(ABD) = A^2 B^2 CD = CD \quad \text{(length 2)} \quad \Rightarrow R_{\text{II}}
\end{aligned}

To achieve **Resolution IV** in $2^4$ blocked into 4 blocks, we **must** accept a longer design or use:

\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= ACD \\
I_2 &= BCD
\end{aligned}

GI $= ABCD$ (length 4). Defining relation: $\{ACD, BCD, ABCD\}$. Shortest word length = 3 $\Rightarrow R_{\text{III}}$. Still not IV.

**Resolution IV Solution ŌĆö The Standard Approach:**

To get $R_{\text{IV}}$ in a $2^4$ blocked into $2^2 = 4$ blocks, the defining contrasts must be such that their GI has length $\geq 4$. With only 4 letters, the GI of any two length-3 words will have length $\leq 4$. The only way to get a length-4 GI is to choose length-4 defining contrasts:

\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= ABCD \quad \text{(length 4)} \\
I_2 &= ABCE \quad \text{(Invalid for } k=4)
\end{aligned}

**Resolution IV in $2^4$ is therefore IMPOSSIBLE in 4 blocks** ŌĆö the maximum achievable resolution is **$R_{\text{III}}$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Correct Design for $2^4$ in 4 Blocks:**
> Use $I_1 = ABC$ and $I_2 = ABD$ (or similar). The defining relation is $\{ABC, ABD, CD\}$. Resolution = **III**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Design Layout for the $2^4$ in 4 Blocks ($R_{\text{III}}$)

**Defining Contrasts:** $I_1 = ABC$, $I_2 = ABD$, $I_3 = CD$ (GI).

**Block Assignment Rule:** Each run is placed in the block based on the signs of $(ABC)$ and $(ABD)$.

| Run | A | B | C | D | ABC | ABD | Block |
|-----|---|---|---|---|-----|-----|-------|
| 1   | - | - | - | - | -   | -   | I     |
| 2   | + | - | - | - | +   | +   | II    |
| 3   | - | + | - | - | +   | +   | II    |
| 4   | + | + | - | - | -   | -   | I     |
| 5   | - | - | + | - | +   | -   | III   |
| 6   | + | - | + | - | -   | +   | IV    |
| 7   | - | + | + | - | -   | +   | IV    |
| 8   | + | + | + | - | +   | -   | III   |
| 9   | - | - | - | + | -   | +   | IV    |
| 10  | + | - | - | + | +   | -   | III   |
| 11  | - | + | - | + | +   | -   | III   |
| 12  | + | + | - | + | -   | +   | IV    |
| 13  | - | - | + | + | +   | +   | II    |
| 14  | + | - | + | + | -   | -   | I     |
| 15  | - | + | + | + | -   | -   | I     |
| 16  | + | + | + | + | +   | +   | II    |

**Block Summary:**
- Block I: Runs $\{1, 4, 14, 15\}$
- Block II: Runs $\{2, 3, 13, 16\}$
- Block III: Runs $\{5, 8, 10, 11\}$
- Block IV: Runs $\{6, 7, 9, 12\}$

**Confounded Effects (Alias Sets):**

\begin{aligned}
I &= ABC = ABD = CD \\
A &= BC = BD = ACD \\
B &= AC = AD = BCD \\
C &= AB = ABCD = D \\
D &= ABD = ABC = C
\end{aligned}

(Note: $C$ and $D$ are confounded with each other ŌĆö this is the **defining characteristic of $R_{\text{III}}$**: main effects can be aliased with each other when their product is in the defining relation.)

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flow ŌĆö Selecting the Number of Blocks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: 2^k Factorial Design] --> B{Is homogeneity\nachievable?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Run all 2^k in one block\nNo confounding]
    B -- No --> D{Identify nuisance\nfactor levels}
    D --> E[Number of nuisance\nlevels = 2^p]
    E --> F[Block size = 2^(k-p)]
    F --> G{Select p highest-order\ninteractions as\ndefining contrasts}
    G --> H[Construct generalized\ninteractions]
    H --> I{Compute shortest\nword length}
    I -- Length 3 --> J[Resolution III Design\nMain effects confounded\nwith 2-factor interactions]
    I -- Length 4 --> K[Resolution IV Design\nMain effects clean of\n2-factor confusions]
    I -- Length 5 --> L[Resolution V Design\n2-factor clean of\n3-factor confusions]
    J --> M[Assign runs to blocks\nusing sign rule]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Randomize run order\nwithin each block]
    N --> O[Conduct experiment]
    O --> P[ANOVA with block factor\nEstimate confounded effects\nas 0 or negligible]
```

```mermaid
    B1 --> R1[Replicate runs\nwithin block]
    B2 --> R2[Replicate runs\nwithin block]
    B3 --> R3[Replicate runs\nwithin block]
    B4 --> R4[Replicate runs\nwithin block]

    R1 --> Y[Yates Algorithm\nAdjusted for Blocks]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Concept[Confounding in 2^k Design] --> L1[Length 1 Word:\nResolution I\nUseless Design]
    Concept --> L2[Length 2 Word:\nResolution II\nRare Use]
    Concept --> L3[Length 3 Word:\nResolution III\nScreening Designs]
    Concept --> L4[Length 4 Word:\nResolution IV\nIndustrial Standard]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Central[Defining Relation:\nI = ABCD]

    Central --> Branch1[Length 4 Words:\nMain effects Aliased\nwith 3-factor interactions]
    Central --> Branch2[Length 4 GIs:\n2-factor Aliased\nwith 2-factor]

    Branch1 --> SubA[A is aliased with BCD]
    Branch1 --> SubB[B is aliased with ACD]
    Branch1 --> SubC[C is aliased with ABD]
    Branch1 --> SubD[D is aliased with ABC]

    Branch2 --> SubAB[AB is aliased with CD]
    Branch2 --> SubAC[AC is aliased with BD]
    Branch2 --> SubAD[AD is aliased with BC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The $R_{\text{IV}}$ property is evident ŌĆö main effects ($A, B, C, D$) are *only* confounded with 3-factor interactions (never with each other or with 2-factor interactions). This is the engineering "safety net" of $R_{\text{IV}}$ designs.
