$$\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} e_{ij}^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(y_{ij}-\bar{y}_{i\cdot})^2 + n_i(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}-\hat{y}_i)^2$$

**4. Sum over all $i$** (2 marks):
$$SS_E = \underbrace{\sum_i\sum_j(y_{ij}-\bar{y}_{i\cdot})^2}_{SS_{PE}} + \underbrace{\sum_i n_i(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}-\hat{y}_i)^2}_{SS_{LOF}}$$

**Meaning:** $SS_{PE}$ is variation due to replicate noise; $SS_{LOF}$ is variation due to model inadequacy.

---

**Q(b)** An experiment measures hardness ($y$, BHN) as a function of cooling rate ($x$, ┬░C/min). Data:

| $x_i$ | Replicates $y_{ij}$ |
|---|---|
| 10 | 35, 36, 38 |
| 20 | 42, 43, 45 |
| 30 | 50, 51, 49 |
| 40 | 58, 60, 59 |
| 50 | 68, 66, 70 |

Fit a linear model and perform the **Lack of Fit test** at $\alpha = 0.05$. Recommend action if needed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Level means:**

$\bar{y}_{1\cdot}=36.33,\; \bar{y}_{2\cdot}=43.33,\; \bar{y}_{3\cdot}=50,\; \bar{y}_{4\cdot}=59,\; \bar{y}_{5\cdot}=68$.
$N=15$, $m=5$, $p=2$. Grand mean $\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}=51.33$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Linear fit:**

$\hat{\beta}_1 = 0.81$, $\hat{\beta}_0 = 27.79$.
Fitted model: $\hat{y} = 27.79 + 0.81 x$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Predictions:**

$\hat{y}_1=35.89,\; \hat{y}_2=43.99,\; \hat{y}_3=52.09,\; \hat{y}_4=60.19,\; \hat{y}_5=68.29$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Pure Error SS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{PE} &= (35-36.33)^2+(36-36.33)^2+(38-36.33)^2 \\
&\quad +(42-43.33)^2+(43-43.33)^2+(45-43.33)^2 \\
&\quad +(50-50)^2+(51-50)^2+(49-50)^2 \\
&\quad +(58-59)^2+(60-59)^2+(59-59)^2 \\
&\quad +(68-68)^2+(66-68)^2+(70-68)^2 \\
&= 4.67 + 4.67 + 14.67 = 24.0 \text{ (sum across all 5 levels)} \\
&\text{(detailed computation: each level contributes 2.67, 2.67, 2.0, 2.0, 8.0 ŌåÆ total = 14.67 + ...)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Working level-by-level: level 1 = $1.78+0.11+2.78 = 4.67$; level 2 = $1.78+0.11+2.78 = 4.67$; level 3 = $0+1+1=2$; level 4 = $1+1+0=2$; level 5 = $0+4+4=8$. So $SS_{PE}=21.33$.

$df_{PE} = 10$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Lack of Fit SS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{LOF} &= 3(36.33-35.89)^2 + 3(43.33-43.99)^2 + 3(50-52.09)^2 \\
&\quad + 3(59-60.19)^2 + 3(68-68.29)^2 \\
&= 3(0.194) + 3(0.435) + 3(4.368) + 3(1.416) + 3(0.084) \\
&= 0.58 + 1.30 + 13.10 + 4.25 + 0.25 = 19.48
\end{aligned}
$$

$df_{LOF} = 3$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö F-statistic:**

$$
F_0 = \frac{19.48/3}{21.33/10} = \frac{6.49}{2.13} = 3.05
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Decision:**

$F_{0.05,\,3,\,10} = 3.71$. Since $F_0 = 3.05 < 3.71$, **fail to reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** Model is adequate. No remedial action required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Confusing $SS_{PE}$ with $SS_{LOF}$ when computing. Remember: $SS_{PE}$ involves *raw replicates vs. local mean*, $SS_{LOF}$ involves *local mean vs. predicted value*.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting the weights $n_i$ in $SS_{LOF}$. Students often write $\sum(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}-\hat{y}_i)^2$ without the $n_i$, losing 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Mis-assigning degrees of freedom. Memorize: $df_{PE}=N-m$ and $df_{LOF}=m-p$.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Forgetting to verify replication exists before invoking the LOF test. If no replication, **the test is undefined** ŌĆö write "Test not applicable" for full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 5**: Using a *two-tailed* F-test. The LOF test is **strictly right-tailed**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Testing for Lack of Fit**

- **When to apply**: Only when the design contains **replicated observations** ($n_i \geq 2$ for some $i$).
- **Hypotheses**: $H_0$ ŌĆö model is adequate; $H_1$ ŌĆö model lacks fit.
- **Partition identity**: $SS_E = SS_{PE} + SS_{LOF}$, with $df_E = (N-m) + (m-p)$.
- **$SS_{PE}$ formula**: $SS_{PE} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j}(y_{ij}-\bar{y}_{i\cdot})^2$, $df = N-m$.
- **$SS_{LOF}$ formula**: $SS_{LOF} = \sum_i n_i(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}-\hat{y}_i)^2$, $df = m-p$.
- **Test statistic**: $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_{LOF}}{MS_{PE}} = \dfrac{SS_{LOF}/(m-p)}{SS_{PE}/(N-m)}$.
- **Distribution under $H_0$**: $F_0 \sim F_{m-p,\; N-m}$.
- **Decision**: Reject $H_0$ if $F_0 > F_{\alpha,\,m-p,\,N-m}$ (right-tailed test).
- **Remedial actions if rejected**: Add higher-order polynomial terms (e.g., $x^2$); include interaction terms; transform variables (e.g., $\log y$, $\sqrt{y}$); switch to a non-linear model.
- **Default $\alpha$**: 0.05 in industrial contexts; 0.01 in safety-critical applications.
- **Cross-term in derivation vanishes** because $\sum_j (y_{ij}-\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = 0$ by definition of the mean ŌĆö this is the **key algebraic step**.
- **Practical insight**: Lack of fit is a problem of *model form*, not of *measurement noise*. Fix it by **changing the model**, not by collecting more replicates alone.
- **KTU 2024 outcome mapping**: This topic maps to **CO4** (Model adequacy & validation) at Bloom's **Analyze / Evaluate** cognitive level.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Response Surface Methodology: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Response Surface Methodology: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** is a collection of mathematical and statistical techniques that are useful for developing, improving, and optimizing processes. It uses a sequence of designed experiments to obtain an optimal response, employing a *low-order polynomial* (typically first-order or second-order) in the space of the independent variables to approximate the true but unknown functional relationship between a response $Y$ and a set of controllable input factors $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k$.

The methodology, formally introduced by **George E. P. Box and K. B. Wilson in 1951**, integrates elements of:
- **Design of Experiments (DOE)** ŌĆö for efficient data collection.
- **Regression Analysis** ŌĆö for fitting empirical models.
- **Optimization Techniques** ŌĆö for finding the optimal operating conditions.

The general model fitted by RSM is:
$$Y = f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k) + \varepsilon$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is the unknown *response surface function* (approximated by a polynomial) and $\varepsilon$ is the random error component (assumed $N(0, \sigma^2)$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OECHT727 - Module 4):**
> RSM is used when the objective is to **locate the optimal operating conditions** of a process, *not* merely to identify significant factors (which is the role of earlier factorial designs).

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | KTU-Standard Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Response ($Y$)** | The measured outcome / dependent variable / quality characteristic being optimized. |
| **Independent Variables ($x_i$)** | Controllable input process factors whose levels are manipulated by the experimenter. |
| **Response Surface** | A geometric visualization (3D plot) showing how the response $Y$ varies as a function of two or more factors. |
| **Contour Plot** | A 2D topographical map showing lines of constant response. |
| **Stationary Point** | The point where the slope of the fitted surface equals zero ($\partial \hat{Y}/\partial x_i = 0$). |
| **Region of Optimization** | The bounded experimental region inside which the optimum is sought. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Mountain Climber's Dilemma

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine you are blindfolded on a hilly landscape and asked to find the **highest peak** (a *maximum*) or the **deepest valley** (a *minimum*).
>
> - You cannot see the whole terrain. All you can do is **probe the ground** at certain locations and ask, *"How high am I here?"*
> - Each probe is an **experimental run**. The set of probed locations forms your **experimental design** (e.g., a Central Composite Design).
> - From a small cluster of probed heights, you fit a **smooth mathematical surface** (the response surface) that locally approximates the true terrain.
> - You then *climb* ŌĆö that is, move along the gradient ŌĆö until you reach a peak where the ground is flat in every direction. This is the **stationary point**, the mathematical analogue of the summit.
>
> RSM is essentially this iterative, gradient-following climb performed mathematically with polynomials instead of footsteps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** RSM is heavily used in *chemical engineering* (yield optimization), *manufacturing* (surface roughness, tensile strength), *food technology*, and *biotechnology* ŌĆö anywhere the underlying mechanism is too complex to model from first principles, but a **near-optimal empirical recipe** is required quickly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need a "Response Surface"?

In earlier modules (Factorial Designs, ANOVA), we answered the question:
> *"Which factors significantly affect the response?"*

RSM goes further and answers:
> *"Given these significant factors, what **levels** of each factor produce the **best** response?"*

This shift from *screening* to *optimization* demands a model that is **predictive** over a continuous region ŌĆö hence a polynomial surface.

## 2.2 Two-Stage Iterative Strategy of Box & Wilson

> [!NOTE]
> The genius of RSM lies in its **two-stage iterative procedure**, each stage using a *different* polynomial order:

### Stage 1 ŌĆö First-Order Model (Steepest Ascent / Descent)
Used far from the optimum where the surface is locally **flat / linear**.

$$Y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \varepsilon$$

- **Goal:** Identify the direction of steepest improvement.
- **Tool:** $2^k$ Factorial Design with centre points.
- **Test for curvature:** If centre-point response differs significantly from factorial-point average ŌåÆ curvature exists ŌåÆ move to Stage 2.

### Stage 2 ŌĆö Second-Order Model (Optimization Near Optimum)
Used near the optimum where **curvature is dominant**.

$$Y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum_{i<j} \sum \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \varepsilon$$

- **Goal:** Locate the stationary point precisely.
- **Tool:** Central Composite Design (CCD) or Box-Behnken Design.

## 2.3 Logical Flow of RSM

1. **Define the problem** and identify the response $Y$ and the $k$ independent variables.
2. **Choose an experimental design** (Factorial ŌåÆ CCD ŌåÆ Box-Behnken).
3. **Conduct the experiments** in a randomized order.
4. **Fit a low-order polynomial** using Least Squares.
5. **Perform ANOVA** to check model adequacy (lack of fit, $R^2$, $R^2_{\text{adj}}$).
6. **Locate the optimum** ŌĆö either analytically (set partial derivatives = 0) or graphically (contour plots).
7. **Confirm** by running confirmation runs at the predicted optimum.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Meaning / Use** |
|---|---|---|
| General RSM Model | $Y = f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k) + \varepsilon$ | True (unknown) functional relationship |
| First-Order Model | $Y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \varepsilon$ | Used in Stage 1 (Steepest Ascent) |
| Second-Order Model | $Y = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \sum \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \varepsilon$ | Used in Stage 2 (Optimization) |
| Matrix form of regression | $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ | Compact representation; $\mathbf{X}$ is the design matrix |
| Least Squares Estimator | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y}$ | Solves for unknown $\beta$ coefficients |
| Fitted Response | $\hat{Y} = \mathbf{x}'\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ | Prediction at any point $\mathbf{x}$ |
| Stationary Point Condition | $\dfrac{\partial \hat{Y}}{\partial x_i} = 0 \quad \forall i$ | Necessary condition for optimum |
| Stationary Point Solution | $\mathbf{x}_s = -\dfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ | $\mathbf{B}$ = matrix of quadratic coefficients, $\mathbf{b}$ = vector of linear coefficients |
| Curvature Test Statistic | $t = \dfrac{\bar{Y}_F - \bar{Y}_C}{s \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_F} + \frac{1}{n_C}}}$ | Checks if Stage 1 model is adequate |
| $R^2$ (Coefficient of Determination) | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{\text{Res}}}{SS_{\text{Total}}}$ | Proportion of variation explained; $\vert R^2 \vert \le 1$ |
| $R^2_{\text{Adjusted}}$ | $R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{\text{Res}}/(n-p)}{SS_{\text{Total}}/(n-1)}$ | Penalizes extra terms; better model comparator |
| Natural / Coded Variables | $x_i = \dfrac{X_i - (X_{\text{high}} + X_{\text{low}})/2}{(X_{\text{high}} - X_{\text{low}})/2}$ | Transforms to $\pm 1$ levels for numerical stability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In Second-Order RSM, the number of unknown parameters for $k$ factors is $p = \binom{k+2}{2} = \dfrac{(k+1)(k+2)}{2}$. Memorize this ŌĆö it directly determines the *minimum number of runs* for any second-order design.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Engineering:** Maximizing yield of a reactor, minimizing by-product formation.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Optimizing CNC machining parameters (speed, feed, depth) to minimize surface roughness.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Formulation optimization in tablet manufacturing.
- **Food Industry:** Optimizing baking temperature and time for crispness.

The unified benefit across all domains is that RSM delivers an **explicit, closed-form empirical model** that engineers can interrogate using calculus ŌĆö without needing to derive the underlying physics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Taylor's Series to the First-Order Model

Assume the true unknown function $Y = f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k)$ is continuous and differentiable. Expand $f(\cdot)$ about a reference point $(x_{10}, x_{20}, \dots, x_{k0})$ using the multivariate **Taylor's series expansion**:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}_0) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\bigg|_{\mathbf{x}_0} (x_i - x_{i0}) + \dfrac{1}{2!}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{k} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}\bigg|_{\mathbf{x}_0} (x_i - x_{i0})(x_j - x_{j0}) + \cdots$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Truncate after the linear terms** (i.e., assume the second-order partials are negligible when we are *far from the optimum*). This is justified because near a flat region of the response, curvature is small.

$$f(\mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_0) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\bigg|_{\mathbf{x}_0} (x_i - x_{i0})$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define new parameters** to absorb the constants:
- Let $\beta_0 = f(\mathbf{x}_0) - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\big|_{\mathbf{x}_0}\, x_{i0}$
- Let $\beta_i = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\big|_{\mathbf{x}_0}$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute** these into the truncated expansion:

$$Y \approx \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \varepsilon$$

This is the **First-Order RSM Model**, where $\varepsilon$ captures both the experimental noise and the Taylor truncation error.

## 3.2 Derivation: Second-Order Model by Retaining Quadratic Terms

**Step 1 ŌĆö Now retain terms up to second order** in the Taylor expansion (because we are *near the optimum* where curvature is significant):

$$f(\mathbf{x}) \approx \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{k} \beta_{ij}\, x_i x_j$$

where $\beta_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}\big|_{\mathbf{x}_0}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expand the double summation** explicitly for clarity ($i = j$ gives the squared terms; $i \ne j$ gives the cross-product terms):

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum_{i < j} \beta_{ij} x_i x_j$$

This is the canonical **Second-Order RSM Model**. Note that $\beta_{ii}$ here corresponds to $\frac{1}{2}$ of the standard Taylor coefficient ŌĆö a notational convention universally adopted in RSM literature.

**Step 3 ŌĆö The total number of unknown coefficients** for $k$ factors is:

$$p = 1 + k + k + \binom{k}{2} = 1 + 2k + \dfrac{k(k-1)}{2} = \dfrac{(k+1)(k+2)}{2}$$

For $k = 2$: $p = 6$. For $k = 3$: $p = 10$. For $k = 4$: $p = 15$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Least Squares Estimator of Coefficients

Given $n$ experimental runs, the model is written compactly in **matrix form**:

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{y}$ is an $(n \times 1)$ vector of observed responses.
- $\mathbf{X}$ is an $(n \times p)$ design matrix whose $i$-th row contains the polynomial terms for the $i$-th run.
- $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is a $(p \times 1)$ vector of unknown coefficients.
- $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ is an $(n \times 1)$ error vector.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the residual vector:** $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Form the Least Squares objective ŌĆö minimize the Sum of Squared Errors:**

$$S(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \mathbf{e}'\mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})'(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})$$

$$S(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \mathbf{y}'\mathbf{y} - 2\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}'\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y} + \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}'\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Differentiate $S$ with respect to $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ and set equal to zero:**

$$\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}} = -2\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$** (the so-called *Normal Equations*):

$$\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y}$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The matrix $\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}$ is called the *information matrix*. Its inverse, $(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}$, is the *dispersion matrix* of $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$. The choice of design directly controls the *precision* of the estimates ŌĆö a central theme in KTU Module 4.

## 3.4 Derivation: Stationary Point of the Second-Order Surface

Write the fitted second-order model in **matrix notation**:

$$\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \mathbf{x}'\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}'\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x}$ is a $(k \times 1)$ vector of factor levels.
- $\mathbf{b}$ is a $(k \times 1)$ vector of linear coefficients.
- $\mathbf{B}$ is a $(k \times k)$ symmetric matrix of quadratic / interaction coefficients.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Take the gradient (vector of partial derivatives) and equate to zero:**

$$\dfrac{\partial \hat{Y}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{b} + 2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_s = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve for the stationary point $\mathbf{x}_s$:**

$$\mathbf{x}_s = -\dfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** The *nature* of the stationary point is determined by the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{B}$:
> - All eigenvalues **negative** ŌåÆ **Maximum** (response surface is a hill).
> - All eigenvalues **positive** ŌåÆ **Minimum** (response surface is a bowl).
> - Eigenvalues of **mixed sign** ŌåÆ **Saddle Point** (no optimum in region ŌĆö re-design needed).

The **predicted response at the stationary point** is obtained by substituting $\mathbf{x}_s$ back:

$$\hat{Y}_s = \hat{\beta}_0 + \dfrac{1}{2}\,\mathbf{x}_s'\,\mathbf{b}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of RSM

The following Mermaid flowchart shows the iterative architecture of the Response Surface Methodology, including the curvature-driven transition from Stage 1 to Stage 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Define Problem and Identify Factors"] --> B["Select First-Order Experimental Design e.g. 2k Factorial with Centre Points"]
    B --> C["Conduct Experiments in Randomized Order"]
    C --> D["Fit First-Order Polynomial Model Y = Beta0 + Sum Betai xi"]
    D --> E["Test for Lack of Fit and Curvature using ANOVA"]
    E --> F{Curvature Significant?}
    F -- No --> G["Compute Direction of Steepest Ascent or Descent"]
    G --> H["Move Along Steepest Ascent Path with New Runs"]
    H --> C
    F -- Yes --> I["Switch to Second-Order Design e.g. Central Composite or Box Behnken"]
    I --> J["Fit Second-Order Polynomial with Interaction and Quadratic Terms"]
    J --> K["Perform ANOVA and R-Squared Check"]
    K --> L["Compute Stationary Point xs = Minus Half B Inverse b"]
    L --> M{Nature of Stationary Point}
    M -- Maximum --> N["Report Optimum with Confirmation Runs"]
    M -- Minimum --> N
    M -- Saddle Point --> O["Rescale Region or Add Constraints and Redesign"]
    O --> I
    N --> P["End of Optimization"]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Mapping of the Two-Stage Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage1["STAGE 1: Steepest Ascent Region"]
        S1A["Linear Region Flat Surface"] --> S1B["First-Order Model Y equals Beta0 plus Sum Betai xi"]
        S1B --> S1C["Direction Vector d equals sign of b"]
        S1C --> S1D["Steps along Gradient"]
    end
    subgraph Stage2["STAGE 2: Optimum Region"]
        S2A["Curved Region Convex or Concave"] --> S2B["Second-Order Model Y equals Beta0 plus Linear plus Quadratic plus Interaction"]
        S2B --> S2C["Stationary Point xs equals minus half B inverse b"]
        S2C --> S2D["Classify by Eigenvalues of B"]
    end
    Stage1 --> Stage2
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Response Surface Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Quadratic Form of Second-Order Surface"] --> R{Eigenvalues of B}
    R -- All Positive --> R1["Bowl Shape MINIMUM"]
    R -- All Negative --> R2["Dome Shape MAXIMUM"]
    R -- Mixed Signs --> R3["Saddle Shape NO INTERIOR OPTIMUM"]
    R -- One Nonzero and Others Zero --> R4["Ridge or Valley STEEPEST DESCENT REQUIRED"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams:** Each block represents a *computational or experimental action* in RSM. The arrows denote the **logical flow of decisions**. This functional block-diagram representation is KTU-friendly as it focuses on *process logic* rather than abstract mathematics, making it directly relevant to Part B descriptive questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Style]**
Define **Response Surface Methodology**. Mention any two situations in engineering where RSM is preferred over classical one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) experimentation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Response Surface Methodology (RSM) is a collection of mathematical and statistical techniques used to develop, improve, and optimize processes by fitting a low-order polynomial (first or second order) to the response obtained from a set of designed experiments, thereby approximating the true but unknown functional relationship between the response and the independent variables.

**Two situations where RSM is preferred over OFAT (1 mark):**
1. When the interaction effects between factors are suspected to be significant ŌĆö OFAT cannot detect interactions.
2. When the goal is to find an **optimal combination** of factor levels rather than just the individual effect of each factor ŌĆö RSM provides a predictive model from which the optimum can be derived using calculus.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Style]**
State the **second-order response surface model** for $k = 3$ independent variables. How many unknown parameters does this model contain?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Model (2 marks):** For $k = 3$, let the variables be $x_1, x_2, x_3$. The second-order model is:

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \beta_3 x_3 + \beta_{11} x_1^2 + \beta_{22} x_2^2 + \beta_{33} x_3^2 + \beta_{12} x_1 x_2 + \beta_{13} x_1 x_3 + \beta_{23} x_2 x_3 + \varepsilon$$

**Number of parameters (1 mark):** Using the formula $p = \dfrac{(k+1)(k+2)}{2}$, for $k = 3$:

$$p = \dfrac{(3+1)(3+2)}{2} = \dfrac{4 \times 5}{2} = 10 \text{ parameters}$$

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
### Internal Choice: Answer **either** Question A or Question B.

---

### **Question A ŌĆö [14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **two-stage strategy of Box and Wilson** in Response Surface Methodology. Describe how the **method of steepest ascent** is used to move from a first-order region toward the optimum.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider a process with two factors $x_1$ and $x_2$. The fitted second-order model in coded variables is:

$$\hat{Y} = 50 + 4x_1 + 6x_2 - 2x_1^2 - 3x_2^2 + x_1 x_2$$

**Find the stationary point and classify its nature.**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) ŌĆö Two-Stage Strategy of Box-Wilson [7 marks]**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Box and Wilson (1951) proposed RSM as a sequential procedure that uses *different* polynomial orders in two distinct stages. The basic premise is that an experimenter, far from the optimum, can use a simple linear model to *navigate* the factor space, then switch to a more elaborate quadratic model once the *neighborhood of the optimum* is reached.

**Stage 1 ŌĆö First-Order Region (3 marks):**

1. A $2^k$ factorial design (with centre points) is run at the current region of interest.
2. A first-order model $Y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \varepsilon$ is fitted.
3. **Test for curvature:** Compare the average response at factorial points ($\bar{Y}_F$) with the average response at centre points ($\bar{Y}_C$). If $| \bar{Y}_F - \bar{Y}_C \vert$ is statistically significant, curvature is present, and the experimenter must switch to Stage 2.
4. If no curvature, the linear model is adequate for *this* region, and the direction of steepest ascent is calculated.

**Method of Steepest Ascent (2 marks):**

- The **direction vector** of steepest ascent is proportional to the linear coefficients $\beta_1, \beta_2, \dots, \beta_k$.
- Specifically, the change in $x_i$ per unit step is proportional to $\beta_i$: $\Delta x_i \propto \beta_i$.
- One practical scaling: set $\Delta x_k = 1$ and compute $\Delta x_i = (\beta_i / \beta_k) \Delta x_k$.
- New experiments are conducted at points along this direction until the response **no longer improves** ŌĆö this identifies the new region of interest.

**Stage 2 ŌĆö Second-Order Region (1 mark):** A second-order design (CCD or Box-Behnken) is now run in the new region, and the quadratic model is fitted for precise optimization.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) ŌĆö Stationary Point Calculation [7 marks]**

**Given (1 mark):**
$$\hat{Y} = 50 + 4x_1 + 6x_2 - 2x_1^2 - 3x_2^2 + x_1 x_2$$

So $\hat{\beta}_0 = 50$, $\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix}$, and $\mathbf{B} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Solve for the stationary point (3 marks):** Using $\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$:

$$\det(\mathbf{B}) = (-2)(-3) - (0.5)(0.5) = 6 - 0.25 = 5.75$$

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{5.75}\begin{pmatrix} -3 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = \dfrac{1}{5.75}\begin{pmatrix} -3 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & -2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{5.75}\begin{pmatrix} -15 \\ -14 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -2.6087 \\ -2.4348 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{x}_s = -\dfrac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} -2.6087 \\ -2.4348 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.3043 \\ 1.2174 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Predicted response at the stationary point (1 mark):**

$$\hat{Y}_s = 50 + \dfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_s'\mathbf{b} = 50 + \dfrac{1}{2}(1.3043)(4) + \dfrac{1}{2}(1.2174)(6)$$

$$\hat{Y}_s = 50 + 2.6087 + 3.6522 = 56.2609$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Classify the stationary point using eigenvalues (2 marks):** Solve $\det(\mathbf{B} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$:

$$\det\begin{pmatrix} -2 - \lambda & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & -3 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = 0$$

$$(-2-\lambda)(-3-\lambda) - 0.25 = 0$$
$$\lambda^2 + 5\lambda + 6 - 0.25 = 0$$
$$\lambda^2 + 5\lambda + 5.75 = 0$$
$$\lambda = \dfrac{-5 \pm \sqrt{25 - 23}}{2} = \dfrac{-5 \pm \sqrt{2}}{2}$$
$$\lambda_1 = \dfrac{-5 + 1.414}{2} = -1.793, \quad \lambda_2 = \dfrac{-5 - 1.414}{2} = -3.207$$

**Both eigenvalues are negative** ŌåÆ the stationary point is a **MAXIMUM**. The maximum predicted response is $\hat{Y}_s \approx 56.26$ at $(x_1, x_2) = (1.30, 1.22)$.

---

### **Question B ŌĆö [14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **Least Squares Estimator** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y}$ from the matrix form of the linear regression model. State the assumptions required for this estimator to be the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **Central Composite Design (CCD)**. List its three constituent parts and explain why CCD is the most popular second-order design in RSM.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) ŌĆö Derivation of LSE [7 marks]**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Linearity: $E(\mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$.
2. Homoscedasticity & Independence: $\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$.
3. $\mathbf{X}$ is of full rank (column rank = $p$).
4. Errors are normally distributed: $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I})$.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Model in matrix form:** $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$, where $\mathbf{y}$ is $n \times 1$, $\mathbf{X}$ is $n \times p$, $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is $p \times 1$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define residuals:** $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sum of squared residuals:**
$$S(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \mathbf{e}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y}'\mathbf{y} - 2\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}'\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y} + \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}'\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Differentiate w.r.t. $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ and equate to zero:**
$$\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}} = -2\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve the normal equations:**
$$\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y} \implies \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y}$$

**Why BLUE? (1 mark):** Under the Gauss-Markov assumptions listed above, the LSE is:
- **Linear:** because $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ is a linear combination of $\mathbf{y}$.
- **Unbiased:** because $E(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \boldsymbol{\beta}$.
- **Best:** has minimum variance among all linear unbiased estimators.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) ŌĆö Central Composite Design [7 marks]**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Central Composite Design (CCD), introduced by Box and Wilson (1951), is a **second-order design** that efficiently fits a quadratic response surface. It is widely used in industrial RSM applications.

**Three Constituent Parts (3 marks):**

| Part | Description | Number of Runs |
|---|---|---|
| **Factorial Points** | A $2^k$ full factorial (or fractional $2^{k-1}$) at levels $\pm 1$ | $2^k$ |
| **Axial (Star) Points** | Points along each axis at $\pm \alpha$ with all other factors at 0 | $2k$ |
| **Centre Points** | Replicated runs at $(0, 0, \dots, 0)$ | $n_c$ (typically 5ŌĆō6) |

**Total runs:** $N = 2^k + 2k + n_c$.

**Choice of $\alpha$ (1 mark):** For a **rotatable** CCD (where prediction variance is constant on spheres), $\alpha = (2^k)^{1/4}$. For $k = 3$: $\alpha = (8)^{1/4} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

**Why CCD is the most popular second-order design (1 mark):**
1. **Sequential** ŌĆö the factorial portion can be augmented with axial and centre points to upgrade a first-order design to second order.
2. **Rotatable** ŌĆö with proper $\alpha$, the design gives equal precision of prediction in all directions from the centre.
3. **Efficient** ŌĆö fewer runs than a full $3^k$ factorial for the same estimation accuracy.
4. **Flexible** ŌĆö can be made orthogonal or face-centered ($\alpha = 1$) for bounded regions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Most common error in Q.A(b):** Forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{2}$ in the second-order expansion when converting to matrix form. RSM convention uses $\beta_{ii}$ to absorb the $\frac{1}{2}$ from Taylor's series. Students who write $\hat{Y} = \beta_0 + \mathbf{b}'\mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}'\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$ and forget to absorb the half will compute $\mathbf{x}_s = -\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ (incorrect) instead of $\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.
> - **Most common error in Q.B(a):** Skipping the assumptions. KTU expects *explicit listing* of Gauss-Markov conditions before deriving the LSE. Marks are deducted if "BLUE" is claimed without justification.
> - **Most common error in Q.B(b):** Confusing CCD with Box-Behnken Design (BBD). CCD has *axial* points; BBD does *not* have axial points and stays within the original factorial cube. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU OECHT727 Module 4 (RSM Introduction)**

- [ ] **RSM = Optimization tool**, not a screening tool. Use it *after* significant factors have been identified by factorial / fractional factorial designs.
- [ ] **Two-Stage Strategy (Box-Wilson 1951):** Stage 1 = First-order model + Steepest Ascent. Stage 2 = Second-order model + Stationary Point analysis.
- [ ] **First-order model:** $Y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \varepsilon$. Used far from optimum.
- [ ] **Second-order model:** $Y = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \sum \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \varepsilon$. Used near optimum.
- [ ] **Number of parameters:** $p = \dfrac{(k+1)(k+2)}{2}$. Memorize this ŌĆö it is the *minimum number of runs* for any second-order design.
- [ ] **Least Squares Estimator:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y}$. Requires $\mathbf{X}$ to be full-rank.
- [ ] **Stationary Point formula:** $\mathbf{x}_s = -\dfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$. Always include the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor (RSM convention).
- [ ] **Classification of stationary point** via eigenvalues of $\mathbf{B}$:
  - All $\lambda_i < 0$ ŌåÆ **Maximum**
  - All $\lambda_i > 0$ ŌåÆ **Minimum**
  - Mixed signs ŌåÆ **Saddle point**
- [ ] **Coded variables** ($x_i \in [-1, +1]$) are preferred over natural variables for numerical stability and rotatability.
- [ ] **Curvature Test:** If $\bar{Y}_F$ differs significantly from $\bar{Y}_C$ ŌåÆ first-order model inadequate ŌåÆ upgrade to second-order design.
- [ ] **$R^2$ vs $R^2_{\text{adj}}$:** Always prefer $R^2_{\text{adj}}$ when comparing models with different numbers of terms ŌĆö $R^2$ *always* increases with added terms, even useless ones.
- [ ] **Gauss-Markov Assumptions** (for BLUE): Linearity, Independence, Homoscedasticity ($\sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$), and Full-rank design matrix.
- [ ] **CCD total runs:** $N = 2^k + 2k + n_c$. For rotatable CCD: $\alpha = (2^k)^{1/4}$.
- [ ] **Difference: CCD vs Box-Behnken:** CCD has axial points at $\pm \alpha$; BBD does not and is therefore always *within* the original factor bounds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Steepest Ascent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Method of Steepest Ascent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The **Method of Steepest Ascent** is a sequential optimization procedure employed in the Response Surface Methodology (RSM) of Design of Experiments (DoE). It is used to navigate the experimental region efficiently when the analyst is operating **far from the optimal region** (i.e., far from the maximum or minimum of the response). The method works by fitting a **first-order linear model** to the response data obtained from a 2-level factorial design, computing the direction of the gradient of this fitted surface, and then performing a series of experiments by moving the factor levels along this gradient direction in steps of a chosen size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Method of Steepest Ascent is the second stage of the classical Box-Wilson optimization strategy. Stage 1 is the **2^k Factorial Design with Center Points**, and Stage 2 is **Method of Steepest Ascent**. Stage 3, when curvature is detected, is **Second-Order (Response Surface) Design**.

## Mathematical Statement

Given a first-order model fitted to the data:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i x_i$$

The **direction of steepest ascent** is the vector of regression coefficients $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$, normalized such that the move in the coded factors is proportional to the magnitudes of the coefficients.

## Conceptual Analogy: The Blind Mountain Climber

Imagine you are standing on the slope of a vast mountain range on a foggy night. You cannot see the summit, but you want to climb to the highest peak (the optimum). What do you do?

- **You feel the ground beneath your feet.** That is equivalent to running a 2^k factorial experiment and collecting response values at strategic points.
- **You measure the local slope.** The local tilt of the ground tells you the direction in which the elevation rises fastest. This is the **gradient vector**, computed from the coefficients of the first-order model.
- **You take a fixed-size step uphill.** Because you cannot see far, you take a reasonable step ($\Delta$) in the steepest uphill direction. Each step is a new experiment.
- **You stop when the slope flattens.** Once additional steps no longer improve the response, you are near the ridge/peak. This is when you switch to a **second-order design** to find the exact optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Analogy (Chemical Engineering):** Suppose a chemical engineer is trying to maximize the yield (%) of a chemical reaction. Two factors ŌĆö temperature (┬░C) and catalyst concentration (%). The current operating conditions give a low yield. By performing a 2┬▓ factorial, fitting a first-order model, and following the steepest ascent path, the engineer rapidly converges to the region of maximum yield without running hundreds of random experiments.

## Key Terminology (KTU Mandatory Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **First-Order Model** | A linear polynomial model $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \Sigma \hat{\beta}_i x_i$ that approximates the response surface locally. |
| **Gradient Vector** | The vector of first-order regression coefficients that points in the direction of maximum rate of change of the response. |
| **Step Size ($\Delta$)** | A user-defined magnitude of a single move along the gradient path, expressed in coded factor units. |
| **Path of Steepest Ascent** | The sequence of experimental runs conducted by varying all factors simultaneously, proportional to their regression coefficients. |
| **Curvature** | The departure from linearity detected by replicated center points; when present, the first-order model is inadequate and a second-order design is required. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## When and Why is the Method of Steepest Ascent Used?

The method is invoked when the following two conditions are satisfied simultaneously:

1. The factorial design (typically $2^k$) analysis of variance (ANOVA) reveals **lack of curvature** in the current region (i.e., center point response is statistically equivalent to factorial point responses).
2. The factor main effects and/or interactions are **statistically significant**, meaning the response surface is tilted and a meaningful direction of improvement exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Cue:** The standard Board Exam trigger question is: *"What test confirms that the current region has no curvature?"* The answer is the **t-test for curvature** comparing the factorial point average with the center point average.

## Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The Method of Steepest Ascent is a 5-step algorithmic procedure:

### Step 1 ŌĆö Fit a First-Order Model
Using ordinary least squares (OLS), regress the observed response $y$ on the coded factor levels $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k$ to obtain:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_1 + \hat{\beta}_2 x_2 + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_k x_k$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the Gradient Direction
The direction of steepest ascent is along the vector of regression coefficients:

$$\mathbf{d} = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$$

In coded variables, the experimenter moves in the direction $\mathbf{d}$ such that the move in each factor is **proportional** to its corresponding coefficient.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Choose a Step Size ($\Delta$)
The experimenter selects a step size based on practical considerations (e.g., production limits, equipment safety). A common convention is to set $\Delta$ such that the factor with the **largest absolute coefficient** changes by exactly one unit in coded terms.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the Actual Moves in Original Variables
For each factor $i$, the move in coded units is:

$$\Delta x_i^{coded} = \frac{\hat{\beta}_i}{|\hat{\beta}_i|_{\max}} \cdot \Delta$$

This is then converted to natural (uncoded) units:

$$\Delta x_i^{natural} = \Delta x_i^{coded} \cdot \left(\frac{x_{i,\max} - x_{i,\min}}{2}\right)$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Execute Sequential Experiments
Starting from the center of the original factorial, new experimental points are generated as:

$$x_{i,\text{new}} = x_{i,\text{center}} + n \cdot \Delta x_i^{natural}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

The experimenter runs experiments at $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ until the response no longer improves. This identifies the new center for the next factorial design.

## KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Purpose / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First-Order Model | $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \Sigma \hat{\beta}_i x_i$ | Local linear approximation of the response. |
| 2 | OLS Estimator of $\hat{\beta}_i$ | $\hat{\beta}_i = \dfrac{\Sigma x_i y}{N}$ | Where $N = 2^k$ runs of the orthogonal $2^k$ design. |
| 3 | Steepest Ascent Direction (Coded) | $\Delta x_i = \dfrac{\hat{\beta}_i}{\vert \hat{\beta}_i \vert_{\max}} \cdot \Delta$ | $\Delta$ is the user-chosen step size in coded units. |
| 4 | Conversion to Natural Units | $\Delta x_i^{nat} = \Delta x_i^{coded} \cdot \dfrac{\text{Range}_i}{2}$ | Range = High level ŌłÆ Low level. |
| 5 | New Experimental Point | $x_{i,\text{new}} = x_{i,\text{center}} + n \cdot \Delta x_i^{nat}$ | Sequential walk along the gradient. |
| 6 | Curvature Test Statistic | $t = \dfrac{\bar{y}_F - \bar{y}_C}{\sqrt{MSE \left(\dfrac{1}{n_F} + \dfrac{1}{n_C}\right)}}$ | If $\vert t \vert > t_{\alpha/2, df}$, curvature exists. |
| 7 | Stopping Condition | $\hat{y}_{n+1} < \hat{y}_n$ | Stop when the response begins to decline. |
| 8 | Stopping Condition (Engineering) | Process constraint or budget exhausted | Practical termination criterion. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This method is the backbone of process optimization in semiconductor manufacturing (yield improvement), pharmaceutical formulation (maximizing drug dissolution), and petroleum refining (catalyst activity tuning). It minimizes the number of expensive pilot-plant experiments required to reach the optimal operating window.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Coefficients Give the Gradient Direction

Let the true (unknown) response surface near the current region be approximated as:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_1 + \hat{\beta}_2 x_2 + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_k x_k$$

The directional derivative of $\hat{y}$ in the direction of a unit vector $\mathbf{u} = (u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_k)$ subject to the constraint $\Sigma u_i^2 = 1$ is:

$$D_{\mathbf{u}} \hat{y} = \nabla \hat{y} \cdot \mathbf{u} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i u_i$$

To maximize $D_{\mathbf{u}} \hat{y}$, we apply the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i u_i \leq \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k} u_i^2} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i^2}$$

Equality holds if and only if $\mathbf{u}$ is **parallel** to $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$, i.e.,

$$u_i = \frac{\hat{\beta}_i}{\sqrt{\Sigma \hat{\beta}_i^2}}$$

Therefore, the direction of **maximum increase** of $\hat{y}$ is along the vector of regression coefficients themselves. This justifies the canonical rule: *move proportional to the regression coefficients.*

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

### Problem Setup

A chemical engineer studies the effect of **Reaction Temperature** ($x_1$) and **Catalyst Concentration** ($x_2$) on the percentage **Yield** of a product. A $2^2$ factorial design with 4 corner points and 3 center points (for curvature check) was conducted.

| Run | $x_1$ (Temp, ┬░C) | $x_2$ (Catalyst, %) | Yield $y$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 80 (Low, ŌłÆ1) | 2.0 (Low, ŌłÆ1) | 39 |
| 2 | 100 (High, +1) | 2.0 (Low, ŌłÆ1) | 52 |
| 3 | 80 (Low, ŌłÆ1) | 4.0 (High, +1) | 51 |
| 4 | 100 (High, +1) | 4.0 (High, +1) | 65 |
| 5 | 90 (Center, 0) | 3.0 (Center, 0) | 60 |
| 6 | 90 (Center, 0) | 3.0 (Center, 0) | 61 |
| 7 | 90 (Center, 0) | 3.0 (Center, 0) | 60 |

### Step 1 ŌĆö Fit the First-Order Model in Coded Variables

In coded form, the design matrix is orthogonal. The OLS estimators are:

$$\hat{\beta}_0 = \frac{\Sigma y}{N} = \frac{39 + 52 + 51 + 65}{4} = 51.75$$

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\Sigma x_1 y}{N} = \frac{(-1)(39) + (+1)(52) + (-1)(51) + (+1)(65)}{4} = \frac{27}{4} = 6.75$$

$$\hat{\beta}_2 = \frac{\Sigma x_2 y}{N} = \frac{(-1)(39) + (-1)(52) + (+1)(51) + (+1)(65)}{4} = \frac{25}{4} = 6.25$$

The fitted first-order model is:

$$\hat{y} = 51.75 + 6.75\,x_1 + 6.25\,x_2$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Curvature Test (Center Point Comparison)

$$\bar{y}_{\text{factorial}} = 51.75, \quad \bar{y}_{\text{center}} = \frac{60 + 61 + 60}{3} = 60.333$$

Difference $= \bar{y}_{\text{center}} - \bar{y}_{\text{factorial}} = 60.333 - 51.75 = 8.583$. This large positive deviation indicates the presence of **curvature** (the true surface is not flat). 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a strict KTU Board Exam, this curvature finding would actually direct the analyst to proceed directly to a **second-order (Central Composite or Box-Behnken) design** rather than steepest ascent. However, for pedagogical demonstration, we continue with steepest ascent to illustrate the method.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Choose Step Size

Suppose the engineer wishes the **temperature** (the factor with the largest coefficient magnitude) to increase by approximately **5 ┬░C per step** (one-quarter of the temperature range of 20 ┬░C, i.e., 5/10 = 0.5 in coded units). 

$$\Delta = 0.5 \quad (\text{coded})$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the Coded Moves

The maximum coefficient is $\vert \hat{\beta}_1 \vert = 6.75$, so:

$$\Delta x_1 = \frac{6.75}{6.75} \times 0.5 = 0.50 \quad \text{(coded)}$$

$$\Delta x_2 = \frac{6.25}{6.75} \times 0.5 = 0.463 \quad \text{(coded)}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Convert to Natural (Uncoded) Units

$$\Delta x_1^{nat} = 0.50 \times \frac{100 - 80}{2} = 0.50 \times 10 = 5\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\Delta x_2^{nat} = 0.463 \times \frac{4.0 - 2.0}{2} = 0.463 \times 1.0 = 0.463\ \%$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Construct the Steepest Ascent Path

Starting from the current center (90 ┬░C, 3.0%), generate the path:

| Step $n$ | Temperature (┬░C) | Catalyst (%) | Predicted $\hat{y}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 90.0 | 3.000 | 60.33 |
| 1 | 95.0 | 3.463 | ŌĆö |
| 2 | 100.0 | 3.926 | ŌĆö |
| 3 | 105.0 | 4.389 | ŌĆö |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |

The experimenter runs actual experiments at each new point until $\hat{y}$ or the actual $y$ begins to decrease.

## 3.3 Summary Matrix of the Analytical Procedure

| Stage | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-condition | Run $2^k$ + center points | Data with curvature test |
| Step 1 | Fit $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \Sigma \hat{\beta}_i x_i$ | Coefficients $\hat{\beta}_i$ |
| Step 2 | Compute gradient vector | $\mathbf{d} = (\hat{\beta}_1, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$ |
| Step 3 | Choose $\Delta$ | Step size in coded units |
| Step 4 | Compute coded moves $\Delta x_i$ | Vector of proportional moves |
| Step 5 | Convert to natural units | Engineering-scale moves |
| Step 6 | Walk the path | New experiments until response drops |
| Termination | Locate new optimum region | Begin next factorial design |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: 2^k Factorial Done] --> B[Add Center Points]
    B --> C{Curvature Test}
    C -- Curvature Detected --> D[Go to Second-Order Design]
    C -- No Curvature --> E[Fit First-Order Model]
    E --> F[Estimate Regression Coefficients beta_i]
    F --> G[Compute Gradient Vector d = beta_1 ... beta_k]
    G --> H[Select Step Size Delta in Coded Units]
    H --> I[Compute Coded Moves Delta_x_i]
    I --> J[Convert Coded Moves to Natural Units]
    J --> K[Set n = 1]
    K --> L[Run Experiment at x_center + n times Delta_x_natural]
    L --> M{Response Improved?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Increment n by 1]
    N --> L
    M -- No --> O[Stop: New Optimum Region Located]
    O --> P[Run New 2^k Factorial at New Center]
    P --> Q[End]
    D --> Q
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram ŌĆö Inputs, Process, Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        A1[2^k Factorial Data]
        A2[Center Point Data]
        A3[Factor Ranges]
    end
    subgraph Process
        B1[OLS Estimation]
        B2[Gradient Computation]
        B3[Step Size Selection]
        B4[Coordinate Conversion]
    end
    subgraph Outputs
        C1[Steepest Ascent Path]
        C2[Predicted Response Curve]
        C3[New Operating Region]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    A3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Initial 2^k Design Completed] --> X2{Is Lack of Fit Significant?}
    X2 -- Yes --> X3[Higher Order Design Required]
    X2 -- No --> X4{Are Main Effects Significant?}
    X4 -- No --> X5[Response is Flat: No Improvement Possible]
    X4 -- Yes --> X6[Apply Method of Steepest Ascent]
    X6 --> X7[Walk Along Gradient]
    X7 --> X8{Response Still Increasing?}
    X8 -- Yes --> X7
    X8 -- No --> X9[Stop at Approximate Optimum]
    X9 --> X10[Run Second-Order RSM Design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The mermaid diagrams above represent the **functional control flow** of the steepest ascent algorithm as executed by a DoE analyst. In a physical experiment, the "blocks" correspond to mathematical computation steps, and the "data flow arrows" represent information passed between successive stages of the analysis pipeline.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Model Question]**

> **Q:** Define the *Method of Steepest Ascent* in Design of Experiments. Under what condition is this method applied after a 2^k factorial design?

**Model Answer:**

The Method of Steepest Ascent is a sequential optimization procedure used in Response Surface Methodology to move the experimental conditions toward the region of optimum response. It begins by fitting a first-order model $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \Sigma \hat{\beta}_i x_i$ to data from a 2^k factorial design, identifying the gradient vector of regression coefficients as the direction of maximum increase of the response, and then performing a series of experiments by moving the factor levels along this direction in fixed steps.

**Condition of application:** It is applied **only when the analysis of the 2^k design confirms absence of significant curvature** (i.e., the center point mean is not significantly different from the factorial point mean), AND **at least one main effect (or interaction) is statistically significant**. This combination indicates that the response surface is locally linear and tilted, justifying a directional walk toward the optimum.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **Q:** In the Method of Steepest Ascent, how is the step size chosen? Explain with the help of the formula used to compute the move in each factor.

**Model Answer:**

The step size $\Delta$ is chosen by the experimenter based on practical considerations such as the process constraints, equipment operating range, cost per experimental run, and the desired resolution of the optimization. A common convention is to set $\Delta$ such that the factor with the **largest absolute regression coefficient** changes by approximately **one coded unit** per step.

The move in each coded factor is computed as:

$$\Delta x_i = \frac{\hat{\beta}_i}{\vert \hat{\beta}_i \vert_{\max}} \cdot \Delta$$

This is then converted to natural (uncoded) units using:

$$\Delta x_i^{nat} = \Delta x_i^{coded} \cdot \frac{x_{i,\text{high}} - x_{i,\text{low}}}{2}$$

The experimenter generates new experimental points by adding $n \cdot \Delta x_i^{nat}$ to the center of the original factorial design, sequentially for $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$, and continues until the response no longer improves.

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö KTU ESE Internal Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Model Question]**

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the step-by-step procedure of the **Method of Steepest Ascent** as used in Response Surface Methodology. Clearly state the pre-conditions, the role of the first-order model, and the stopping criterion.

> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A 2┬▓ factorial design with center points was conducted to study the effect of Temperature ($x_1$) and Pressure ($x_2$) on the Yield of a chemical. The fitted first-order model is $\hat{y} = 45.5 + 4.2\,x_1 + 3.6\,x_2$. The original factor levels were: $x_1$ ŌĆö Low = 60 ┬░C, High = 100 ┬░C; $x_2$ ŌĆö Low = 2 atm, High = 6 atm. Choose a step size of $\Delta = 0.5$ coded units and compute the first three points on the steepest ascent path in natural units.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1: Pre-Condition Check.** Verify that the 2^k factorial analysis shows no significant curvature (i.e., center point response is not significantly different from the factorial response). Verify that at least one main effect is significant, indicating a tilted response surface.

**Step 2: Fit First-Order Model.** Using OLS, obtain the coefficients $\hat{\beta}_i$:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i x_i$$

**Step 3: Compute Gradient Direction.** The vector of regression coefficients $(\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$ defines the direction of steepest ascent.

**Step 4: Choose Step Size.** Select $\Delta$ based on practical considerations, typically $\Delta = 0.5$ or $1.0$ in coded units.

**Step 5: Compute Coded Moves.**

$$\Delta x_i = \frac{\hat{\beta}_i}{\vert \hat{\beta}_i \vert_{\max}} \cdot \Delta$$

**Step 6: Convert to Natural Units.**

$$\Delta x_i^{nat} = \Delta x_i^{coded} \cdot \frac{\text{Range}_i}{2}$$

**Step 7: Sequential Walk.** Starting from the center of the original factorial, run experiments at $x_{i,\text{new}} = x_{i,\text{center}} + n \cdot \Delta x_i^{nat}$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$.

**Step 8: Stopping Criterion.** Stop when the observed response begins to decrease (i.e., $\hat{y}_{n+1} < \hat{y}_n$) OR when a process constraint is hit OR when the experimental budget is exhausted. The stopping point is the approximate location of the optimum, where a new second-order design should be initiated.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- Fitted model: $\hat{y} = 45.5 + 4.2\,x_1 + 3.6\,x_2$
- $\hat{\beta}_1 = 4.2$, $\hat{\beta}_2 = 3.6$, $\vert \hat{\beta}_i \vert_{\max} = 4.2$
- Step size $\Delta = 0.5$ coded units
- $x_1$ range: $100 - 60 = 40$ ┬░C $\Rightarrow$ half-range = 20 ┬░C
- $x_2$ range: $6 - 2 = 4$ atm $\Rightarrow$ half-range = 2 atm
- Center: $x_1 = 80$ ┬░C, $x_2 = 4$ atm

**Compute coded moves:**

$$\Delta x_1 = \frac{4.2}{4.2} \times 0.5 = 0.50$$

$$\Delta x_2 = \frac{3.6}{4.2} \times 0.5 = 0.4286$$

**Convert to natural units:**

$$\Delta x_1^{nat} = 0.50 \times 20 = 10\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\Delta x_2^{nat} = 0.4286 \times 2 = 0.857\ \text{atm}$$

**Construct the steepest ascent path:**

| Step $n$ | Temperature (┬░C) | Pressure (atm) | Predicted $\hat{y}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (Center) | 80.0 | 4.000 | 45.5 |
| 1 | 90.0 | 4.857 | $45.5 + 4.2(0.5) + 3.6(0.4286) = 49.14$ |
| 2 | 100.0 | 5.714 | $45.5 + 4.2(1.0) + 3.6(0.857) = 52.78$ |
| 3 | 110.0 | 6.571 | $45.5 + 4.2(1.5) + 3.6(1.286) = 56.43$ |

(Note: At step 3, the pressure exceeds 6 atm; in practice the engineer would truncate or restart the design once a constraint is hit.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö KTU ESE Internal Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain how the regression coefficients of a first-order model determine the direction of steepest ascent. Prove mathematically that the maximum rate of increase of the response occurs along the gradient vector of the coefficients.

> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A 2┬│ factorial design was conducted to study three factors $A$, $B$, $C$ on the percentage conversion of a reaction. The fitted first-order model (in coded units) is $\hat{y} = 60 + 5.0\,A + 3.5\,B + 1.2\,C$. The natural ranges are: $A$: 50ŌĆō70 ┬░C, $B$: 1ŌĆō3 %, $C$: 10ŌĆō20 units. Using a step size of $\Delta = 1.0$ coded unit, determine the steepest ascent path and verify whether the assumption of linear (first-order) model is reasonable.

#### Model Solution to (a)

Let the fitted first-order response surface be:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_1 + \hat{\beta}_2 x_2 + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_k x_k$$

The gradient of $\hat{y}$ with respect to the factor vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)$ is:

$$\nabla \hat{y} = \left(\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_1}, \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_2}, \ldots, \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_k}\right) = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$$

The directional derivative of $\hat{y}$ in the direction of a unit vector $\mathbf{u} = (u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_k)$ (with $\Sigma u_i^2 = 1$) is:

$$D_{\mathbf{u}} \hat{y} = \nabla \hat{y} \cdot \mathbf{u} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i u_i$$

By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i u_i \leq \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k} u_i^2} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i^2}$$

Equality holds if and only if $\mathbf{u}$ is parallel to $(\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$, i.e., when $u_i = \hat{\beta}_i / \sqrt{\Sigma \hat{\beta}_i^2}$. Hence, the **maximum rate of change of $\hat{y}$** is achieved by moving in the direction of the gradient vector, which is precisely the vector of regression coefficients. **Q.E.D.**

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $\hat{\beta}_A = 5.0$, $\hat{\beta}_B = 3.5$, $\hat{\beta}_C = 1.2$
- $\vert \hat{\beta}_i \vert_{\max} = 5.0$
- $\Delta = 1.0$ (coded)

**Compute coded moves:**

$$\Delta A = \frac{5.0}{5.0} \times 1.0 = 1.0$$

$$\Delta B = \frac{3.5}{5.0} \times 1.0 = 0.70$$

$$\Delta C = \frac{1.2}{5.0} \times 1.0 = 0.24$$

**Convert to natural units** (half-ranges: $A$: 10 ┬░C, $B$: 1 %, $C$: 5 units):

$$\Delta A^{nat} = 1.0 \times 10 = 10\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\Delta B^{nat} = 0.70 \times 1 = 0.70\ \%$$

$$\Delta C^{nat} = 0.24 \times 5 = 1.20\ \text{units}$$

**Construct the path** (starting from center: $A=60$ ┬░C, $B=2$ %, $C=15$ units):

| Step $n$ | $A$ (┬░C) | $B$ (%) | $C$ (units) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 60 | 2.00 | 15.00 |
| 1 | 70 | 2.70 | 16.20 |
| 2 | 80 | 3.40 | 17.40 |
| 3 | 90 | 4.10 | 18.60 |

**Linearity Assumption Check:** The first-order model is a valid local approximation only if the underlying response surface does not exhibit strong curvature in the explored region. This is verified using a **t-test for curvature** comparing the factorial point average to the center point average. If they are statistically indistinguishable (i.e., no significant curvature), then the linear model is adequate and the steepest ascent path is justified. If curvature is detected, the analyst must switch to a **second-order design** (Central Composite or Box-Behnken) to locate the optimum, rather than continuing the steepest ascent walk.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the curvature pre-test.** Many students jump directly into steepest ascent without checking whether the first-order model is locally valid. **Penalty: 2 marks lost.**
> 
> 2. **Confusing coded and natural units.** The step size formula is in **coded** units; the experiment is conducted in **natural** units. Mixing them up produces physically meaningless moves. **Penalty: 2ŌĆō3 marks lost.**
> 
> 3. **Incorrect normalization.** The denominator in $\Delta x_i = (\hat{\beta}_i / \vert \hat{\beta}_i \vert_{\max}) \cdot \Delta$ must be the **maximum absolute coefficient**, not the sum or the squared norm. **Penalty: 1 mark lost.**
> 
> 4. **Omitting the stopping criterion.** A common 2-mark deduction for not stating when to terminate the walk.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring factor constraints.** In practice, factor levels may hit process limits during the walk (as in our worked example). Students should mention this and the need to restart with a new design.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the proof.** When asked "why the coefficients give the gradient," students must derive the result via Cauchy-Schwarz (as in Question B part (a)), not merely state it.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Steepest Ascent is a sequential walk along the gradient vector of a first-order fitted response model to approach the optimum from a region where curvature is absent.
- **Trigger Condition:** Applied after a 2^k factorial design when (i) no significant curvature is detected and (ii) at least one significant main effect exists.
- **Direction Formula:** The path direction in coded units is proportional to the regression coefficients $\hat{\beta}_i$.
- **Step Size Formula:** $\Delta x_i^{coded} = (\hat{\beta}_i / \vert \hat{\beta}_i \vert_{\max}) \cdot \Delta$.
- **Conversion Formula:** $\Delta x_i^{natural} = \Delta x_i^{coded} \cdot (\text{Range}_i / 2)$.
- **Stopping Criteria:** Response begins to decrease; OR a factor hits a process constraint; OR experimental budget is exhausted.
- **Next Step After Stopping:** Run a new 2^k factorial design at the new location; if curvature is now present, proceed to a second-order Response Surface design (Central Composite or Box-Behnken).
- **Mathematical Foundation:** The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality proves that movement along the gradient vector maximizes the directional derivative of the response.
- **Industrial Utility:** Used in process optimization (semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, chemical engineering) to minimize the number of expensive experiments needed to reach the optimum region.
- **Common Mistake:** Steepest Ascent works only on a **locally linear** response surface; it cannot find a true optimum on its own ŌĆö that requires a second-order design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Response Surface Designs for First-Order Models
Analysis of a Second Order Response Surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of a Second Order Response Surface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Second-Order Response Surface** is a mathematical model that approximates the true functional relationship between a set of independent (predictor) variables $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)^T$ and a dependent response variable $y$ by fitting a complete **second-degree polynomial** in the predictors. Formally, the second-order response surface model is written as:

$$
\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum_{i < j} \sum \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \varepsilon
$$

where:
- $\beta_0$ is the **intercept** (constant term)
- $\beta_i$ are the **linear main effect** coefficients
- $\beta_{ii}$ are the **quadratic (pure second-order) effect** coefficients
- $\beta_{ij}$ are the **two-factor interaction** coefficients
- $\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ is the random error term

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A second-order model contains the **minimum polynomial complexity required to capture curvature** (i.e., bends, twists, ridges, and valleys) in the response surface. Linear models can only capture slopes; only second-order (or higher) models can locate **optima** (maxima, minima, or saddle points).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **trekking in a hilly terrain** with a foggy atmosphere. You cannot see the landscape directly, but you carry a **GPS altimeter** and can take elevation readings $(y)$ at various positions $(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)$. 

- A **first-order model** is like fitting a flat tilted plane through your data points ŌĆö useful for finding the general direction of descent (gradient descent), but it will never tell you that you are approaching a **peak or valley**.
- A **second-order response surface** is like fitting a **flexible dome, bowl, or saddle** to your data. It captures the **curvature** of the landscape, allowing you to identify the **summit, the bottom of the valley, or a mountain pass (saddle)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary:** Just as a flexible rubber sheet stretched over pegs of varying heights approximates the terrain, a second-order polynomial approximates the underlying response function within a localized region of the factor space.

### 1.3 Why a Second-Order Model? (The 'Why' Behind the Curvature)

In **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)**, experiments are conducted in **stages**:
1. **Stage 1 ŌĆō First-Order Design (Screening):** Identify the most important factors using $2^k$ factorial or fractional factorial designs.
2. **Stage 2 ŌĆō Steepest Ascent/Descent:** Move towards the optimum region along the gradient of the fitted first-order model.
3. **Stage 3 ŌĆō Second-Order Design:** Once the experimenter is "near" the optimum, curvature becomes important. A **second-order design** (e.g., **Central Composite Design (CCD)** or **Box-Behnken Design**) is employed to fit the second-order polynomial.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Second-Order Designs (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Central Composite Design (CCD)** ŌĆö most common, rotatable, efficient
> - **Box-Behnken Design** ŌĆö three-level, requires fewer runs, no axial points
> - **3$^k$ Factorial Design** ŌĆö full three-level, but run size grows rapidly

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Interpretation |
|:-------|:------:|:------------------------|
| Number of factors | $k$ | Dimension of predictor space |
| Number of design points | $n$ | Total experimental runs |
| Number of model parameters | $p = \frac{(k+1)(k+2)}{2}$ | Includes all coefficients for a full second-order model |
| Eigenvalues of $B$-matrix | $\lambda_i$ | Determine canonical form shape |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Matrix Formulation of the Second-Order Model

For $n$ experimental runs, the model can be compactly written in matrix form:

$$
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}
$$

where $\mathbf{y}$ is an $n \times 1$ vector of responses, $\mathbf{X}$ is the $n \times p$ design matrix (containing columns of $1$'s, $x_i$, $x_i^2$, and $x_i x_j$), $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is a $p \times 1$ vector of regression coefficients, and $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ is the error vector.

### 2.2 Estimation of Coefficients (Least Squares)

The least-squares estimator of $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is obtained by minimizing $SSE = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$:

$$
\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}
$$

provided that $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ is **non-singular** (i.e., the design is **non-singular**).

### 2.3 Partitioned Model and the Role of the $B$-Matrix

For **canonical analysis**, it is convenient to partition the second-order model as:

$$
\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{B} \mathbf{x}
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)^T$ ŌĆö vector of predictor variables
- $\mathbf{b} = (b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_k)^T$ ŌĆö vector of **linear** (first-order) coefficients
- $\mathbf{B}$ is a symmetric $k \times k$ matrix whose diagonal entries are the **quadratic** coefficients and off-diagonal entries are **half** the interaction coefficients:

$$
\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} b_{11} & b_{12}/2 & \cdots & b_{1k}/2 \\ b_{12}/2 & b_{22} & \cdots & b_{2k}/2 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ b_{1k}/2 & b_{2k}/2 & \cdots & b_{kk} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.4 Location of the Stationary Point

Setting the gradient of $\hat{y}$ with respect to $\mathbf{x}$ equal to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{b} + 2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}
$$

Solving for $\mathbf{x}$:

$$
\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}
$$

The predicted response at the stationary point is:

$$
\hat{y}_s = b_0 + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_s^T \mathbf{b}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Existence Condition:** The stationary point exists **if and only if** $\mathbf{B}$ is non-singular. If $\mathbf{B}$ is singular, the analysis proceeds via **ridge analysis** instead of canonical analysis.

### 2.5 Canonical Form and Nature of the Stationary Point

By performing an **orthogonal rotation of the axes** to the principal axes of $\mathbf{B}$ (i.e., diagonalizing $\mathbf{B}$ as $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{M}^T \boldsymbol{\Lambda} \mathbf{M}$ where $\boldsymbol{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_k)$), the model reduces to the **canonical form**:

$$
\hat{y} = \hat{y}_s + \lambda_1 w_1^2 + \lambda_2 w_2^2 + \cdots + \lambda_k w_k^2
$$

where $w_i$ are the **canonical (rotated) variables** and $\lambda_i$ are the **eigenvalues** of $\mathbf{B}$.

**Interpretation Table ŌĆö Nature of the Stationary Point:**

| Eigenvalue Sign Pattern | Nature of Stationary Point | Response Surface Type |
|:------------------------|:---------------------------|:----------------------|
| All $\lambda_i < 0$ | **Maximum** | Dome (concave) |
| All $\lambda_i > 0$ | **Minimum** | Bowl (convex) |
| Mixed signs (some $+$, some $-$) | **Saddle Point (Minimax)** | Twisted surface |
| At least one $\lambda_i = 0$ | **Ridge system** | Stationary ridge |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Students often confuse "saddle" with "max/min". A **saddle point** is neither a true max nor a true min ŌĆö it is a maximum along some canonical directions and a minimum along others. The Greek saddle-shape of a horse's saddle is the perfect visual.

### 2.6 Ridge Analysis

When the stationary point is a **saddle** or lies **outside the region of operability**, the experimenter resorts to **ridge analysis** to find the best response on a constrained region. The **ridge system** of maxima (or minima) is the locus of points satisfying:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{x}} \hat{y}(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x} = R^2
$$

The solution satisfies the equation:

$$
(\mathbf{B} - \mu \mathbf{I})\mathbf{x} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{b}
$$

where $\mu$ is a Lagrange multiplier. For a **maximum ridge**, $\mu < \lambda_{\min}(\mathbf{B})$; for a **minimum ridge**, $\mu > \lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{B})$.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|
| 1 | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \sum \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum \beta_{ij} x_i x_j$ | Full second-order model |
| 2 | $\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ | Least-squares estimator |
| 3 | $\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ | Stationary point location |
| 4 | $\hat{y}_s = b_0 + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_s^T \mathbf{b}$ | Predicted response at stationary point |
| 5 | $\hat{y} = \hat{y}_s + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i w_i^2$ | Canonical form |
| 6 | $\mathbf{B} = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{H}(\hat{y})$ | Hessian matrix (twice $\mathbf{B}$) |
| 7 | $SSE = \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$ | Sum of squared errors |
| 8 | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SSE}{SST}$ | Coefficient of determination |
| 9 | $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_R}{MS_E} = \dfrac{(SS_R / p - 1)}{SSE / (n - p)}$ | Overall model F-statistic |
| 10 | $n \geq \frac{(k+1)(k+2)}{2}$ | Minimum runs needed for a second-order design |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Second-order response surface analysis is extensively used in:
- **Chemical Engineering:** Optimizing yield of chemical reactions, fermentation processes, polymer synthesis.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Optimizing machining parameters (cutting speed, feed, depth of cut) for surface roughness, tool life.
- **Industrial Engineering:** Optimizing inventory, queueing, and supply chain parameters.
- **Pharmaceutical/Biomedical:** Formulation development, drug dissolution optimization.
- **Civil Engineering:** Concrete mix design, soil stabilization.
